# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define dendrimers. State any two characteristic features that distinguish them from conventional linear polymers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Dendrimers are **highly branched, monodisperse, three-dimensional macromolecules** synthesized in a generation-by-generation (layer-by-layer) manner around a central multifunctional core, exhibiting a tree-like architecture with well-defined molecular weight, size, and surface chemistry.

**Two distinguishing features (2 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Monodispersity:** Dendrimers have a **polydispersity index (Ð) ≈ 1.000**, whereas linear polymers are polydisperse (Ð > 1.0). This means every dendrimer molecule in a sample has *exactly* the same molecular weight.
2. **Architectural Precision:** Dendrimers possess a **perfectly branched, symmetric architecture** with mathematically predictable number of surface end groups ($N_s = N_c \cdot N_b^{\,G}$), unlike the random coil morphology of linear polymers.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by a "generation" in a dendrimer? Why is there an upper limit to the number of generations that can be synthesized?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition of a generation (1.5 marks):**
A **generation (G)** refers to one complete layer of branching units added around the dendrimer core during the iterative synthesis. Each new generation multiplies the number of surface end groups by the branching multiplicity $N_b$ and increases the molecular diameter by ~0.7–1.0 nm.

**Upper-generation limit — De Gennes dense-packing effect (1.5 marks):**
Beyond a critical generation (typically **G = 7 for PAMAM**), the dendrimer periphery becomes so sterically crowded that incoming branching monomers cannot access the reactive end groups. The dendrimer surface reaches a **dense-packing limit**, and further growth produces defective, imperfect structures rather than true dendrimers. This phenomenon is known as the **De Gennes dense-packing limit**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Never confuse divergent and convergent synthesis** — this is the single most-deducted mark in KTU valuation. Divergent grows *outward from the core*; convergent grows *inward from the surface*.
> 2. **Always state the value of $N_c$ and $N_b$** in numerical problems before applying $N_s = N_c \cdot N_b^{\,G}$. If you write "$N_s = 2^G$" for a PAMAM problem without verifying the core, expect at least a 1-mark cut.
> 3. **Surface group counting:** For "**half generations**" (e.g., G0.5, G1.5), PAMAM has ester (–COOCH₃) end groups; for "**full generations**" (G1, G2, ...), the surface is –NH₂. Examiners mark this distinction.
> 4. **Biological applications** carry 3–4 marks in 14-mark questions. A purely structural answer without at least 2–3 named applications will lose marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **divergent synthesis** of a PAMAM dendrimer. Identify the core, the two alternating reactions, and the generation-numbering convention. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **molecular weight, number of surface groups, and approximate diameter** of a **G4 PAMAM dendrimer** ($N_c = 4$, $N_b = 2$, $M_{\text{core}} = 60.1$ g/mol, $M_{\text{unit}} = 114.2$ g/mol, diameter increment per generation ≈ 0.7 nm). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Divergent synthesis of PAMAM (7 marks):**

1. **Step 1 — Core selection (1 mark):**
An **ethylenediamine (EDA)** molecule is selected as the central core. It has **4 reactive N–H sites** ($N_c = 4$).

2. **Step 2 — First Michael addition (1 mark):**
EDA reacts with a large excess of **methyl acrylate** in a **Michael addition**. Each N–H bond opens a double bond of methyl acrylate, attaching a –CH₂–CH₂–COOCH₃ chain. After this step, the product is a **half-generation G0.5** dendrimer with **4 terminal ester (–COOCH₃) groups**.

3. **Step 3 — First amidation (1 mark):**
The G0.5 product reacts with a large excess of **ethylenediamine**. Each ester is converted into a primary amide with a fresh –NH₂ terminus. After this step, the product is **G1** (full generation) with **8 surface –NH₂ groups** ($N_s = 4 \cdot 2^1 = 8$).

4. **Step 4 — Iterative cycles (1 mark):**
Repeating steps 2 and 3 in alternation produces **G1.5 → G2 → G2.5 → G3 → ... → G_n**. At each full generation, surface groups double.

5. **Schematic representation (2 marks):**
A textbook-style concentric ring diagram should be drawn showing:
   - Center: EDA core
   - Ring 1: G0.5 (4 ester arms)
   - Ring 2: G1 (8 –NH₂ arms)
   - Ring 3: G1.5 (8 ester arms)
   - Ring 4: G2 (16 –NH₂ arms)
   - The student should label both **half- and full-generation** states.

6. **Purification note (1 mark):**
After each generation, **excess reagents are removed by dialysis, ultrafiltration, or size-exclusion chromatography** to maintain monodispersity.

**Part (b) — Numerical calculations (7 marks):**

1. **Number of surface end groups (2 marks):**

$$N_s = N_c \cdot N_b^{\,G} = 4 \cdot 2^{4} = 4 \cdot 16 = 64 \text{ surface –NH}_2 \text{ groups}$$

2. **Theoretical molecular weight (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_4 &= M_{\text{core}} + M_{\text{unit}} \cdot N_c \cdot \frac{N_b^{\,G+1} - 1}{N_b - 1} \\
&= 60.1 + 114.2 \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{2^{5} - 1}{1} \\
&= 60.1 + 114.2 \cdot 4 \cdot 31 \\
&= 60.1 + 114.2 \cdot 124 \\
&= 60.1 + 14{,}160.8 \\
&\approx 14{,}220.9 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Approximate diameter (2 marks):**

$$D \approx D_{\text{core}} + G \cdot \Delta D = 1.5 \text{ nm} + 4 \cdot 0.7 \text{ nm} = 1.5 + 2.8 = 4.3 \text{ nm}$$

**Final values:** $N_s = 64$, $M_4 \approx 14{,}221$ g/mol, $D \approx 4.3$ nm.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare the **divergent** and **convergent** methods of dendrimer synthesis in terms of strategy, defect density, scalability, and steric limitations. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss **any four biological applications** of dendrimers with suitable examples. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Divergent vs. Convergent synthesis comparison (7 marks):**

Present the answer in a **comparison table format** (recommended for full marks in KTU valuation):

| Parameter (1.4 marks each row × 5 rows = 7 marks) | Divergent Method (Tomalia, 1985) | Convergent Method (Fréchet, 1990) |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategy** | Build outward from the core | Build inward from the surface |
| **First step** | Initiate from multifunctional core | Build complete dendron wedges at periphery |
| **Coupling reaction** | Iterative Michael addition + amidation | Etherification / amide coupling / click chemistry |
| **Defect density** | High (defects accumulate with G) | Low (only final dendron-core coupling step) |
| **Scalability** | Excellent (industrial production of PAMAM) | Limited (steric crowding at high G) |
| **Maximum practical G** | Up to G = 10 (with difficulty) | Up to G = 5–6 (steric limit) |
| **Purification** | Difficult at high G (similar-mass by-products) | Easier (dendrons purified before coupling) |

**Conclusion line (0 marks, but examiner-pleasing):** *The choice of method depends on the target application: divergent for high-G bulk synthesis, convergent for low-defect research-grade dendrimers.*

**Part (b) — Four biological applications of dendrimers (7 marks):**

1. **Targeted drug delivery in cancer chemotherapy (2 marks):**
PAMAM dendrimers surface-functionalized with **folic acid** ligands selectively deliver doxorubicin or paclitaxel to folate-receptor-overexpressing tumor cells (e.g., ovarian, breast cancers). The dendrimer payload can be **2.5 g drug per g dendrimer** — vastly higher than liposomes (~0.1 g/g) — reducing systemic toxicity.

2. **Gene transfection vectors (1.5 marks):**
Cationic dendrimers (e.g., PAMAM, PPI) electrostatically complex with negatively charged **plasmid DNA or siRNA** to form "dendriplexes." These protect the nucleic acid from nuclease degradation and promote endosomal escape via the **"proton sponge" mechanism** (uncatalyzed protonation of tertiary amines buffers endosomal pH, causing osmotic rupture and cytosolic release of the cargo).

3. **MRI contrast enhancement (1.5 marks):**
Gadolinium (Gd³⁺) chelates (e.g., Gd-DOTA) covalently attached to a PAMAM surface produce macromolecular contrast agents with **8–10× higher relaxivity** than free Gd-DTPA. They extend intravascular circulation time and improve tumor boundary delineation.

4. **Vaccine delivery and immunotherapy (1 mark):**
Lysine-based peptide dendrimers displaying multiple **antigenic epitopes** (e.g., from influenza hemagglutinin or HPV E7 protein) elicit strong humoral and cellular immune responses. The multivalent display mimics natural pathogen surfaces, enhancing B-cell activation.

5. **Antimicrobial coatings (1 mark):**
Cationic dendrimers disrupt bacterial cell membranes by inserting their positively charged surface into the anionic phospholipid bilayer, causing **leakage of cytoplasmic contents** and cell death. Effective against *E. coli*, *S. aureus*, and methicillin-resistant strains (MRSA).

*(Any four of the above with one example each is sufficient for full marks.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the KTU exam):**

- [ ] **Definition:** Dendrimer = monodisperse, highly branched, 3D, tree-like macromolecule built generation-by-generation around a multifunctional core.
- [ ] **Three zones:** Core → Interior (dendrons, cavity) → Surface (functional groups).
- [ ] **Master formula (MUST memorize):** $N_s = N_c \cdot N_b^{\,G}$.
- [ ] **Molecular weight formula:** $M_G = M_{\text{core}} + M_{\text{unit}} \cdot N_c \cdot \dfrac{N_b^{\,G+1} - 1}{N_b - 1}$.
- [ ] **PAMAM defaults:** EDA core → $N_c = 4$, $N_b = 2$, $M_{\text{core}} = 60.1$ g/mol.
- [ ] **PPI defaults:** DAB core → $N_c = 4$, $N_b = 2$.
- [ ] **De Gennes dense-packing limit:** reached at G ≈ 7 for PAMAM.
- [ ] **Synthesis methods:** Divergent (core-outward, scalable) vs. Convergent (surface-inward, low-defect).
- [ ] **Radius scaling law:** $R \propto N^{1/3}$ (low G) → $R \propto N^{2/5}$ (high G, dense sphere).
- [ ] **Surface chemistry controls biology:** –NH₂ = cationic, gene delivery; –COOH/–OH = neutral, biocompatible; PEG = stealth, long circulation.
- [ ] **Biological applications to name in any answer:**
  1. Cancer drug delivery (folate-targeted doxorubicin)
  2. Gene / siRNA delivery ("proton sponge" mechanism)
  3. MRI contrast agents (Gd-PAMAM)
  4. Vaccines (multivalent antigen display)
  5. Antimicrobial agents (membrane disruption)
  6. Biosensors (fluorescence, electrochemical)
- [ ] **Comparison edge case:** Dendrimers are **not** micelles, liposomes, or linear polymers — they are **single molecules** with covalent architecture, not self-assembled aggregates.
- [ ] **Polydispersity benchmark:** Ð ≈ 1.000 is the dendrimer's defining purity hallmark.
- [ ] **Numerical trap:** Always check whether the question asks for a "**half generation**" (ester surface) or a "**full generation**" (amine surface) — these have different end-group chemistry and different downstream reactivity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrode potential and Nernst equation for single electrode and cell (numerical problems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrode potential and Nernst equation for single electrode and cell (numerical problems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electrode Potential — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrode Potential ($E$)** is the measure of the tendency of an electrode to lose or gain electrons when it is in contact with its electrolyte solution. It is the potential difference developed at the interface between a metal (electrode) and its salt solution (electrolyte) due to the establishment of an equilibrium between the metal atoms and its ions.

It is measured in **Volts (V)** against the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** as the reference (arbitrarily assigned $E^\circ = 0.00 \text{ V}$ at **298 K** and **1 atm** pressure for H₂ gas and **1 M** H⁺ concentration).

There are two types:
- **Oxidation Potential** → tendency to lose electrons (anode process)
- **Reduction Potential** → tendency to gain electrons (cathode process)

By IUPAC convention, **Standard Reduction Potential ($E^\circ_{\text{red}}$)** is always tabulated.

## 1.2 Standard Electrode Potential

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Electrode Potential ($E^\circ$)** is the electrode potential measured under standard conditions: temperature = **298 K**, pressure = **1 atm** (for gases), and concentration of all species in solution = **1 M (1 mol/L)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tank Model"

Imagine two water tanks connected by a pipe:
- One tank is **high** (high pressure potential) → represents a metal with **high reduction potential** (e.g., Cu²⁺/Cu)
- The other tank is **low** (low pressure potential) → represents a metal with **low reduction potential** (e.g., Zn²⁺/Zn)
- Water naturally flows from **high** to **low pressure** — similarly, electrons flow from the electrode with **lower reduction potential** (anode) to the electrode with **higher reduction potential** (cathode).
- The **height difference** between the two tanks = the **cell EMF ($E_{\text{cell}}$)**.

This makes the abstract concept of "potential" as intuitive as water pressure!

## 1.4 Galvanic vs Electrolytic Cell

> [!NOTE]
> - **Galvanic (Voltaic) Cell**: A spontaneous redox reaction generates electrical energy. $E_{\text{cell}} > 0$.
> - **Electrolytic Cell**: External electrical energy drives a non-spontaneous reaction. $E_{\text{cell}} < 0$.

| Cell Type | $\Delta G$ | $E_{\text{cell}}$ | Process |
|---|---|---|---|
| Galvanic | Negative | Positive | Spontaneous |
| Electrolytic | Positive | Negative | Non-spontaneous |

The **Faraday Constant** $F = 96485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$ is a key physical constant in all electrochemistry equations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Electrode Potential

When a metal (M) is dipped in its salt solution (Mⁿ⁺), two competing processes occur at the interface:

1. **Oxidation** (Metal dissolution): $\text{M(s)} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^-$
2. **Reduction** (Metal deposition): $\text{M}^{n+} + n e^- \rightarrow \text{M(s)}$

Initially, one process dominates, but at equilibrium, both proceed at the same rate. This creates a **potential difference** at the metal-solution interface — the **electrode potential**.

## 2.2 The Nernst Equation — Conceptual Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nernst Equation** relates the electrode potential of a half-cell to the **standard electrode potential** and the **activities (concentrations)** of the chemical species involved. It quantifies how the potential deviates from standard conditions when concentrations are not 1 M.

It is fundamentally derived from the **isothermal reversible work** of Gibbs free energy:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all essential formulas** required for numerical problems on this topic. Memorize the units and conventions strictly.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ | Nernst Equation (at 298 K, log base 10) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Nernst Equation (general form) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Oxidised form}]}{[\text{Reduced form}]}$ | Nernst for single electrode | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Cell EMF from half-cell potentials | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$ | Standard cell EMF | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q_{\text{cell}}$ | Nernst for the complete cell | Volts (V) |
| 7 | $\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$ | Gibbs free energy from cell EMF | Joules (J) |
| 8 | $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ | Standard Gibbs free energy | Joules (J) |
| 9 | $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c$ | Relation between $E^\circ$ and equilibrium constant | Volts (V) |
| 10 | $K_c = 10^{\dfrac{nE^\circ_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591}}$ | Equilibrium constant from standard EMF | Dimensionless |

### Constants to Memorize

| Symbol | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $R$ | $8.314 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$ | Universal gas constant |
| $F$ | $96485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$ | Faraday constant |
| $T$ | $298 \text{ K}$ | Standard temperature |
| $\dfrac{RT}{F}$ | $0.02569 \text{ V}$ | Thermal voltage at 298 K |
| $\dfrac{2.303 RT}{F}$ | $0.0591 \text{ V}$ | Logarithmic Nernst factor at 298 K |

## 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Utility

- **Batteries & Fuel Cells**: Design of Li-ion, Ni-Cd, and H₂-O₂ fuel cells depends entirely on Nernst calculations.
- **Corrosion Engineering**: Predicting corrosion tendency of metals in different environments.
- **Biomedical Sensors**: pH meters, blood-gas analyzers use electrode potential measurements.
- **Electroplating & Metal Refining**: Determining optimal voltage for deposition.
- **Biological Systems**: Nerve impulse transmission and membrane potentials are governed by Nernst-type equations (Goldman equation).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation for a Single Electrode

### Starting Point: Thermodynamic Relation

For a general reversible electrochemical reaction:

$$\text{Oxidised form} + n e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Reduced form}$$

The change in Gibbs free energy is given by:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q = \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ is the reaction quotient (excluding electrons, which are pure solids).

### Relating $\Delta G$ to Electrode Potential

From thermodynamics, the maximum reversible electrical work is:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$$

### Substituting into the Gibbs Relation

$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

### Dividing Both Sides by $-nF$

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

### Converting to log₁₀ form (multiply by 2.303)

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303\, RT}{nF} \log Q$$

### Substituting $T = 298 \text{ K}$

Since $\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.0591 \text{ V}$, the equation simplifies to:

$$\boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}}$$

Or equivalently (writing concentrations explicitly for a half-cell $\text{M}^{n+} + n e^- \rightarrow \text{M}$):

$$\boxed{E = E^\circ + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log [\text{M}^{n+}]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key rule for writing the Nernst equation**: For a half-cell reaction written as a **reduction**, the Nernst equation takes the form: $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$. The "minus" sign is fixed by the convention that standard potentials are **reduction potentials**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Nernst Equation for an Electrochemical Cell

### Cell Reaction Setup

Consider the Daniel Cell:

- **Anode (oxidation)**: $\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$ , $E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$
- **Cathode (reduction)**: $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}$ , $E^\circ_{\text{cathode}}$

### Net Cell Reaction

$$\text{Zn(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) + \text{Cu(s)}$$

### Applying Nernst to Each Half-Cell

$$E_{\text{anode}} = E^\circ_{\text{anode}} + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log [\text{Zn}^{2+}]$$

$$E_{\text{cathode}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log [\text{Cu}^{2+}]$$

### Cell EMF Definition

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$$

### Substituting the Two Equations

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} + \frac{0.0591}{2}\log[\text{Cu}^{2+}] - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log[\text{Zn}^{2+}]$$

### Grouping Terms

$$E_{\text{cell}} = (E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}) + \frac{0.0591}{2}\log\frac{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]}{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}$$

### Final Cell Nernst Equation

$$\boxed{E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log\frac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The reaction quotient for the **cell reaction** is $Q_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{[\text{Products}]}{[\text{Reactants}]}$, excluding pure solids and liquids. In the above, $Q = \dfrac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]}$ (since Zn and Cu are solids).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problems (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following worked examples mirror the **KTU numerical problem** style. Each problem is solved step-by-step with full algebraic detail.

---

### Numerical Problem 1 — Single Electrode Nernst Calculation

**Problem**: Calculate the electrode potential of a copper electrode in a 0.01 M CuSO₄ solution at 298 K. Given: $E^\circ_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34 \text{ V}$.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Write the half-cell reduction reaction.
$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}, \quad n = 2$$

**Step 2**: Write the Nernst equation.
$$E = E^\circ + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log[\text{Cu}^{2+}]$$

**Step 3**: Substitute the values.
$$E = 0.34 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.01)$$

**Step 4**: Evaluate $\log(0.01)$.
$$\log(0.01) = \log(10^{-2}) = -2$$

**Step 5**: Calculate.
$$E = 0.34 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-2) = 0.34 - 0.0591$$

**Step 6**: Final answer.
$$\boxed{E = +0.2809 \text{ V} \approx +0.281 \text{ V}}$$

**Interpretation**: As the Cu²⁺ concentration decreased from 1 M to 0.01 M, the reduction potential **decreased** (less driving force for reduction when ion concentration is lower).

---

### Numerical Problem 2 — Cell EMF Calculation

**Problem**: Calculate the EMF of the Daniel cell at 298 K when $[\text{Zn}^{2+}] = 0.1 \text{ M}$ and $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 1.0 \text{ M}$. Given: $E^\circ_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76 \text{ V}$, $E^\circ_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34 \text{ V}$.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Identify the half-cells.
- Anode (oxidation): $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$, $E^\circ = -0.76 \text{ V}$
- Cathode (reduction): $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$, $E^\circ = +0.34 \text{ V}$

**Step 2**: Calculate $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$.
$$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = +1.10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3**: Write the cell Nernst equation.
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]}$$

**Step 4**: Substitute values. $n = 2$.
$$E_{\text{cell}} = 1.10 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log\frac{0.1}{1.0}$$

**Step 5**: Evaluate the log.
$$\log\frac{0.1}{1.0} = \log(0.1) = -1$$

**Step 6**: Calculate.
$$E_{\text{cell}} = 1.10 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-1) = 1.10 + 0.02955$$

**Step 7**: Final answer.
$$\boxed{E_{\text{cell}} = +1.1296 \text{ V} \approx +1.13 \text{ V}}$$

**Interpretation**: EMF is positive → reaction is **spontaneous** (galvanic cell). Lowering the Zn²⁺ concentration (product) increased the cell EMF, as expected by Le Chatelier's principle.

---

### Numerical Problem 3 — Concentration Cell

**Problem**: A concentration cell is constructed with two hydrogen electrodes. One has $[\text{H}^+] = 1.0 \text{ M}$ and the other has $[\text{H}^+] = 0.001 \text{ M}$. Calculate the EMF at 298 K.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Identify the half-cells.
- Anode (lower concentration): $2\text{H}^+(0.001 \text{ M}) + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2$, $E^\circ = 0.00 \text{ V}$
- Cathode (higher concentration): $2\text{H}^+(1.0 \text{ M}) + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2$, $E^\circ = 0.00 \text{ V}$

**Step 2**: Calculate $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$.
$$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 0.00 - 0.00 = 0.00 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3**: Cell reaction.
$$2\text{H}^+(\text{conc.}) \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+(\text{dilute}), \quad n = 2$$

**Step 4**: Apply Nernst.
$$E_{\text{cell}} = 0 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.001}{1.0} = -\frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-3)$$

**Step 5**: Final answer.
$$\boxed{E_{\text{cell}} = +0.0887 \text{ V} \approx +0.089 \text{ V}}$$

**Interpretation**: A positive EMF is obtained from a concentration difference alone — the cell runs spontaneously to equalize the concentrations.

---

### Numerical Problem 4 — Equilibrium Constant from $E^\circ$

**Problem**: For the cell reaction $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$, calculate the equilibrium constant $K_c$ at 298 K. $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 1.10 \text{ V}$, $n = 2$.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Use the relation.
$$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = \frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c$$

**Step 2**: Rearrange.
$$\log K_c = \frac{n \times E^\circ_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591} = \frac{2 \times 1.10}{0.0591} = \frac{2.20}{0.0591}$$

**Step 3**: Calculate.
$$\log K_c = 37.225$$

**Step 4**: Final answer.
$$\boxed{K_c = 10^{37.225} \approx 1.68 \times 10^{37}}$$

**Interpretation**: A very large $K_c$ confirms the reaction proceeds essentially to completion in the forward direction.

---

### Numerical Problem 5 — Gibbs Free Energy from Cell EMF

**Problem**: Calculate $\Delta G^\circ$ for the Daniel cell at 298 K. Given: $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 1.10 \text{ V}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$.

**Solution**:

**Step 1**: Apply the relation.
$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}}$$

**Step 2**: Substitute.
$$\Delta G^\circ = -(2)(96485)(1.10)$$

**Step 3**: Calculate.
$$\Delta G^\circ = -212267 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \approx -212.27 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4**: Final answer.
$$\boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -212.27 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}$$

**Interpretation**: Negative $\Delta G^\circ$ confirms spontaneity of the Daniel cell reaction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of the Daniel Cell (Galvanic Cell)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Zinc Anode Zn] -- Oxidation: Zn to Zn2+ plus 2e- --> B[ZnSO4 Solution 1M]
    B -- Salt Bridge KCl in Agar --> C[CuSO4 Solution 1M]
    C -- Reduction: Cu2+ plus 2e- to Cu --> D[Copper Cathode Cu]
    D -- Electron flow e- --> A
    B -. Ion migration .-> C
    C -. Ion migration .-> B
    E[Voltmeter V] --- A
    E --- D
    A2[External Circuit] --- A
    A2 --- D
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the Daniel Cell, electrons flow externally from **Zn anode** → **Cu cathode** through the wire. Inside the salt bridge, ions migrate to maintain electrical neutrality.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Nernst Equation Application Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1: Identify the half reactions] --> stepB[Step 2: Determine anode and cathode]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Find n number of electrons transferred]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Write the standard EMF E0cell = E0cathode minus E0anode]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Write the reaction quotient Qcell]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Apply the Nernst equation Ecell = E0cell minus 0.0591 over n times log Qcell]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Substitute concentrations and solve]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8: Interpret sign of Ecell for spontaneity]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Relationship Between Key Electrochemical Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Standard EMF E0cell] -- n electrons and F Faraday --> nodeB[Standard Gibbs Energy dG0]
    nodeA -- n and 0.0591 --> nodeC[Equilibrium Constant Kc]
    nodeB -- dG equals minus nFE --> nodeD[Cell EMF Ecell via Nernst]
    nodeA -- n and Q reaction quotient --> nodeD
    nodeD -- Relation to deltaG --> nodeE[Spontaneity Sign]
    nodeC -- Large Kc means products favored --> nodeE
```

## 4.4 Sign Convention Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Is E cell positive?}
    q1 -- Yes --> r1[Spontaneous Galvanic Cell]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is E cell negative?}
    q2 -- Yes --> r2[Non spontaneous Electrolytic Cell]
    q2 -- No --> r3[E cell equals 0 System at Equilibrium]
    r1 --> s1[Delta G is negative]
    r2 --> s2[Delta G is positive]
    r3 --> s3[Delta G is zero Kc equals Q]
```
