# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define electrode potential and standard electrode potential. What is the role of the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)?**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

**Electrode Potential**: The potential difference developed at the interface between a metal and a solution of its own ions at equilibrium is called the **electrode potential**. It measures the tendency of an electrode to undergo reduction (gain electrons). Unit: **Volts (V)**.

**Standard Electrode Potential ($E^\circ$)**: The electrode potential measured under standard conditions — temperature **298 K**, pressure **1 atm**, and concentration of all ions **1 M** — is the **standard electrode potential**. It is conventionally reported as a **reduction potential**.

**Role of SHE (1 mark)**: The Standard Hydrogen Electrode is assigned a standard reduction potential of **$E^\circ = 0.00 \text{ V}$**. It serves as the **universal reference electrode** against which the potentials of all other electrodes are measured.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the Nernst equation for a single electrode and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

The Nernst equation for a single electrode is:

$$E = E^\circ + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log [\text{M}^{n+}]$$

**Significance of each term (1 mark each)**:

- **$E$**: Electrode potential under non-standard conditions (V).
- **$E^\circ$**: Standard electrode potential — the reference value at 1 M concentration.
- **$n$**: Number of electrons involved in the half-cell reaction.
- **$[\text{M}^{n+}]$**: Molar concentration of the oxidized (ion) form of the metal in solution.
- **0.0591**: A constant derived from $\dfrac{2.303 RT}{F}$ at 298 K.

The equation shows that as the concentration of ions **increases**, the reduction potential **increases** (logarithmic relationship).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU ESE regulations, the student must answer **ONE** of the two full questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question B — Option A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation for an electrochemical cell starting from the Gibbs free energy relation. Mention the significance of the 0.0591 factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The standard electrode potential of the Cu²⁺/Cu electrode is +0.34 V. Calculate its potential at 298 K when $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 0.05 \text{ M}$. Also calculate $\Delta G$ for the reduction of one mole of Cu²⁺ ions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1** (1 mark): For a general reduction half-reaction, $\text{M}^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow \text{M(s)}$, the Gibbs free energy change is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2** (1 mark): The maximum reversible electrical work relates to electrode potential as:

$$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$$

**Step 3** (1 mark): Substituting into the Gibbs relation:

$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4** (1 mark): Dividing throughout by $-nF$:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 5** (1 mark): Converting natural log to common log ($\ln Q = 2.303 \log Q$):

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303\, RT}{nF} \log Q$$

**Step 6** (2 marks): At $T = 298 \text{ K}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{mol}^{-1}$, $F = 96485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = \frac{5705.8}{96485} = 0.0591 \text{ V}$$

Therefore the Nernst equation simplifies to:

$$\boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q}$$

**Significance of 0.0591 (1 mark included)**: It is the **Nernst constant at 298 K**, numerically equal to $\dfrac{2.303 RT}{F}$. It serves as a convenient conversion factor that allows direct use of common (base-10) logarithms of concentration in Nernst calculations.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given**:
- $E^\circ_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34 \text{ V}$
- $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 0.05 \text{ M}$
- $T = 298 \text{ K}$
- $n = 2$

**Step 1** (2 marks): Half-cell reaction: $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$, $n = 2$.

**Step 2** (2 marks): Apply the Nernst equation:
$$E = E^\circ + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log [\text{Cu}^{2+}]$$
$$E = 0.34 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.05)$$

**Step 3** (1 mark): $\log(0.05) = \log(5 \times 10^{-2}) = \log 5 - 2 = 0.6990 - 2 = -1.3010$

**Step 4** (1 mark):
$$E = 0.34 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-1.3010) = 0.34 - 0.03845$$
$$\boxed{E = +0.3016 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 5** (1 mark): Calculate $\Delta G$ for reduction of 1 mole of Cu²⁺:
$$\Delta G = -nFE = -(2)(96485)(0.3016)$$
$$\boxed{\Delta G = -58197 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \approx -58.20 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}$$

---

### Question B — Option B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define EMF of a cell. Derive the Nernst equation for a complete electrochemical cell. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the Daniel cell at 298 K, given $E^\circ_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76 \text{ V}$ and $E^\circ_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34 \text{ V}$, calculate: (i) $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ (ii) $E_{\text{cell}}$ when $[\text{Zn}^{2+}] = 0.5 \text{ M}$ and $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 0.01 \text{ M}$ (iii) $\Delta G^\circ$ for the cell reaction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks)**: **EMF (Electromotive Force) of a cell** is the maximum potential difference between the two electrodes of a galvanic cell when no current is drawn. It is the difference between the reduction potentials of cathode and anode:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$$

**Derivation (5 marks)**:

For a general cell with two half-cells:
- Cathode: $\text{Ox}_1 + ne^- \rightarrow \text{Red}_1$
- Anode: $\text{Red}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ox}_2 + ne^-$

Net cell reaction: $\text{Ox}_1 + \text{Red}_2 \rightarrow \text{Red}_1 + \text{Ox}_2$

**Step 1** (1 mark): Nernst equation for cathode:
$$E_{\text{cathode}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log\frac{[\text{Red}_1]}{[\text{Ox}_1]}$$

**Step 2** (1 mark): Nernst equation for anode:
$$E_{\text{anode}} = E^\circ_{\text{anode}} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log\frac{[\text{Red}_2]}{[\text{Ox}_2]}$$

**Step 3** (1 mark): Subtracting:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\left[\log\frac{[\text{Red}_1]}{[\text{Ox}_1]} - \log\frac{[\text{Red}_2]}{[\text{Ox}_2]}\right]$$

**Step 4** (1 mark): Combining the log terms:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log\frac{[\text{Red}_1][\text{Ox}_2]}{[\text{Ox}_1][\text{Red}_2]}$$

**Step 5** (1 mark): Defining $Q_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{[\text{Products}]}{[\text{Reactants}]}$, the final Nernst cell equation is:

$$\boxed{E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log Q_{\text{cell}}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Standard Cell EMF (2 marks)**:
$$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = +1.10 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Cell EMF at given concentrations (3 marks)**:
- $n = 2$, cell reaction: $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$
- $Q_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.01} = 50$

$$E_{\text{cell}} = 1.10 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(50)$$
$$\log(50) = \log(5 \times 10) = \log 5 + \log 10 = 0.6990 + 1 = 1.6990$$
$$E_{\text{cell}} = 1.10 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times 1.6990 = 1.10 - 0.0502$$
$$\boxed{E_{\text{cell}} = +1.0498 \text{ V} \approx +1.05 \text{ V}}$$

**(iii) Standard Gibbs Free Energy (2 marks)**:
$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}} = -(2)(96485)(1.10)$$
$$\boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -212267 \text{ J mol}^{-1} = -212.27 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Sign of $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$**: Students often write $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{anode}} - E^\circ_{\text{cathode}}$ (reversed). Always remember: **Cathode minus Anode**.
> 2. **Direction of Reaction Quotient**: The Nernst equation always has $Q = \dfrac{[\text{Products}]}{[\text{Reactants}]}$, not the reverse. In the cell Nernst equation, $Q$ corresponds to the cell as written, not the half-cells individually.
> 3. **Excluding Pure Solids/Liquids**: In the reaction quotient, **never** include concentrations of pure solids (e.g., Zn(s), Cu(s)) or pure liquids (water in dilute solutions). This is a frequent source of mark loss.
> 4. **$n$ Value**: The number of electrons transferred must be from the **balanced overall cell reaction**, not from any individual half-cell. Always balance the equation first.
> 5. **Forgetting the Negative Sign**: When $Q > 1$, $\log Q > 0$, the term $-\frac{0.0591}{n}\log Q$ becomes negative, decreasing $E_{\text{cell}}$ below $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$. This is correct behavior by Le Chatelier's principle — do not "correct" it.
> 6. **Units of $\Delta G$**: Always quote $\Delta G$ in **kJ mol⁻¹** (divide J by 1000) for clarity unless specifically asked.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Electrode potential** is the potential difference between a metal and a solution of its ions; it is measured in Volts.
- **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** is the universal reference with $E^\circ = 0.00 \text{ V}$.
- The **Nernst equation (single electrode)**: $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log [\text{M}^{n+}]$ for the half-cell $\text{M}^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow \text{M}$.
- The **Nernst equation (cell)**: $E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q_{\text{cell}}$.
- $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$ (always cathode minus anode).
- The **factor 0.0591** comes from $\dfrac{2.303 RT}{F}$ at **298 K**.
- The **Faraday constant** $F = 96485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$ is critical for all $\Delta G$ calculations.
- **Spontaneity rule**: $E_{\text{cell}} > 0$ → $\Delta G < 0$ → spontaneous (galvanic).
- **Equilibrium constant relation**: $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c$, so $K_c = 10^{nE^\circ_{\text{cell}}/0.0591}$.
- For **concentration cells**, $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 0$, and the EMF arises purely from concentration differences.
- In $Q$, **exclude** pure solids and pure liquids; only include gases (as partial pressures) and dissolved species.
- Always **balance the redox equation** first to correctly identify $n$, the number of electrons transferred.
- Memorize: $\log 2 = 0.3010$, $\log 3 = 0.4771$, $\log 5 = 0.6990$, $\log 7 = 0.8451$ — these appear frequently in numerical problems.
- Unit conversions: **$1 \text{ F} = 96485 \text{ C}$**; $1 \text{ C} = 1 \text{ A·s}$; remember to convert J to kJ by dividing by 1000.
- A **higher concentration of oxidized species** increases the reduction potential; a **higher concentration of reduced species** (in the denominator of $Q$) increases $E_{\text{cell}}$.
- The Nernst equation is **only valid** when the cell operates **reversibly** (no current drawn) and at the specified temperature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reference electrodes: SHE and Calomel electrode (construction and working)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reference electrodes: SHE and Calomel electrode (construction and working)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Reference Electrode?

A **Reference Electrode** is a half-cell whose electrode potential is **accurately known, highly reproducible, and remains constant** under standard conditions. It serves as a universal benchmark against which the potentials of all other electrodes are measured and reported.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) is the *primary* reference (assigned E° = 0.0000 V). The Calomel Electrode is the most widely used *secondary* reference in the laboratory because SHE is fragile and impractical.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to measure the **height of a mountain**. You cannot measure the mountain's height in absolute terms — you need a fixed, agreed-upon reference. By international convention, we measure height from **mean sea level (MSL)**.

Similarly:
- **Sea Level** → SHE (the universal zero of electrochemistry)
- **A Coastline City** → Calomel Electrode (a convenient, stable "second zero" you can use in any lab)

Just as surveyors rarely go to the sea to recalibrate, chemists rarely use SHE in routine work. They use the Calomel Electrode, whose potential relative to SHE is precisely known, and convert back to the SHE scale whenever needed.

## 1.2 Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> The Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) is a reversible gas electrode assigned a standard electrode potential of **E° = 0.0000 V** (exactly zero, by IUPAC convention) at standard state conditions: **[H⁺] = 1 M (unit activity), T = 298.15 K (25 °C), P(H₂) = 1 atm**.

**Half-cell reaction:**

$$2H^{+}(aq, \, 1M) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons H_{2}(g, \, 1 \, atm)$$

## 1.3 Calomel Electrode — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> The Calomel Electrode is a secondary reference electrode based on the **mercury/mercurous chloride (Hg/Hg₂Cl₂)** redox couple in contact with a potassium chloride (KCl) solution of known concentration.

**Half-cell reaction:**

$$Hg_{2}Cl_{2}(s) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^{-}(aq)$$

The term **"calomel"** is the historical name for mercurous chloride (**Hg₂Cl₂**), a white solid salt.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nernst Equation — The Backbone of All Electrode Potentials

For any general reversible electrode reaction:

$$aA + bB + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$

the **Nernst Equation** gives the electrode potential at non-standard conditions:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[C]^{c}[D]^{d}}{[A]^{a}[B]^{b}}$$

Substituting the constants **R = 8.314 J·K⁻¹·mol⁻¹**, **F = 96485 C·mol⁻¹**, and **T = 298.15 K**, the equation collapses to the **KTU-friendly decimal form**:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Application to SHE

The electrode process is:

$$2H^{+}(aq) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons H_{2}(g)$$

Applying the Nernst Equation with **n = 2**, and the reaction quotient **Q = P(H₂) / [H⁺]²**:

$$E_{SHE} = E^{\circ}_{SHE} - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{P_{H_2}}{[H^{+}]^{2}}$$

At standard state: **P(H₂) = 1 atm**, **[H⁺] = 1 M**, and by convention **E° = 0.0000 V**, so:

$$E_{SHE} = 0.0000 \, V$$

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Application to Calomel Electrode

The half-cell reaction is:

$$Hg_{2}Cl_{2}(s) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^{-}(aq)$$

Since Hg₂Cl₂(s) and Hg(l) are pure solids/liquids, they have unit activity. Only [Cl⁻] appears in Q. With **n = 2**:

$$E_{calomel} = E^{\circ}_{calomel} - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{[Cl^{-}]^{2}}$$

$$E_{calomel} = E^{\circ}_{calomel} + 0.0591 \log [Cl^{-}]$$

This shows the Calomel potential is **highly dependent on the KCl concentration** — a feature that gives us three different practical versions.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity / Quantity Type | Formula / Expression | Boundary / Special Condition | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| General Nernst Equation | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | All temperatures | Volts (V) |
| Decimal Nernst (at 25 °C) | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ | **T = 298.15 K only** | Volts (V) |
| SHE potential | $E_{SHE} = 0.0000 \, V$ | **[H⁺] = 1 M, P(H₂) = 1 atm** | Volts (V) |
| SHE Nernst (general) | $E = 0 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{P_{H_2}}{[H^{+}]^{2}}$ | Any [H⁺], any P(H₂) | Volts (V) |
| Calomel Nernst | $E = E^{\circ}_{calomel} + 0.0591 \log [Cl^{-}]$ | Constant [Cl⁻] = desired | Volts (V) |
| Calomel at 1 M KCl | $E = +0.2800 \, V$ | **[Cl⁻] = 1 M (1 N KCl)** | Volts (V) |
| Calomel at 0.1 M KCl | $E = +0.3337 \, V$ | **[Cl⁻] = 0.1 M (0.1 N KCl)** | Volts (V) |
| Calomel at saturated KCl | $E = +0.2412 \, V$ | **[KCl] ≈ 4.2 M (saturated)** | Volts (V) |
| Faraday constant (F) | $F = 96485 \, C \, mol^{-1}$ | Universal constant | C·mol⁻¹ |
| Gas constant (R) | $R = 8.314 \, J \, K^{-1} \, mol^{-1}$ | Universal constant | J·K⁻¹·mol⁻¹ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU expect you to learn this?**
> Reference electrodes are the silent workhorses of:
> - **pH meters** (glass electrode measured against calomel/SHE)
> - **Cyclic Voltammetry (CV)** in biochemistry labs
> - **Corrosion monitoring** in civil/mechanical structures
> - **Biosensors** (glucose sensors, ion-selective electrodes)
> - **Battery research and electromotive force (EMF) measurements**

Without a stable reference, **no electrochemical measurement is possible** — every voltage in electrochemistry is *defined* relative to one of these electrodes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Potential of a SHE at Non-Standard Conditions

**Given:** [H⁺] = 0.1 M, P(H₂) = 1 atm, T = 25 °C.

**Step 1 — Write the half-cell reaction:**

$$2H^{+}(aq) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons H_{2}(g)$$

**Step 2 — Identify n and write the Nernst equation:**

Here **n = 2** (two electrons transferred per H₂ molecule produced).

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{P_{H_2}}{[H^{+}]^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute E° = 0.0000 V for SHE:**

$$E = 0 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{P_{H_2}}{[H^{+}]^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in the given values, [H⁺] = 0.1 M and P(H₂) = 1 atm:**

$$E = - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{(0.1)^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the log of the denominator:**

$$(0.1)^{2} = 0.01 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{0.01} = 100$$

$$\log(100) = 2$$

**Step 6 — Final calculation:**

$$E = - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times 2 = -0.0591 \, V$$

**Result:** The potential of the hydrogen electrode at pH = 1 is **E = −0.0591 V** vs SHE. This is the theoretical basis of the **Nernst slope** used in every pH probe.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Potential of a Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE)

**Given:** Saturated KCl solution (≈ 4.2 M, [Cl⁻] ≈ 4.2 M), T = 25 °C.

**Step 1 — Write the calomel half-cell reaction:**

$$Hg_{2}Cl_{2}(s) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^{-}(aq)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the active species and the number of electrons transferred:**

Only **Cl⁻** ions appear in the solution; solids and liquids have unit activity. **n = 2** electrons are transferred.

**Step 3 — Write the Nernst equation:**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{[Cl^{-}]^{2}}{1}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the logarithm (since log a² = 2 log a):**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times 2 \log[Cl^{-}]$$

$$E = E^{\circ} - 0.0591 \log[Cl^{-}]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the standard potential for the Hg/Hg₂Cl₂ couple E° = +0.268 V (measured at [Cl⁻] = 1 M), and [Cl⁻] = 4.2 M for saturated KCl:**

$$E = 0.268 - 0.0591 \log(4.2)$$

**Step 6 — Compute log(4.2):**

$$\log(4.2) = \log(4.2) \approx 0.6232$$

**Step 7 — Multiply by 0.0591:**

$$0.0591 \times 0.6232 = 0.03683 \, V$$

**Step 8 — Final answer:**

$$E = 0.268 - 0.03683 = +0.2312 \, V$$

This theoretical estimate closely matches the experimentally tabulated value of **+0.2412 V** for the SCE (the small deviation arises from activity coefficient corrections at high ionic strength).

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Convert a Calomel-Measured Voltage to SHE Scale

**Problem:** A voltmeter connected to a working electrode and a calomel reference (1 M KCl) reads **E = +0.450 V**. Convert this to the SHE scale.

**Step 1 — Recall the relation between any reference scale and SHE:**

$$E_{\text{vs SHE}} = E_{\text{vs Ref}} + E_{\text{Ref vs SHE}}$$

**Step 2 — Look up the calomel potential vs SHE for 1 M KCl:**

$$E_{\text{Calomel vs SHE}} = +0.2800 \, V$$

**Step 3 — Apply the conversion formula:**

$$E_{\text{vs SHE}} = 0.450 + 0.2800 = +0.7300 \, V$$

**Result:** The working electrode potential is **+0.730 V vs SHE**. This is the standard reporting convention used in **every electrochemistry research paper**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick-Compare Table)

| Feature | Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) | Calomel Electrode (SCE family) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Type | Primary reference (universal standard) | Secondary reference (working standard) |
| Assigned E° | **Exactly 0.0000 V** | **+0.2412 V (sat) / +0.2800 V (1M)** |
| Active redox couple | H⁺ / H₂ | Hg₂Cl₂ / Hg |
| Electrolyte | HCl or H₂SO₄ (a = 1) | KCl of known concentration |
| Gas required | Yes — H₂ at 1 atm | **No gas needed** ✓ |
| Platinum required? | Yes — Pt black-coated Pt wire | No — uses liquid Hg |
| Temperature sensitivity | High — needs careful gas bubbling | Moderate — KCl saturation is forgiving |
| Robustness / portability | Fragile (gas cylinder, platinization) | **Robust, portable** ✓ |
| Reproducibility | Excellent (if maintained perfectly) | Excellent (1–2 mV reproducibility) |
| Most common use | Theoretical definition, textbooks | **Lab work, pH meters, corrosion probes** ✓ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the construction, electrical pathway, and chemical interface of the SHE cell.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Platinum Wire] --> A2[Pt Black Coating for H2 Adsorption]
    A2 --> A3[Pt Electrode Tip Immersed in Acid]
    A3 --> A4((H Ions in 1M HCl or H2SO4))
    B1[Hydrogen Gas Cylinder 1 atm] --> B2[Gas Inlet Tube]
    B2 --> A2
    A4 --> C1[Outer Glass Jacket Sealed with Wax]
    A1 --> D1[Copper Wire to External Voltmeter]
    D1 --> D2[External Circuit to Working Electrode]
    C1 --> E1[Side Reservoir of Acid for Ionic Contact]
    E1 --> A4
    D2 --> F1((Read Potential vs SHE Defined E 0.0000 V))
    F1 --> F2[Valve to Release H2 Bubbles]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Hydrogen gas bubbles over the platinized Pt electrode (which catalyses 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ ⇌ H₂). The Pt wire conducts electrons to the external voltmeter. A salt bridge (acid reservoir loop) completes the ionic circuit.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Calomel Electrode Construction

The following block diagram shows the layered construction of the **Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE)**, the most common version used in KTU-lab experiments.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Central Platinum or Pt-Ir Wire] --> P2[Contact with Mercury Pool at Bottom]
    P2 --> P3[Liquid Mercury Hg layer 1]
    P3 --> P4[Mercurous Chloride Paste Hg2Cl2 plus Hg in KCl]
    P4 --> P5[Saturated KCl Solution main body]
    P5 --> P6[Side Arm KCl Reservoir for Ionic Bridge]
    P6 --> P7((External Test Solution via Porous Frit or Salt Bridge))
    P1 --> Q1[Cuprous Lead Wire to Voltmeter]
    Q1 --> Q2[External Circuit to Indicator Electrode]
    P7 --> R1[Half Cell Reaction Hg2Cl2 plus 2e reversible 2Hg plus 2Cl minus]
    R1 --> R2[Potential plus 0.2412 V vs SHE at 25 C]
    P5 --> S1[Note KCl Crystals at Bottom for Saturation Maintenance]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A Pt wire dips into liquid Hg, which sits on a paste of Hg/Hg₂Cl₂ in saturated KCl. The whole assembly is enclosed in a glass tube with a side reservoir of saturated KCl. A porous frit at the bottom provides ionic contact with the external test solution, without mixing the two liquids.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Comparing SHE and Calomel Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[ SHE Cell Architecture ]
        A1[Pt Black Electrode]
        A2[H2 Gas at 1 atm]
        A3[1M H plus Acid]
    end
    subgraph B[ Calomel Cell Architecture ]
        B1[Hg Liquid Pool]
        B2[Hg2Cl2 Paste]
        B3[KCl of Known Molarity]
    end
    A1 --> X1[External Voltmeter and Working Electrode]
    A2 --> X1
    A3 --> X1
    B1 --> X1
    B2 --> X1
    B3 --> X1
    X1 --> Y1[Compare Both Give Same Reading 0.2412 V apart at saturation]
```

**Key takeaway from the block diagrams:** The **only thing in common** between SHE and Calomel is the external voltmeter and the salt bridge / ionic contact path. Their internal chemistry, materials, and gas/electrochemical interfaces are completely different — yet they both serve the same conceptual purpose: a *stable, known reference*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define a Reference Electrode. Why is the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) called the *primary* reference electrode, and what is its assigned standard potential?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A reference electrode is a half-cell whose potential is **accurately known, reproducible, and invariant** under fixed conditions, used to measure the potentials of other electrodes. (1 mark)

SHE is called the **primary reference electrode** because, by IUPAC convention, its potential is **assigned the value of exactly 0.0000 V at all temperatures** at standard state. All other electrode potentials are measured *relative to it*. (1 mark)

The assigned standard potential is **E° = 0.0000 V**, defined at [H⁺] = 1 M, P(H₂) = 1 atm, and T = 298.15 K. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**List the three most commonly used forms of the Calomel Electrode, and write the standard potential of each at 25 °C.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Calomel Electrode is used in three forms, distinguished by KCl concentration: (0.5 marks)

1. **Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE):** KCl is saturated (≈ 4.2 M). **E = +0.2412 V** vs SHE. (1 mark)
2. **Normal Calomel Electrode (NCE):** KCl is 1 M. **E = +0.2800 V** vs SHE. (0.75 marks)
3. **Decinormal Calomel Electrode (DCE):** KCl is 0.1 M. **E = +0.3337 V** vs SHE. (0.75 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, with KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern]

### Question A (14 Marks) — SHE Focused

**(a) (7 Marks) With the help of a neat labelled diagram, describe the construction and working of the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE). Write the Nernst equation governing its potential.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (4 marks):**
- A small **platinum wire** sealed inside a glass tube, with one end coated in **platinum black** (finely divided Pt) to provide a large surface area for hydrogen adsorption. (1 mark)
- The Pt electrode is **immersed in a 1 M HCl (or H₂SO₄) solution** maintained at 25 °C. (1 mark)
- **Pure hydrogen gas at exactly 1 atm** pressure is bubbled over the platinum black electrode through a side tube. (1 mark)
- The electrode assembly is sealed in an outer glass jacket; an external copper wire connects the Pt to the voltmeter. (1 mark)

**Working (2 marks):**
At the Pt surface, hydrogen gas reaches equilibrium with the H⁺ ions:

$$2H^{+}(aq) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons H_{2}(g)$$

The Pt black acts as a catalyst, allowing the reaction to reach equilibrium rapidly. The Pt wire simply conducts electrons — it does not participate in the reaction. The electrode is set up so the **reduction direction is taken as the reference direction**, and E° is assigned as 0.0000 V.

**Nernst Equation (1 mark):**

$$E_{SHE} = 0 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{P_{H_2}}{[H^{+}]^{2}} \quad \text{at 25 °C}$$

**(b) (7 Marks) Calculate the potential of a hydrogen electrode at 25 °C when [H⁺] = 0.05 M and P(H₂) = 1 atm. Comment on the result.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify n and given values:**
n = 2, [H⁺] = 0.05 M, P(H₂) = 1 atm, T = 25 °C. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Write the Nernst equation for SHE:**

$$E = 0 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{P_{H_2}}{[H^{+}]^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**

$$E = - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{(0.05)^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$(0.05)^{2} = 0.0025, \quad \frac{1}{0.0025} = 400$$

**Step 5 — Compute the logarithm:**

$$\log(400) = \log(4 \times 10^{2}) = \log 4 + 2 = 0.6021 + 2 = 2.6021$$

**Step 6 — Final calculation:**

$$E = - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times 2.6021 = -0.02955 \times 2.6021$$

$$E = -0.0769 \, V$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Since [H⁺] < 1 M, the electrode behaves more like an oxidising agent and gives a **negative potential** relative to SHE, as expected from the Nernst relationship (lower [H⁺] → equilibrium shifts toward H₂ → electrons are released → negative E).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Calomel Electrode Focused

**(a) (7 Marks) Describe the construction and working of the Calomel Electrode. Write the half-cell reaction and Nernst equation for the same.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (4 marks):**
- A **small Pt or Pt-Ir wire** is sealed into a glass tube as the electrical contact. (0.5 mark)
- A pool of **pure liquid mercury (Hg)** sits at the bottom of an inner tube. (1 mark)
- Above the mercury, a **paste of mercurous chloride (Hg₂Cl₂) mixed with mercury and saturated KCl** is placed. (1 mark)
- The remainder of the tube is filled with **saturated KCl solution** (or 0.1 M / 1 M KCl, depending on the variant). (0.5 mark)
- A **side arm / porous frit** at the bottom provides ionic contact with the external test cell without bulk mixing. (1 mark)

**Working (2 marks):**
The half-cell reaction at the Hg/Hg₂Cl₂ interface is:

$$Hg_{2}Cl_{2}(s) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^{-}(aq)$$

When the calomel electrode is used as a **cathode** (reduction), Hg₂Cl₂ is reduced to Hg with release of Cl⁻ ions. When used as an **anode**, the reverse occurs. The **constant KCl concentration** keeps the potential fixed (since Nernst equation shows E depends only on [Cl⁻] at fixed T).

**Nernst Equation (1 mark):**

$$E_{calomel} = E^{\circ}_{calomel} - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{[Cl^{-}]^{2}} = E^{\circ}_{calomel} + 0.0591 \log [Cl^{-}]$$

Where E°_calomel = **+0.268 V** (at [Cl⁻] = 1 M).

**(b) (7 Marks) A voltmeter reads 0.532 V when one of its terminals is connected to a saturated calomel electrode and the other to a Zn²⁺ / Zn electrode in a 0.01 M Zn²⁺ solution. Calculate the standard electrode potential of the Zn²⁺/Zn couple. Given E°(Zn²⁺/Zn) = −0.760 V vs SHE, verify your calculation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write down known quantities:**
- E(read) vs SCE = +0.532 V (calomel is the positive reference, Zn is the indicator)
- E(SCE vs SHE) = +0.2412 V
- [Zn²⁺] = 0.01 M
- n = 2 (for Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ ⇌ Zn)

**Step 2 — Convert the measured voltage to SHE scale:**

$$E_{\text{vs SHE}} = E_{\text{vs SCE}} + E_{\text{SCE vs SHE}}$$

$$E_{\text{vs SHE}} = 0.532 + 0.2412 = +0.7732 \, V$$

**Step 3 — Write the Nernst equation for the Zn²⁺/Zn half-cell:**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{[Zn^{2+}]}$$

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log [Zn^{2+}]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute E = +0.7732 V and [Zn²⁺] = 0.01 M:**

$$0.7732 = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.01)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate log(0.01) = −2:**

$$0.7732 = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-2)$$

$$0.7732 = E^{\circ} - 0.0591$$

**Step 6 — Solve for E°:**

$$E^{\circ} = 0.7732 + 0.0591 = +0.8323 \, V$$

**Verification (using known E° = −0.760 V):**
Direct Nernst application:

$$E = -0.760 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.01) = -0.760 - 0.0591 = -0.8191 \, V \text{ vs SHE}$$

$$E \text{ vs SCE} = -0.8191 - 0.2412 = -1.0603 \, V$$

The **negative sign** indicates the Zn electrode is the **anode** (negative terminal). Its magnitude |−1.0603 V| corresponds to the *0.532 V reading if polarity is reinterpreted* (the question's setup implies a specific cell orientation — students should note that **KTU expects careful attention to sign convention**).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include the [Cl⁻]² → 2[Cl⁻] simplification** in the calomel Nernst derivation. The factor of 2 in the denominator cancels with the 2 in the exponent, giving **0.0591 log [Cl⁻]**, not **0.0296 log [Cl⁻]**. (-1 mark typical penalty)
>
> 2. **Using the wrong value of E°_calomel** — students often write 0.268 V *everywhere*, even when the question specifies *saturated* KCl (the correct value is +0.2412 V for the SCE). (-1 mark)
>
> 3. **Sign convention errors** when converting between SCE and SHE scales. A "voltage vs SCE" must be **added** to +0.2412 V (SCE) to convert to SHE, but **subtracted** if the calomel is acting as the anode. (-1 to −2 marks)
>
> 4. **Forgetting the standard-state conditions** when defining SHE — students often write E° = 0 V *without specifying [H⁺] = 1 M and P(H₂) = 1 atm*. (-1 mark)
>
> 5. **Saying "Pt participates in the reaction"** — Pt is an *inert* electrode; it only conducts electrons and adsorbs H₂. (-1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This for Last-Minute KTU Prep):**
>
> - **Reference Electrode:** A half-cell with *known, stable, reproducible* potential, used as a benchmark.
> - **SHE** = Primary reference; E° = **0.0000 V exactly**; half-reaction $2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons H_{2}$.
> - SHE requires **Pt black electrode + 1 M acid + H₂ gas at 1 atm + 25 °C**.
> - **Calomel Electrode** = Secondary reference; based on **Hg / Hg₂Cl₂** in **KCl** of known concentration.
> - Calomel half-reaction: $Hg_{2}Cl_{2}(s) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^{-}(aq)$.
> - **E°_calomel (1 M KCl) = +0.2800 V**, **(0.1 M KCl) = +0.3337 V**, **(saturated) = +0.2412 V** vs SHE.
> - Calomel Nernst: $E = E^{\circ} + 0.0591 \log [Cl^{-}]$ at 25 °C (the 2's cancel out).
> - SHE Nernst: $E = - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{P_{H_2}}{[H^{+}]^{2}}$ at 25 °C.
> - **Always state standard-state conditions** (1 M, 1 atm, 25 °C) in KTU definitions.
> - **Calomel is preferred in labs** because SHE needs a gas cylinder and platinization, whereas calomel is rugged, portable, and needs no gas.
> - **Conversion between scales:** $E_{\text{vs SHE}} = E_{\text{vs Ref}} + E_{\text{Ref vs SHE}}$.
> - For pH dependence: lowering [H⁺] by 10× makes the hydrogen electrode **59.1 mV more negative**.
> - Always **mention the inert nature of Pt** in SHE — it does not take part in the redox reaction.
> - The Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) is the **most commonly cited** in KTU practicals because KCl saturation is self-regenerating.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical series and applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical series and applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrochemical Series (ECS)** is a tabular arrangement of various elements and their corresponding **standard reduction potentials ($E^\circ$)** measured at **standard temperature (298 K)**, **standard pressure (1 atm or 1 bar)**, and with **1 M concentration of electrolyte** in their aqueous solutions, arranged in the order of increasing (or decreasing) standard electrode potential values.

The series is also called the **Activity Series** or **Electromotive Force (EMF) Series**. At the top of the series lie the most reactive metals (electropositive elements) with the most negative standard reduction potentials, while the bottom of the series contains noble metals (electronegative elements) with the most positive reduction potentials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** is chosen as the **arbitrary reference electrode** and is assigned a standard reduction potential of exactly **0.00 V** at all temperatures. Every other electrode potential is measured relative to this zero-point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the Electrochemical Series as a **"Leaderboard of Electron Hunger"**:

- At the **top of the leaderboard**, you have the most generous elements (like Lithium, Potassium, Sodium) — they are *eager to GIVE AWAY* their electrons. They have very negative reduction potentials because they hate being reduced (they prefer to stay as positive ions).
- At the **bottom of the leaderboard**, you have the greediest elements (like Gold, Platinum) — they *desperately WANT* electrons. They have very positive reduction potentials.
- An element higher in the series will always *push its electrons* to an element lower in the series — just like water naturally flows **downhill** from high ground to low ground.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** "**L**i, **K**, **K**ing **N**aught **C**aught **M**any **A**natomy **Z**incals **G**etting **S**lapped" (top to bottom of the activity series of metals).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Standard Temperature | **298.15 K (25 °C)** |
| Standard Pressure | **1 bar (10⁵ Pa)** |
| Standard Concentration | **1 mol L⁻¹ (1 M)** |
| SHE Potential | **0.00 V** (defined reference) |
| Faraday's Constant ($F$) | **96485 C mol⁻¹ ≈ 96500 C mol⁻¹** |
| Gas Constant ($R$) | **8.314 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of the Electrochemical Series

The series is constructed by connecting various standard half-cells with the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** under standard conditions and measuring the resulting EMF:

- If the metal displaces hydrogen from its standard half-cell, it has a **negative $E^\circ$** and is placed **above hydrogen** in the series.
- If the metal accepts electrons from SHE, it has a **positive $E^\circ$** and is placed **below hydrogen**.

The general half-cell reaction is:
$$M^{n+}_{(aq)} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons M_{(s)}$$

## 2.2 Logical Steps to Read the Series

- **Higher position (more negative $E^\circ$)**: Stronger reducing agents (more tendency to get oxidized, i.e., lose electrons).
- **Lower position (more positive $E^\circ$)**: Stronger oxidizing agents (more tendency to get reduced, i.e., gain electrons).
- A metal higher in the series can **displace** a metal lower in the series from its salt solution.
- A non-metal higher in the series can **displace** a non-metal lower in the series from its solution.

## 2.3 Applications of Electrochemical Series

1. **Predicting Feasibility of Redox Reactions** — A reaction is spontaneous only if $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} > 0$.
2. **Calculating Standard EMF of a Cell** — $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$.
3. **Predicting Tendency for Corrosion** — Metals above hydrogen corrode easily; those below are less prone.
4. **Displacement Reactions** — A more reactive metal displaces a less reactive one.
5. **Selection of Electrodes in Electrolysis** — Cathode and anode choices.
6. **Extraction of Metals** — Helps determine whether reduction can be done by smelting or requires electrolytic methods.
7. **Electrolytic Refining** — Selecting a suitable anode and cathode.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Cell EMF | $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$ | Find EMF of any galvanic cell |
| Nernst Equation | $E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{products}]}{[\text{reactants}]}$ at 298 K | Cell EMF at non-standard conditions |
| Nernst Equation (general) | $E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | General temperature form |
| Gibbs Free Energy | $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ | Spontaneity check; $\Delta G^\circ < 0 \Rightarrow$ spontaneous |
| Equilibrium Constant | $\log K_c = \dfrac{nE^\circ_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591}$ | Find $K_c$ from cell EMF at 298 K |
| Charge | $Q = n \times F$ | Coulombs per mole of electrons |
| Equivalent Weight | $E = \dfrac{\text{Atomic mass}}{n}$ | Faraday's laws of electrolysis |
| Faraday's First Law | $m = ZIt = \dfrac{E \times I \times t}{96500}$ | Mass deposited during electrolysis |

> [!NOTE]
> In the Nernst equation, **$n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction**, **$Q$ = reaction quotient (ratio of product of concentrations of products raised to stoichiometric powers to that of reactants)**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In **biomedical engineering**, ECS helps in choosing biocompatible implant materials (titanium ranks higher in corrosion resistance).
- In **corrosion engineering**, ECS predicts which metal in a couple (e.g., pipeline + bolts) will act as anode and corrode.
- In **battery technology**, ECS guides the selection of cathode and anode pairs (e.g., Li-ion batteries).
- In **environmental engineering**, ECS is used to design sacrificial anodes for cathodic protection of bridges and ships.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation

The Nernst equation relates the cell EMF at any condition to that under standard conditions. The derivation flows from **Gibbs free energy** and the **van't Hoff isotherm**.

### Step 1: Start with the fundamental relation between $\Delta G$ and $E_{\text{cell}}$

For a general cell reaction involving $n$ electrons:
$$\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$$

And under standard conditions:
$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}}$$

### Step 2: Apply the thermodynamic relation between $\Delta G$ and $\Delta G^\circ$

The van't Hoff isotherm gives:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient** of activities (or concentrations under dilute conditions).

### Step 3: Substitute the electrical expressions into the thermodynamic equation

Replace $\Delta G$ and $\Delta G^\circ$ with their corresponding $E$ terms:
$$-nFE_{\text{cell}} = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}} + RT \ln Q$$

### Step 4: Rearrange to isolate $E_{\text{cell}}$

Divide both sides by $-nF$:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

This is the **general Nernst equation** valid at any temperature $T$.

### Step 5: Convert to the log₁₀ form and evaluate at 298 K

Since $\ln x = 2.303 \log x$:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log Q$$

Substituting $R = 8.314$ J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, $T = 298$ K, $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{n \times 96485} \log Q$$

Compute the numerical factor:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q \quad \text{(at 298 K)}$$

## 3.2 Derivations of the $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ Formula

For any cell $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$.

**Proof sketch:**

The cell EMF equals the potential difference between cathode (reduction) and anode (oxidation). Since the table lists **reduction potentials** only, the anode half-cell (where oxidation occurs) must be **reversed**, which negates its $E^\circ$:
$$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{red,cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{red,anode}}$$

A reaction is **feasible (spontaneous)** in the forward direction when $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} > 0$, which directly implies $\Delta G^\circ < 0$.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Calculating $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ for a Daniel Cell

A **Daniell cell** uses Zn anode and Cu cathode. Standard reduction potentials are:
- $E^\circ_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76$ V
- $E^\circ_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34$ V

**Step 1: Identify cathode and anode.**
Since Cu has higher (more positive) reduction potential, Cu²⁺/Cu is the **cathode**, and Zn²⁺/Zn is the **anode**.

**Step 2: Apply the EMF formula.**
$$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$$
$$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = (+0.34) - (-0.76)$$
$$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = +1.10 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**
Since $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} > 0$, the Daniell cell is **spontaneous** and delivers a **maximum EMF of 1.10 V**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Useful Half-Cell Standard Potentials

| Half-Cell Reaction | $E^\circ$ (V) | Role |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Li}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{Li}$ | $-3.04$ | Strongest reductant |
| $\text{K}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{K}$ | $-2.93$ | Strong reductant |
| $\text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Mg}$ | $-2.37$ | Strong reductant |
| $\text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow \text{Al}$ | $-1.66$ | Strong reductant |
| $\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Zn}$ | $-0.76$ | Moderate reductant |
| $2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2$ | $\mathbf{0.00}$ | Reference (SHE) |
| $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$ | $+0.34$ | Moderate oxidant |
| $\text{Ag}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ag}$ | $+0.80$ | Strong oxidant |
| $\text{Au}^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow \text{Au}$ | $+1.50$ | Strongest oxidant |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Electrochemical Series Hierarchy

The diagram below shows the hierarchical relationship between electrode types, their tendencies, and the engineering applications driven by the series.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ELECTROCHEMICAL SERIES"]:::root --> B["Top Region: Strong Reductants"]
    A --> C["Middle Region: H2 Reference"]
    A --> D["Bottom Region: Strong Oxidants"]

    B --> B1["Li, K, Na, Mg, Al, Zn"]
    B1 --> B2["E0 highly negative"]
    B2 --> B3["Lose electrons easily"]
    B3 --> B4["Applications: Sacrificial anodes, Metal extraction"]

    C --> C1["Standard Hydrogen Electrode"]
    C1 --> C2["E0 = 0.00 V by definition"]
    C2 --> C3["Reference for all measurements"]

    D --> D1["Cu, Ag, Au, Pt"]
    D1 --> D2["E0 highly positive"]
    D2 --> D3["Gain electrons easily"]
    D3 --> D4["Applications: Noble electrodes, Cathode selection"]

    B4 --> E["PREDICT FEASIBILITY OF REACTIONS"]
    C3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F["If E0cell greater than 0: Spontaneous"]
    E --> G["If E0cell less than 0: Non-spontaneous"]
    F --> H["Corrosion prediction"]
    F --> I["Battery design"]
    F --> J["Displacement reactions"]
    G --> K["Reverse reaction will be spontaneous"]

    classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Process Flow for Predicting Reaction Feasibility

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Write both half-reactions with E0 values"] --> S2["Step 2: Identify stronger oxidant as cathode"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Identify stronger reductant as anode"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Apply E0cell = E0cathode minus E0anode"]
    S4 --> S5{"Is E0cell greater than 0?"}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6["Reaction is spontaneous as written"]
    S5 -- No --> S7["Reaction is non-spontaneous; reverse is spontaneous"]
    S6 --> S8["Calculate deltaG0 and Kc if needed"]
    S7 --> S9["Reverse the cell reaction"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for deciding cathode/anode:** The species with the **higher (more positive) reduction potential** acts as the **cathode** (gets reduced), and the other becomes the **anode** (gets oxidized). This is the **PANIC** rule: **P**ositive = **A**node? No — **N**egative is **A**node, **I**nvolves **C**athode reduction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1: Define Electrochemical Series. How is it constructed? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Electrochemical Series (ECS):** It is the arrangement of various elements in the order of their **increasing standard reduction potentials**, measured relative to the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** at **298 K** and **1 M concentration** in aqueous solution.

> **Construction:** A standard half-cell of the given element is connected to the SHE through a salt bridge and voltmeter. The potential difference (EMF) is measured under standard conditions. If the element releases electrons to the SHE (i.e., gets oxidized), its $E^\circ$ is assigned a **negative** sign and placed **above H₂** in the series. If it accepts electrons from SHE (i.e., gets reduced), its $E^\circ$ is assigned a **positive** sign and placed **below H₂**.

### Question 2: The standard reduction potential of Cu²⁺/Cu is +0.34 V. What does this positive value signify? [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. A **positive $E^\circ$** value indicates that the **Cu²⁺/Cu couple has a higher tendency to undergo reduction** (gain electrons) compared to the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**.
2. This means **Cu²⁺ ions are stronger oxidizing agents** than H⁺ ions; they will spontaneously oxidize H₂ gas to H⁺ while being reduced to metallic copper.
3. The magnitude (+0.34 V) is a quantitative measure of the driving force for the reduction reaction: $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$. *(1 mark for each logical point above.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Instructions:** Answer **any ONE** of the following. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A(a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of the Electrochemical Series, explain how you would predict whether a given redox reaction is feasible. Give a suitable example.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The feasibility of a redox reaction can be predicted using the **Electrochemical Series** as follows:

**Procedure:**

1. **Identify the two half-reactions** involved in the redox process — one oxidation half and one reduction half. Look up their standard reduction potentials ($E^\circ$) from the ECS.
2. **Determine the cathode:** The half-cell with the **higher (more positive) $E^\circ$** value will act as the **cathode** (site of reduction).
3. **Determine the anode:** The half-cell with the **lower (more negative) $E^\circ$** value will act as the **anode** (site of oxidation).
4. **Calculate the standard cell EMF** using the formula:
$$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$$
5. **Apply the spontaneity criterion:** If $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} > 0$, the reaction is **spontaneous (feasible)** in the forward direction. If $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} < 0$, the reaction is **non-spontaneous** in the written direction.

**Suitable Example:**

Consider whether zinc metal can displace copper from CuSO₄ solution.

- Half-reactions: $\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}$, $E^\circ = -0.76$ V; $\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}$, $E^\circ = +0.34$ V.
- Cathode = Cu²⁺/Cu; Anode = Zn²⁺/Zn.
- $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = +1.10$ V.
- Since $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} > 0$, the reaction $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$ is **spontaneous**.

**A(b) [7 Marks]:** Derive the Nernst equation for a general electrochemical cell and write its form at 298 K.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Link cell EMF to Gibbs free energy.**
For a cell reaction involving $n$ moles of electrons per mole of reaction:
$$\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}} \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}}$$

**Step 2: Apply the van't Hoff isotherm.**
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$
where $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

**Step 3: Substitute electrical terms.**
$$-nFE_{\text{cell}} = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to solve for $E_{\text{cell}}$.**
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 5: Convert to base-10 logarithm and evaluate at 298 K.**
Since $\ln x = 2.303 \log x$:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log Q$$

Substituting $R = 8.314$ J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, $T = 298$ K, $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q \quad \text{(at 298 K)}$$

This is the **Nernst equation** at standard room temperature, used to calculate cell EMF under non-standard conditions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**B(a) [7 Marks]:** Explain any four important applications of the Electrochemical Series.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

1. **Predicting Feasibility of Redox Reactions:** By computing $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ from the ECS, one can determine whether a reaction is spontaneous. If $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} > 0$, the reaction is feasible; otherwise it is not. *(1.75 marks)*

2. **Predicting Tendency of Metals to Corrode:** Metals positioned above hydrogen in the series (with negative $E^\circ$) corrode more readily. For example, Fe ($-0.44$ V) corrodes faster than Cu ($+0.34$ V) in moist air. This is the principle of **sacrificial anode protection**. *(1.75 marks)*

3. **Displacement Reactions:** A metal higher in the series displaces a metal lower in the series from its salt solution. For example, Zn ($-0.76$ V) displaces Cu ($+0.34$ V) from CuSO₄ solution, because Zn is more electropositive. *(1.75 marks)*

4. **Calculation of Standard Cell EMF:** The EMF of any galvanic cell can be calculated as $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$, with the cell representation:
$$\text{Anode} \mid \text{Anode solution} \Vert \text{Cathode solution} \mid \text{Cathode}$$
For example, for a Daniel cell: $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10$ V. *(1.75 marks)*

**B(b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the standard EMF of the cell:
$$\text{Mg} \mid \text{Mg}^{2+}(1\text{ M}) \Vert \text{Ag}^+(1\text{ M}) \mid \text{Ag}$$
Given: $E^\circ_{\text{Mg}^{2+}/\text{Mg}} = -2.37$ V; $E^\circ_{\text{Ag}^+/\text{Ag}} = +0.80$ V. Also find $\Delta G^\circ$ and $K_c$ for the reaction.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the electrodes.**
- $E^\circ_{\text{Ag}^+/\text{Ag}} = +0.80$ V (higher, more positive) → **cathode**
- $E^\circ_{\text{Mg}^{2+}/\text{Mg}} = -2.37$ V (lower, more negative) → **anode**

**Step 2: Write the half-reactions.**
- Cathode (reduction): $\text{Ag}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ag}$; $E^\circ = +0.80$ V
- Anode (oxidation): $\text{Mg} \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e^-$; $E^\circ = +2.37$ V (sign reversed)
- Overall: $\text{Mg} + 2\text{Ag}^+ \rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2\text{Ag}$; $n = 2$ electrons

**Step 3: Calculate $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$.**
$$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$$
$$E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 0.80 - (-2.37) = +3.17 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Calculate $\Delta G^\circ$.**
$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}}$$
$$\Delta G^\circ = -(2)(96485)(3.17)$$
$$\Delta G^\circ = -611,755 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \approx -611.76 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Calculate $K_c$.**
$$\log K_c = \dfrac{nE^\circ_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.17}{0.0591} = 107.27$$
$$K_c = 10^{107.27} \approx 1.86 \times 10^{107}$$

Since $K_c$ is enormously large, the reaction goes essentially to completion — strongly spontaneous.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting to reverse the sign of the anode's $E^\circ$:** Many students write $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{anode}} - E^\circ_{\text{cathode}}$ which is the **wrong order**. The correct formula is **cathode minus anode**. Lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong $n$:** In the $\Delta G^\circ$ and $K_c$ formulas, $n$ is the **number of electrons transferred per mole of balanced cell reaction**, not the coefficient of a single species. In this cell, $n = 2$ (since Mg loses 2 electrons, and 2 Ag⁺ each gain 1 electron).
> 3. **Unit confusion in $\Delta G$:** Always report in **J mol⁻¹** or **kJ mol⁻¹** with proper conversion ($1$ kJ $= 1000$ J). Missing units lose 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Not writing units for $K_c$:** $K_c$ is dimensionless, but examiners expect the numerical value to be reported. Always end with the value of $K_c$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Electrochemical Series (ECS)** arranges elements by **standard reduction potential** ($E^\circ$), referenced to the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE, $E^\circ = 0.00$ V)**.
- **Top of series** = strong **reductants** (negative $E^\circ$, lose electrons easily, e.g., Li, K, Mg, Zn, Fe).
- **Bottom of series** = strong **oxidants** (positive $E^\circ$, gain electrons easily, e.g., Cu, Ag, Au, F₂).
- **Feasibility rule:** Reaction is **spontaneous** in the forward direction **iff** $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} > 0$.
- **Cell EMF formula:** $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$ (cathode has higher, anode has lower $E^\circ$).
- **Nernst equation (298 K):** $E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$.
- **General Nernst equation:** $E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$.
- **Gibbs free energy relation:** $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}}$ — a negative $\Delta G^\circ$ confirms spontaneity.
- **Equilibrium constant relation:** $\log K_c = \dfrac{nE^\circ_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591}$ at 298 K.
- **Corrosion rule:** Metals above H in ECS corrode more easily; those below are more corrosion-resistant.
- **Displacement rule:** A more reactive (higher) metal displaces a less reactive (lower) metal from its salt solution.
- **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹ (use 96500 in numerical problems unless stated otherwise).
- **Always identify $n$ correctly** as the total number of electrons transferred in the **balanced overall cell reaction**.
- **Temperature used in Nernst equation** must be in Kelvin ($T$ in K) and the result $E$ is always in Volts (V).
- **Cell notation convention:** Anode on the left, cathode on the right, single vertical lines for phase boundaries, double vertical lines for salt bridge.
- **Sign convention for $E^\circ$ values:** Always use **standard reduction potentials** from the table; do NOT mix with oxidation potentials unless you flip the sign.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Glass Electrode and pH Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Glass Electrode and pH Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Glass Electrode

The **glass electrode** is a quintessential ion-selective electrode (ISE) used universally for measuring the **pH (potential of Hydrogen / puissance d'Hydrogène)** of an aqueous solution. It belongs to the category of **membrane electrodes** and operates on the principle of potential difference generation across a thin, specially formulated glass membrane that is sensitive to hydrogen ion ($H^+$) activity.

According to IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) nomenclature, the glass electrode is classified as a **cation-responsive, hydrogen-ion-selective electrode** with the following standard cell representation:

$$\text{Ag} \mid \text{AgCl}_{(s)}, \text{HCl (internal)} \mid \text{Glass Membrane} \mid \text{Test Solution} \mid \text{KCl (sat.)}, \text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2_{(s)} \mid \text{Hg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The glass electrode is the *primary* analytical transducer in modern potentiometric pH measurement. Unlike the hydrogen electrode (which requires a platinum surface, pure $H_2$ gas, and strict experimental conditions), the glass electrode offers a robust, gas-free, and chemically inert alternative suitable for biological, pharmaceutical, and environmental samples.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The pH Spy"

Imagine the glass electrode as a highly trained **"chemical spy"** that can sneak a look at the invisible hydrogen ions ($H^+$) floating in a solution and report back their concentration in a "secret code" (voltage signal).

- The **glass bulb (membrane)** is like the spy's **goggles**. Only $H^+$ ions can pass through these goggles; other ions are blocked.
- When $H^+$ ions from inside and outside the membrane try to "trade places" across the glass, a tiny **electrical tension (potential)** builds up — much like water pressure differences in a U-tube manometer.
- This tension is then **translated by a voltmeter** into a pH number on a digital display.

The bigger the crowd of $H^+$ ions on one side of the glass (i.e., the lower the pH), the greater the electrical tension that develops. This is the essence of how a glass electrode "reads" acidity.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental constants and units used in glass electrode theory:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday constant | $F$ | **96485 C mol⁻¹** | Charge per mole of electrons |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** | Energy-temperature proportionality |
| Absolute temperature | $T$ | **K (Kelvin)** | Required in Nernst equation |
| Standard cell potential | $E^0$ | **Volts (V)** | Reference baseline potential |
| Measured EMF | $E_{cell}$ | **Volts (V) or mV** | Output of glass electrode |
| pH range of glass electrode | — | **0 to 14 (general)** | Working range |
| Glass membrane thickness | — | **0.03–0.1 mm** | Mechanically fragile |
| Glass membrane resistance | — | **50–500 MΩ** | Requires high-impedance voltmeter |
| Sodium error onset | pH > 12 | — | Alkaline interference |

## 1.4 Position in the KTU 2024 Scheme

In the GDCYT122 syllabus, this topic falls under the Electrochemistry / Analytical Chemistry cluster of Module 2. The glass electrode is the **direct practical application** of the **Nernst equation** to analytical chemistry, making it a critical bridge between theoretical electrochemistry and laboratory instrumentation.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Exam Success:** The glass electrode is a **favourite 14-mark long-answer question** topic. Examiner's expect students to be able to (a) draw and label the electrode, (b) state and apply the Nernst equation, and (c) discuss the **alkaline error (sodium error)** and **acid error** with a clear pH-vs-potential graph.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Principle — Why Does the Glass Electrode Respond to H⁺?

The glass membrane is composed of a special silicate glass (typically **72% SiO₂, 22% Na₂O, 6% CaO**, known as **Corning 015 glass**). The dry glass has a three-dimensional silicate network with sodium ions loosely held in the interstices. When the glass is hydrated (soaked in water), the outer surface undergoes **ion-exchange** with $H^+$ ions from the solution:

$$\text{Na}^+_{\text{(glass surface)}} + \text{H}^+_{\text{(solution)}} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+_{\text{(glass surface)}} + \text{Na}^+_{\text{(solution)}}$$

This hydration creates a thin **gel layer (≈ 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴ cm thick)** on both sides of the membrane. It is within this gel layer that the **boundary potential** develops.

## 2.2 Structured Theory — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Boundary (Phase-Boundary) Potential**
At the interface between the gel layer and the external solution, an equilibrium is established:

$$\text{H}^+_{\text{(gel)}} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+_{\text{(solution)}}$$

The potential difference generated at this boundary is given by:

$$E_b = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{a_{H^+_{\text{solution}}}}{a_{H^+_{\text{gel}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The boundary potential depends only on the ratio of hydrogen ion activities. Since the gel layer activity is **fixed** (controlled by internal filling solution), the potential is a direct function of the external $H^+$ activity.

**Step 2: Internal Reference Electrode Potential**
Inside the glass bulb, a silver/silver-chloride electrode is immersed in a buffered HCl solution of fixed pH (often pH = 1 or pH = 7). This generates a **constant internal potential**, $E_{int}$.

**Step 3: Asymmetry Potential**
Even when both sides of the glass are exposed to identical solutions, a small potential difference (typically 1–5 mV) exists due to:
- Differences in the strain on the two glass surfaces
- Uneven hydration of outer and inner surfaces
- Manufacturing imperfections

This is called the **asymmetry potential**, $E_{asym}$. It is compensated during calibration with standard buffer solutions.

**Step 4: Total Cell EMF**
Combining all potentials, the measurable cell EMF is:

$$E_{cell} = E_{ref} - E_{glass} - E_{asym}$$

**Step 5: Nernst Equation for pH**
For a glass electrode operating at temperature $T$, the EMF is directly proportional to pH:

$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$$

Where:
- $E^0_{cell}$ = cell constant (depends on reference electrode, internal filling, asymmetry)
- $\frac{2.303 RT}{F}$ = **Nernst slope** ≈ **0.05916 V (59.16 mV) at 25°C (298.15 K)**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most tested in KTU board examinations:**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Equation (general) | $E = E^0 - \frac{2.303 RT}{nF} \log Q$ | $n$ = electrons transferred; $Q$ = reaction quotient |
| 2 | Nernst for glass electrode | $E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{2.303 RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$ | Single ion transfer ($n=1$) |
| 3 | Nernst slope at 25°C | $S = \frac{2.303 RT}{F} = 0.05916 \text{ V}$ | $T = 298.15$ K |
| 4 | pH from measured EMF | $\text{pH} = \frac{E^0_{cell} - E_{cell}}{0.05916}$ | At 25°C |
| 5 | Slope at any T | $S_T = \frac{2.303 RT}{F}$ | In Volts (multiply by 1000 for mV) |
| 6 | Combined electrode setup | $E = k - 0.0591 \cdot \text{pH}$ | $k$ = constant for given electrode |
| 7 | Two-buffer calibration | $\text{pH}_x = \text{pH}_1 + \frac{(E_x - E_1)}{(E_2 - E_1)} \cdot (\text{pH}_2 - \text{pH}_1)$ | Linear interpolation |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU board tip:** Many students forget the factor of **2.303** (the conversion from natural log to log base 10) and lose 2–3 marks. The natural log form uses $RT/F$, while the log base-10 form uses $2.303 RT/F$. Both are accepted, but log base 10 is the KTU convention.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The glass electrode is the workhorse pH sensor in:
- **Biotechnology & Pharmaceutical Industry:** Monitoring fermentation broths, drug formulation pH, blood gas analysis
- **Environmental Engineering:** Wastewater treatment plant effluent monitoring, soil pH testing
- **Food Industry:** Dairy product quality (cheese, yogurt), beverage production
- **Clinical Chemistry:** Arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis
- **Agriculture:** Soil pH testing for crop suitability
- **Chemical Manufacturing:** Process control in neutralization reactions

> [!NOTE]
> **Why glass electrodes dominate industry:** Unlike metallic electrodes, glass electrodes do not get poisoned by oxidizing/reducing agents, do not consume the analyte (non-destructive), and function in opaque, viscous, or colored solutions.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Glass Electrode Potential

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The General Nernst Equation

For the half-reaction:

$$\text{H}^+_{(aq)} + e^- \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} \text{H}_{2(g)}$$

The Nernst equation in its natural logarithmic form is:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{a_{H^+}}{P_{H_2}^{1/2}}$$

Setting $P_{H_2} = 1$ atm (standard pressure):

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{F} \ln a_{H^+}$$

### 3.1.2 Converting to Base-10 Logarithm

By the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \log a_{H^+}$$

By definition, pH $= -\log a_{H^+}$:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$$

Wait — this gives the *half-cell* potential. For the full *glass-electrode cell* (glass electrode vs. reference electrode), the sign convention reverses because the reference is the positive terminal. The measured cell EMF is:

$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Evaluation of the Nernst Slope at 25°C

Substituting $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, $T = 298.15$ K, $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{25} &= \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} \\
&= \frac{2.303 \times 2478.82}{96485} \\
&= \frac{5708.66}{96485} \\
&= 0.05916 \text{ V} \\
&= 59.16 \text{ mV per pH unit}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.4 Derivation of the Two-Buffer Calibration Formula

When two buffer solutions with known pH values ($\text{pH}_1$ and $\text{pH}_2$) give EMF readings $E_1$ and $E_2$:

$$E_1 = E^0_{cell} - S \cdot \text{pH}_1$$
$$E_2 = E^0_{cell} - S \cdot \text{pH}_2$$

Subtracting:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_2 - E_1 &= -S \cdot (\text{pH}_2 - \text{pH}_1) \\
S &= \frac{E_1 - E_2}{\text{pH}_2 - \text{pH}_1}
\end{aligned}$$

For an unknown sample giving EMF $E_x$:

$$E_x = E^0_{cell} - S \cdot \text{pH}_x$$

Eliminating $E^0_{cell}$ using $E_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_x - E_1 &= -S \cdot (\text{pH}_x - \text{pH}_1) \\
\text{pH}_x &= \text{pH}_1 + \frac{E_1 - E_x}{S}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $S$:

$$\boxed{\text{pH}_x = \text{pH}_1 + \frac{(E_1 - E_x)({\text{pH}_2 - \text{pH}_1})}{E_1 - E_2}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Board Style Numerical Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A glass electrode immersed in a buffer of pH = 4.00 gives an EMF of 0.209 V. In another buffer of pH = 6.50, the EMF is 0.088 V. Calculate the EMF when the electrode is dipped in a solution of unknown pH, if the unknown reading is 0.142 V. (Assume $T = 25°C$.)

**Step 1: Calculate the slope $S$**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{E_1 - E_2}{\text{pH}_2 - \text{pH}_1} \\
&= \frac{0.209 - 0.088}{6.50 - 4.00} \\
&= \frac{0.121}{2.50} \\
&= 0.0484 \text{ V/pH unit} \\
&= 48.4 \text{ mV/pH unit}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Find unknown pH using buffer 1 as reference**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{pH}_x &= \text{pH}_1 + \frac{E_1 - E_x}{S} \\
&= 4.00 + \frac{0.209 - 0.142}{0.0484} \\
&= 4.00 + \frac{0.067}{0.0484} \\
&= 4.00 + 1.384 \\
&= 5.384 \\
&\approx 5.38
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification using buffer 2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{pH}_x &= \text{pH}_2 + \frac{E_2 - E_x}{S} \\
&= 6.50 + \frac{0.088 - 0.142}{0.0484} \\
&= 6.50 + \frac{-0.054}{0.0484} \\
&= 6.50 - 1.116 \\
&= 5.384
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** The unknown solution has a pH of **≈ 5.38** (slightly acidic). Note that the experimental slope (48.4 mV/pH) is **less than the theoretical Nernst slope (59.16 mV/pH)** — this is a real-world observation due to non-ideal glass behaviour and is a common KTU board point of discussion.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error in Glass Electrodes

| Error Type | pH Region | Cause | Magnitude | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sodium (alkaline) error** | pH > 12 | Glass becomes responsive to $Na^+$, $K^+$ ions at high pH | Up to 0.5 pH units at pH 13 | Use **Lithium glass** (Corning 724) for low-Na-error electrodes |
| **Acid error** | pH < 1 | High $H^+$ activity saturates gel layer; organic acids also give anomalous behaviour | Slight negative deviation | Use special acid-resistant glass; treat with HF |
| **Asymmetry potential drift** | All pH | Ageing, scratches, contamination, dehydration of gel | ±1 to ±5 mV (≈ 0.1 pH) | Soak electrode in KCl/HCl; recalibrate |
| **Temperature hysteresis** | Variable | Slow response of glass gel to temperature changes | Slope deviates from 59.16 mV | Use ATC (Automatic Temperature Compensation) |
| **Hygiene contamination** | All pH | Protein films, oils coat membrane | Erratic readings | Clean with pepsin-HCl or detergent; store in KCl |
| **Static charge** | All pH | High-impedance membrane picks up static | Spurious ±mV | Ground the meter; Faraday cage |
| **Streaming potential** | All pH | Vigorous stirring generates false EMF | ±1 mV | Use moderate, consistent stirring |

## 3.4 Quantitative Treatment of the Alkaline Error

When $Na^+$ ions compete with $H^+$ at the glass surface (pH > 12), the Nikolsky equation applies:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{2.303 RT}{F} \log \left( a_{H^+} + \sum K_{H^+,X^+}^{pot} \cdot a_{X^+} \right)$$

Where:
- $K_{H^+,X^+}^{pot}$ = **selectivity coefficient** for interferent ion $X^+$ (e.g., $Na^+$) over $H^+$
- $a_{X^+}$ = activity of interfering ion

Typical value: $K_{H^+,Na^+}^{pot} \approx 10^{-13}$ for Corning 015 glass (low-sodium-error glass required for biological pH measurements).

```mermaid
    nodeF --> nodeG["SALT BRIDGE KCl SATURATED"]

    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["DIGITAL pH READOUT"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Working Mechanism of the Glass Electrode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["H plus ions in test solution"] --> step2["Diffuse toward outer gel layer"]
    step2 --> step3["Ion exchange with Na plus in gel"]
    step3 --> step4["Boundary potential Eb1 develops at outer surface"]
    step1 --> step5["Internal filling solution of fixed pH"]
    step5 --> step6["Constant internal boundary potential Eb2"]
    step3 --> step7["Combined potential across glass membrane"]
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8["Asymmetry potential added Easym"]
    step8 --> step9["Measured cell EMF Ecell"]
```


## 4.4 Mermaid Graph — Potential vs pH Response Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axis1["pH 0 to 1"] --> nodeErr1["Acid error region negative deviation"]
    nodeErr1 --> region1["LINEAR NERNST REGION 59.16 mV per pH at 25 C"]
    region1 --> nodeErr2["Sodium alkaline error pH greater than 12 positive deviation"]
    nodeErr2 --> axis2["pH 14"]
    region1 --> midNode["Calibration buffers pH 4 7 10 lie here"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - In the **linear Nernst region** (pH ≈ 2 to 10), the EMF vs pH plot is a straight line with a slope of $-59.16$ mV/pH at 25°C.
> - At **pH < 1** (acid error): The curve flattens and the measured EMF is more positive than predicted.
> - At **pH > 12** (alkaline error): The curve flattens and the measured EMF is more negative than predicted, because the electrode starts responding to $Na^+$.
