Mention any two advantages.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A lithium-ion battery is called a **"rocking chair" battery** because **lithium ions (Li⁺) shuttle back and forth** between the anode (graphite) and cathode (LiCoO₂) through the electrolyte during charging and discharging cycles — analogous to a rocking chair moving between two positions. During **discharge**: Li⁺ moves from **anode → cathode** (oxidation at anode, reduction at cathode). During **charge**: Li⁺ moves from **cathode → anode** (reverse, driven by external source). **Two advantages:** 1. **High energy density** (~150–250 Wh/kg) compared to lead-acid (~40 Wh/kg). 2. **Long cycle life** (500–2000 cycles) with low self-discharge (~2% per month). 3. *(Any other valid advantage: no memory effect, lightweight, environmentally friendlier than Ni-Cd)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Write the half-cell and overall reactions of the H₂–O₂ fuel cell with acid electrolyte.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Half-cell reactions:** **At Anode (Oxidation):** $$\text{H}_2(g) \longrightarrow 2\text{H}^+(aq) + 2e^- \quad E° = 0.00\text{ V}$$ **At Cathode (Reduction):** $$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \quad E° = +1.23\text{ V}$$ **Overall Cell Reaction:** $$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \quad E°_{cell} = 1.23\text{ V}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write the cathode reaction as "O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ → 2H₂O" with $n = 4$. While thermodynamically equivalent, the **per-mole-of-H₂** convention uses $n = 2$ and **½ O₂** for cleaner board-exam presentation. Match your $n$ value to the equation you write. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Describe the **construction** of a lithium-ion battery. With suitable half-cell reactions, explain its **working** during charging and discharging. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the **standard EMF** and **theoretical specific energy (Wh/kg)** of a Li-ion cell using the LiCoO₂/LiC₆ couple. Given: $E°(\text{Li}^+/\text{Li}) = -3.04$ V, $E°(\text{CoO}_2/\text{LiCoO}_2) = +0.66$ V, $F = 96485$ C/mol, Molar mass of LiC₆ = 79 g/mol, Molar mass of CoO₂ = 91 g/mol. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks) **Construction (3 marks):** The Li-ion battery consists of the following components: 1. **Anode**: Graphite (carbon) intercalated with lithium (LiC₆) coated on a **copper** current collector. 2. **Cathode**: Lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO₂) coated on an **aluminum** current collector. (Alternatives: LiFePO₄, LiMn₂O₄) 3. **Electrolyte**: A non-aqueous solution of **lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF₆)** dissolved in a mixture of **ethylene carbonate (EC)** and **dimethyl carbonate (DMC)**. It conducts only Li⁺ ions, not electrons. 4. **Separator**: A microporous membrane of **polyethylene (PE)** or **polypropylene (PP)** that physically separates anode and cathode while allowing Li⁺ ion passage. 5. **SEI Layer (Solid Electrolyte Interphase)**: A protective film (Li₂CO₃, LiF) formed on the anode surface during the first charge cycle, preventing further electrolyte decomposition. **Working during Discharge (2 marks):** The cell acts as a galvanic cell. Li⁺ ions deintercalate from graphite anode and travel through the electrolyte to the cathode, while electrons flow through the external circuit: - **Anode (oxidation):** $\text{LiC}_6 \rightarrow \text{Li}^+ + e^- + 6\text{C}$ - **Cathode (reduction):** $\text{CoO}_2 + \text{Li}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{LiCoO}_2$ - **Overall:** $\text{LiC}_6 + \text{CoO}_2 \rightarrow 6\text{C} + \text{LiCoO}_2$ **Working during Charging (2 marks):** An external DC source reverses the reactions, forcing Li⁺ ions to migrate back to the anode: - **Cathode (oxidation):** $\text{LiCoO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CoO}_2 + \text{Li}^+ + e^-$ - **Anode (reduction):** $\text{Li}^+ + e^- + 6\text{C} \rightarrow \text{LiC}_6$ - **Overall:** $\text{LiCoO}_2 + 6\text{C} \rightarrow \text{LiC}_6 + \text{CoO}_2$ This reversible shuttling of Li⁺ is why it is called a "rocking chair" battery. --- #### Part (b) — EMF and Specific Energy Calculation (7 marks) **Step 1: Standard EMF Calculation (3 marks)** $$E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode} = (+0.66) - (-3.04) = +3.70\text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Number of electrons transferred (1 mark)** From the reaction stoichiometry, **$n = 1$** electron per Li atom transferred. **Step 3: Total molar mass of active materials (1 mark)** $$M_{total} = M(\text{LiC}_6) + M(\text{CoO}_2) = 79 + 91 = 170\text{ g mol}^{-1} = 0.170\text{ kg mol}^{-1}$$ **Step 4: Calculate energy per mole of reaction (1 mark)** $$W = nFE°_{cell} = (1)(96485)(3.70) = 356994.5\text{ J mol}^{-1}$$ Convert to Wh: $$W = \dfrac{356994.5}{3600} = 99.165\text{ Wh mol}^{-1}$$ **Step 5: Calculate specific energy (1 mark)** $$\text{Specific Energy} = \dfrac{99.165\text{ Wh mol}^{-1}}{0.170\text{ kg mol}^{-1}} = 583.3\text{ Wh kg}^{-1}$$ **Final Answer:** $E°_{cell} = 3.70$ V, Theoretical Specific Energy = **583.3 Wh/kg** (practical value is ~150–250 Wh/kg due to inactive components). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the **construction and working** of the H₂–O₂ fuel cell with acid electrolyte. Write the half-cell and overall reactions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The standard EMF of the H₂–O₂ fuel cell is **1.23 V**. Calculate (i) the standard Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G°$ for the cell reaction, and (ii) the **maximum electrical work** obtainable per kilogram of H₂ consumed. Given: $F = 96485$ C/mol, Molar mass of H₂ = 2 g/mol. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks) **Construction (3 marks):** A H₂–O₂ fuel cell consists of: 1. **Two porous carbon electrodes** impregnated with **platinum (Pt) catalyst** — one acts as the **anode** (fuel side) and the other as the **cathode** (oxidant side). 2. **Anode chamber**: Continuous supply of **H₂ gas** through a gas diffusion layer. 3. **Cathode chamber**: Continuous supply of **O₂ gas** (or air) through a gas diffusion layer. 4. **Electrolyte**: Aqueous **sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)** (or a proton-exchange membrane like Nafion) placed between the electrodes. It conducts **H⁺ ions** from anode to cathode. 5. **External circuit**: Connected across both electrodes to draw current for useful work. *(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for the schematic.)* **Working (2.5 marks):** - **At the anode**, H₂ gas adsorbs on the Pt surface, gets oxidized, and releases protons and electrons: $$\text{H}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+(aq) + 2e^-$$ - The **electrons** flow through the **external circuit** (producing electricity). - The **H⁺ ions** migrate through the acid electrolyte to the cathode. - **At the cathode**, O₂ adsorbs on the Pt surface, combines with H⁺ and incoming electrons, and is reduced to water: $$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) + 2\text{H}^+(aq) + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$ - Pure water is expelled from the cathode as the only byproduct. **Overall Reaction (1.5 marks):** $$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \quad E°_{cell} = 1.23\text{ V}$$ **Key Points:** - Fuel cell is a **primary (non-rechargeable)** device — it operates as long as H₂ and O₂ are supplied. - It converts chemical energy **directly** to electrical energy, with no combustion step. - Water is the only emission → environmentally clean. --- #### Part (b) — ΔG° and Maximum Work Calculation (7 marks) **Given:** - $E°_{cell} = 1.23$ V - $n = 2$ electrons per H₂ - $F = 96485$ C/mol - Molar mass of H₂ = 2 g/mol = $2 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol **Step 1: Calculate $\Delta G°$ (3 marks)** $$\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell}$$ $$\Delta G° = -(2)(96485\text{ C mol}^{-1})(1.23\text{ V})$$ $$\Delta G° = -237353.1\text{ J mol}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta G° = -237.35\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}}$$ *(Negative sign confirms spontaneity of the cell reaction.)* **Step 2: Calculate maximum work per mole of H₂ (1.5 marks)** $$W_{max} = -\Delta G° = 237.35\text{ kJ per mole of H}_2$$ **Step 3: Convert to per kilogram basis (2.5 marks)** Number of moles of H₂ in 1 kg: $$n_{H_2} = \dfrac{1000\text{ g}}{2\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 500\text{ mol}$$ Maximum work per kg of H₂: $$W_{max, kg} = 237.35\text{ kJ mol}^{-1} \times 500\text{ mol} = 118675\text{ kJ} = 1.187 \times 10^5\text{ kJ}$$ Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3600 kJ): $$W_{max, kg} = \dfrac{118675}{3600} = 32.97\text{ kWh per kg of H}_2$$ **Final Answer:** - $\Delta G° = -237.35$ kJ/mol - Maximum work = **~33 kWh per kg of H₂** (theoretical) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Loss Zones):** > 1. **Sign convention error:** $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell}$ — students often forget the negative sign. The cell being galvanic means $\Delta G°$ is negative. > 2. **Unit confusion:** Make sure to convert J → kJ (÷1000) and per mole → per kg (multiply by 500). > 3. **Wrong $n$:** For the reaction $\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{H}_2\text{O}$, **$n = 2$**. If you write $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \to 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$, then $n = 4$ per 2 moles of H₂ (i.e., $n = 2$ per mole of H₂). Stay consistent. > 4. **Forgetting to divide by 2:** Molar mass of **H₂ = 2 g/mol**, not 1. Many students use atomic mass of H (= 1) and lose 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Batteries & Fuel Cells** ### ✅ Li-ion Battery — Key Points - [x] Called a **"rocking chair" battery** (Li⁺ shuttles between electrodes). - [x] **Anode**: Graphite/LiC₆ | **Cathode**: LiCoO₂. - [x] Electrolyte: **LiPF₆ in organic carbonates** (non-aqueous). - [x] Separator: **PE/PP microporous membrane**. - [x] Current collectors: **Cu (anode), Al (cathode)**. - [x] SEI layer = protective film (Li₂CO₃, LiF). - [x] Discharge: LiC₆ + CoO₂ → 6C + LiCoO₂, $E_{cell} \approx 3.7$ V. - [x] Charge: Reverse reaction driven by external DC. - [x] Rechargeable, cycle life 500–2000, energy density 150–250 Wh/kg. ### ✅ H₂–O₂ Fuel Cell — Key Points - [x] **Anode**: Porous C + Pt, H₂ inlet. **Cathode**: Porous C + Pt, O₂ inlet. - [x] Electrolyte: **Aqueous H₂SO₄** (acid only as per KTU syllabus). - [x] Charge carrier: **H⁺ (proton)**. - [x] Anode: H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ ($E° = 0$ V). - [x] Cathode: ½O₂ + 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂O ($E° = +1.23$ V). - [x] Overall: H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O, $E°_{cell} = 1.23$ V. - [x] Byproduct: **Pure water only** (used in spacecraft drinking supply). - [x] Theoretical efficiency: **83%** > Carnot (70%). - [x] Primary (non-rechargeable); continuous operation with fuel supply. ### ✅ Master Equations - [x] $E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode}$ - [x] $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell}$ - [x] Nernst: $E = E° - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log Q$ (at 298 K) - [x] $\eta_{therm} = \dfrac{\Delta G°}{\Delta H°} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} = 83\%$ ### ✅ Critical Standard Potentials to Memorize - [x] $E°(\text{Li}^+/\text{Li}) = -3.04$ V - [x] $E°(\text{H}^+/\text{H}_2) = 0.00$ V - [x] $E°(\text{O}_2/\text{H}_2\text{O}, \text{acid}) = +1.23$ V ### ✅ Common KTU Comparison Traps - [x] Li-ion = **closed system** (energy stored internally, rechargeable). - [x] Fuel cell = **open system** (continuous feed, not rechargeable). - [x] Li-ion electrolyte is **non-aqueous**; fuel cell electrolyte is **aqueous acid**. - [x] Li-ion: Li⁺ charge carrier; Fuel cell: H⁺ charge carrier. - [x] Both are **galvanic cells** (spontaneous, convert chemical → electrical). ### ✅ Engineering Applications - [x] **Li-ion**: Smartphones, EVs, laptops, medical implants, grid storage. - [x] **Fuel cell**: Spacecraft (Apollo, Space Shuttle), Toyota Mirai, submarines, backup power.

topics.title: Corrosion control methods: Galvanic series, Cathodic Protection (Sacrificial anodic protection and impressed current cathodic protection)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrosion control methods: Galvanic series, Cathodic Protection (Sacrificial anodic protection and impressed current cathodic protection)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Galvanic Series — Formal Definition

The **Galvanic Series** is a practical, empirically derived list that arranges metals and alloys in the order of their **corrosion potentials measured in a specific environment (typically seawater at 25 °C)**. Unlike the Standard Electrode Potential (EMF) series, which is a thermodynamically ideal list measured under strict standard conditions (1 M, 25 °C, 1 atm), the Galvanic Series reflects **real-world corrosion behavior** where passive films, alloy composition, and ionic concentration drastically alter the actual potential.

The metal placed at the **top (most noble/cathodic end)** is the most resistant to corrosion, while the metal at the **bottom (most active/anodic end)** corrodes most readily. The greater the separation between two metals in this series, the greater the driving voltage and hence the more severe the **galvanic corrosion** when they are electrically coupled in an electrolyte.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Galvanic Series is **environment-dependent**. A single, universal galvanic series does not exist; the series for seawater differs from that for soil or for industrial chemical environments. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

## 1.2 Cathodic Protection — Formal Definition

**Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical corrosion control technique in which the metal (structure) to be protected is forced to become the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell, thereby suppressing the anodic (oxidation) reaction. By providing electrons from an external source to the protected metal, the oxidation reaction $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^-$ is suppressed.

There are two principal methods:
1. **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)** — also called *galvanic cathodic protection*.
2. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** — also called *inert anode cathodic protection*.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam of Electrons"

Think of corrosion as a leaking water tank (the metal), where water (electrons) is being lost through holes (anodic sites). 

- In **SACP**, you attach a "donor tank" made of a more reactive metal (like zinc) that willingly leaks its water into the main tank. The donor empties first, saving the main tank. The donor is "sacrificed."
- In **ICCP**, you connect a pump (a DC rectifier powered by mains AC) that actively pushes electrons into the main tank from an inert source, so the main tank never loses its own water.

The **Galvanic Series** is like a "pecking order" — metals at the bottom always sacrifice themselves for metals at the top when coupled in an electrolyte.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in CP Design:**
> - Density of seawater: **~1.025 g/cm³**
> - Standard reference electrode: **Copper/Copper Sulphate (CSE)** = **+0.316 V vs SHE**; **Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE)** = **+0.244 V vs SHE**; **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl)** = **+0.222 V vs SHE**
> - Typical protection potential for steel: **−0.85 V vs CSE**

---

## 1.4 Placement of the Galvanic Series in Corrosion Control Strategy

Corrosion control methods are broadly classified as:

| Strategy | Method | Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Material Selection** | Using noble alloys | Reduce reactivity |
| **Environmental Control** | Deaeration, desiccation, inhibitors | Remove aggressive species |
| **Surface Coatings** | Paints, platings, conversion coatings | Physical barrier |
| **Design Modification** | Avoid crevices, galvanic couples | Geometry control |
| **Electrochemical Control** | **Cathodic Protection**, Anodic Protection | Shift potential |

Cathodic Protection falls under the **electrochemical control** strategy and is the only method that **actively neutralizes the driving force** of corrosion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Galvanic Series in Detail

### 2.1.1 Partial Galvanic Series (Seawater Environment)

From **most noble (cathodic)** to **most active (anodic)**:

1. Platinum
2. Gold
3. Graphite
4. Titanium
5. Silver
6. Stainless Steel (passive, e.g., 304, 316)
7. Nickel (passive)
8. Copper-Nickel alloys (70/30, 90/10)
9. Bronze (Cu-Sn)
10. Copper
11. Brass (Cu-Zn)
12. Nickel (active)
13. Tin
14. Lead
15. Stainless Steel (active)
16. Cast Iron
17. Mild Steel / Carbon Steel
18. Aluminium alloys
19. Zinc
20. Magnesium alloys
21. Magnesium (most active)

### 2.1.2 Galvanic Series vs Standard EMF Series — Why Both?

- **EMF Series**: Thermodynamic, based on standard reduction potentials. Useful for predicting **whether** a reaction is spontaneous.
- **Galvanic Series**: Kinetic/Empirical, based on actual measured corrosion potentials. Useful for predicting **how fast** corrosion proceeds in real conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> For example, **tin and lead** appear *above* steel in the EMF series (suggesting they are more noble), but in the Galvanic Series they sit *below* certain stainless steels in passive state. The key engineering implication: when **tin-coated steel** is scratched, the tin acts as a *cathode* and corrosion accelerates at the exposed steel underneath. Hence, **tin-plating is "sacrificial in reverse"** — once breached, it accelerates corrosion.

### 2.1.3 Critical Engineering Implications

- **Area Effect**: A small anodic area coupled to a large cathodic area causes **intense localized corrosion** (worst case). E.g., copper rivets in a steel plate → copper is cathode, steel rivet holes corrode.
- **Distance Effect**: Galvanic corrosion is severe when the two metals are in close electrical contact; it diminishes with distance in the electrolyte.
- **Environment Effect**: pH, dissolved oxygen, chloride concentration, and temperature all shift the position of metals in the series.

## 2.2 Cathodic Protection — Working Principle

The fundamental thermodynamic relationship is the **Nernst Equation**:

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_{\text{Red}}}{a_{\text{Ox}}}
$$

Where:
- $E$ = electrode potential (V)
- $E^{\circ}$ = standard reduction potential (V)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $a$ = activities of reduced/oxidized species

To suppress anodic dissolution of metal M:

$$
\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^- \quad \text{(must be suppressed)}
$$

We supply electrons so that the metal potential is shifted **cathodically (more negative)** until the anodic reaction rate → 0.

## 2.3 Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)

### 2.3.1 Principle

A metal that is **more active (anodic)** than the structure to be protected is electrically connected to the structure. The active metal **sacrifices itself** by corroding preferentially, supplying electrons to the protected structure, making it the cathode.

### 2.3.2 Commonly Used Sacrificial Anodes

| Anode Material | Anode Potential (V vs CSE) | Capacity (A·h/kg) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | −1.55 | 1230 | Soil, freshwater pipelines |
| **Magnesium Alloy (AZ63)** | −1.50 | 1230 | High-resistivity soils |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | −1.10 | 820 | Seawater applications (ships, offshore) |
| **Aluminium Alloy (Al-Zn-In)** | −1.05 | 2700 | Offshore platforms, marine |
| **Aluminium Alloy (Al-Zn-Sn)** | −1.00 | 2700 | Marine, hot structures |

### 2.3.3 Current Capacity of Anode

The theoretical current capacity of an anode is given by:

$$
C = \frac{nF}{M} \times 1000 \quad \text{(A·h/kg)}
$$

Where $M$ = atomic mass, $n$ = valency of anode metal.

For **zinc** ($M = 65.4$ g/mol, $n = 2$):

$$
C_{\text{Zn}} = \frac{2 \times 96485}{65.4} \times \frac{1}{3600} \approx 820 \text{ A·h/kg}
$$

### 2.3.4 Design Procedure for SACP (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Determine the surface area** ($A$, in m²) of the structure to be protected.
2. **Determine the current density requirement** ($i$, in A/m²). Typical values:
   - Bare steel in seawater: 80–150 mA/m² initially → 30–50 mA/m² steady state
   - Coated steel: 1–10 mA/m²
3. **Calculate total current** required: $I_{\text{total}} = i \times A$
4. **Select anode material** based on environment.
5. **Calculate mass of anodes**: 

$$
m_{\text{anode}} = \frac{I_{\text{total}} \times t \times 8760}{C \times U \times 1000} \quad \text{(kg)}
$$

Where:
- $t$ = design life (years)
- $U$ = utilization factor (typically 0.85 for Zn, 0.5 for Mg)
- 8760 = hours per year

6. **Determine number of anodes** by dividing the total mass by the mass of a single anode.

### 2.3.5 Advantages and Limitations of SACP

**Advantages:**
- No external power source required
- Simple installation
- Low maintenance
- Self-regulating (current adjusts to environment)
- Reliable for small structures

**Limitations:**
- **Limited driving voltage** (max ~0.25 V for Zn-steel in seawater)
- **Not suitable for high-resistivity electrolytes** (e.g., dry or rocky soil)
- Frequent anode replacement
- Lower current output compared to ICCP

## 2.4 Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)

### 2.4.1 Principle

An **external DC power source** (rectifier, solar panel, or thermoelectric generator) is used. The **positive terminal** is connected to an **inert (impressed current) anode**, and the **negative terminal** is connected to the **structure to be protected**. Electrons are "impressed" (forced) from the external source into the structure, making it the cathode.

### 2.4.2 Typical ICCP Anode Materials

| Anode Type | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Impressed Current Anodes** | | |
| Graphite | C | Soil, freshwater |
| High-Silicon Cast Iron (HSCI) | Fe-14%Si-5%Cr | Soil, seawater |
| Lead-Silver Alloy | Pb-2%Ag | Seawater |
| Magnetite | Fe₃O₄ | Soil, marine |
| Titanium with MMO coating | Ti + Mixed Metal Oxide | Universal, modern |
| Platinum-coated Titanium | Ti/Pt | Critical marine structures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ICCP anodes are **dimensionally stable and inert** — they do not dissolve. The electrolyte is decomposed at the anode surface: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$

### 2.4.3 Current and Voltage Output

Typical ICCP rectifier specifications:
- **Voltage range**: 5–50 V DC
- **Current output**: up to 100 A (or higher for large pipelines)
- **Power source**: AC mains, solar PV, wind, or thermoelectric

### 2.4.4 Advantages and Limitations of ICCP

**Advantages:**
- **Unlimited current capacity** — can protect very large structures
- **Adjustable voltage and current** — adaptable to any environment
- **Anodes are not consumed** — long life
- **Suitable for high-resistivity soils**

**Limitations:**
- Requires external power → ongoing electricity cost
- More complex installation and maintenance
- Risk of **overprotection** → causes **cathodic disbondment, hydrogen embrittlement**, and coating damage
- Needs continuous monitoring

> [!WARNING]
> **Overprotection Warning (KTU Frequently Tested):** If the protected structure is driven too negative (below −1.20 V vs CSE for steel), it triggers excessive hydrogen evolution: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^-$. The hydrogen atoms can enter steel, causing **hydrogen embrittlement**, and can delaminate protective coatings.

## 2.5 Selection Criteria — SACP vs ICCP

| Parameter | SACP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | None (galvanic) | External DC |
| Driving voltage | Low (≤ 0.3 V) | High (5–50 V) |
| Anode consumption | Sacrificed continuously | Inert, lasts years |
| Best for resistivity | < 5000 Ω·cm | Any resistivity |
| Best for current | Low to moderate | High current demand |
| Typical structures | Ships, small tanks, short pipelines | Long pipelines, offshore platforms, jetties |
| Cost | Low initial, high recurring | High initial, low recurring |
| Monitoring | Less critical | Continuous monitoring required |
| Installation complexity | Simple | Complex |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nernst Equation | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{a_{\text{Red}}}{a_{\text{Ox}}}$ | V | $T$ in K, $F$ = 96485 C/mol |
| Faraday's Law (mass) | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | kg | $M$ = molar mass, $t$ in s |
| Anode Capacity | $C = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M \cdot 3.6} \times 1000$ | A·h/kg | Divides by 3600 to convert C→A·h |
| Total Current | $I_{\text{total}} = i \cdot A$ | A | $i$ in A/m², $A$ in m² |
| Anode Mass Required | $m = \dfrac{I_{\text{total}} \cdot t_{\text{life}} \cdot 8760}{C \cdot U \cdot 1000}$ | kg | $t$ in years, $U$ utilization |
| Number of Anodes | $N = \dfrac{m_{\text{total}}}{m_{\text{single anode}}}$ | dimensionless | Round up to integer |
| Anode Life | $t = \dfrac{m \cdot U \cdot C}{I_{\text{total}} \cdot 8760}$ | years | Inverse of anode mass eqn |
| Cell Driving Voltage | $\Delta V = E_{\text{anode}} - E_{\text{structure}}$ | V | Magnitude drives CP current |
| Protection Criterion (Steel) | $E_{\text{steel}} \leq -0.85$ V vs CSE | V | KTU standard threshold |
| Coating Breakdown Factor | $f_c = $ (Coated area × coating current + Bare area × bare current) / Total area | — | Used in mixed systems |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Marine**: Hulls of ships, ballast tanks, offshore platform legs (e.g., SACP with Al-Zn-In anodes)
- **Underground**: Cross-country oil/gas pipelines (ICCP with MMO/Ti anodes, often with solar power in remote areas)
- **Reinforced Concrete**: Bridge decks, parking structures, marine piers (CP against chloride-induced rebar corrosion)
- **Water treatment**: Internal protection of water storage tanks, clarifiers
- **Power plants**: Underground cable sheaths, condenser water boxes
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Current Capacity of a Sacrificial Anode

**Statement:** Show that 1 kg of pure zinc theoretically delivers ~820 A·h of charge when used as a sacrificial anode.

**Step 1:** Write the anodic dissolution reaction of zinc:

$$
\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the number of electrons transferred per atom:

$$
n = 2
$$

**Step 3:** Determine the molar mass of zinc:

$$
M_{\text{Zn}} = 65.4 \text{ g/mol} = 0.0654 \text{ kg/mol}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis — total charge $Q$ delivered by $m$ mass of metal:

$$
Q = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M}
$$

For $m = 1$ kg of zinc:

$$
Q = \frac{1 \text{ kg} \times 2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}}{0.0654 \text{ kg/mol}}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the numerator:

$$
1 \times 2 \times 96485 = 192970 \text{ C}
$$

**Step 6:** Divide by molar mass:

$$
Q = \frac{192970}{0.0654} = 2{,}950{,}611 \text{ C (per kg)}
$$

**Step 7:** Convert coulombs to ampere-hours using $1 \text{ A·h} = 3600 \text{ C}$:

$$
C = \frac{2{,}950{,}611}{3600} \approx 819.6 \text{ A·h/kg} \approx 820 \text{ A·h/kg}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{Zn}} \approx 820 \text{ A·h/kg}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the *theoretical* maximum. The *practical* capacity is lower because of **utilization factor** ($U \approx 0.85$) and self-corrosion losses. Practical capacity $\approx 820 \times 0.85 \approx 697$ A·h/kg.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: SACP Design for a Submarine Pipeline

**Problem Statement:** A bare steel pipeline of diameter 0.30 m and length 2 km is laid in seawater. Design current density required = 100 mA/m². Zinc sacrificial anodes of 5 kg each with utilization factor 0.85 are available. Find: (a) Total current required, (b) Number of anodes for a 5-year design life.

### Step (a): Total Current Required

**Step 1:** Surface area of the cylindrical pipeline:

$$
A = \pi D L
$$

Where $D$ = 0.30 m, $L$ = 2000 m.

$$
A = \pi \times 0.30 \times 2000
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate:

$$
A = 3.14159 \times 600 = 1884.96 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Total current from current density:

$$
I_{\text{total}} = i \times A = 0.100 \text{ A/m}^2 \times 1884.96 \text{ m}^2 = 188.5 \text{ A}
$$

### Step (b): Anode Mass Required

**Step 1:** Design life: $t = 5$ years

**Step 2:** Hours in 5 years: $5 \times 8760 = 43{,}800$ hours

**Step 3:** Practical capacity of zinc anode: 

$$
C_{\text{practical}} = 820 \times 0.85 = 697 \text{ A·h/kg}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the mass formula:

$$
m_{\text{total}} = \frac{I_{\text{total}} \times t \times 8760}{C \times U}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute:

$$
m_{\text{total}} = \frac{188.5 \times 43{,}800}{697}
$$

**Step 6:** Numerator:

$$
188.5 \times 43{,}800 = 8{,}256{,}300
$$

**Step 7:** Divide:

$$
m_{\text{total}} = \frac{8{,}256{,}300}{697} = 11{,}844 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 8:** Number of anodes:

$$
N = \frac{11{,}844 \text{ kg}}{5 \text{ kg/anode}} = 2368.8 \rightarrow 2369 \text{ anodes}
$$

### Final Answer:

$$
\boxed{I_{\text{total}} = 188.5 \text{ A}, \quad N = 2369 \text{ anodes}}
$$

> **Engineering note:** This enormous number of anodes is precisely why pipelines of this scale usually use **ICCP** — only 4–6 impressed current anodes distributed along the pipeline are needed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Circuit Analysis of a CP Cell

The CP system behaves like a **Thévenin equivalent circuit** driving current through the electrolyte resistance.

**Step 1:** The Thévenin voltage (driving EMF):

$$
V_{\text{th}} = E_{\text{anode}} - E_{\text{structure (cathode)}}
$$

For Zn anode (−1.10 V vs CSE) and steel structure (−0.55 V vs CSE naturally):

$$
V_{\text{th}} = -1.10 - (-0.55) = -0.55 \text{ V}
$$

Magnitude: $\vert V_{\text{th}} \vert = 0.55$ V

**Step 2:** The total circuit resistance is the sum of:

$$
R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{anode-electrolyte}} + R_{\text{electrolyte}} + R_{\text{cathode-electrolyte}} + R_{\text{metallic path}}
$$

**Step 3:** The current delivered is:

$$
I = \frac{V_{\text{th}}}{R_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 4:** As the anode corrodes, $R_{\text{anode-electrolyte}}$ increases, causing $I$ to **decrease gradually** — this is the **self-regulating** nature of SACP.

**Step 5:** In contrast, ICCP uses a regulated rectifier to maintain a constant $V_{\text{out}}$, ensuring stable current independent of circuit resistance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Algorithm

| Condition | Recommendation | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Small structure, low-resistivity electrolyte (seawater) | SACP | Simple, cheap, reliable |
| Long pipeline, high-resistivity soil | ICCP | High driving voltage overcomes resistance |
| Remote area, no power grid | SACP (Mg anode) or ICCP with solar | Mg for high-resistivity soil; solar ICCP for pipelines |
| Marine, large hull | SACP (Al alloy) | Al alloy has high capacity (2700 A·h/kg) |
| Existing structure, retrofit | ICCP | Easier to retrofit with few anodes |
| Cathodically sensitive material (e.g., Al) | Controlled ICCP with low voltage | Avoids overprotection-induced alkali attack |

## 3.5 Worked Example: Cathodic Protection Potential Calculation

**Problem:** A buried steel pipeline has a measured open-circuit potential of −0.62 V vs CSE. Calculate the additional cathodic polarization (in V) needed to achieve the protection criterion of −0.85 V vs CSE.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Required shift:

$$
\Delta E = E_{\text{measured}} - E_{\text{protection}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
\Delta E = -0.62 - (-0.85) = +0.23 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3:** The pipeline must be polarized by **0.23 V** in the cathodic (negative) direction.

**Step 4:** In terms of current (using polarization resistance $R_p = 0.1$ Ω·m²):

$$
\Delta I = \frac{\Delta E}{R_p} = \frac{0.23}{0.1} = 2.3 \text{ A/m}^2 \text{ (additional current required)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The −0.85 V vs CSE criterion is **not a thermodynamic** value — it is an **empirical** threshold below which the anodic dissolution of iron effectively stops because the local environment becomes so alkaline (from oxygen reduction $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$) that iron forms a stable passive film.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map of Corrosion Control Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corrosion Control Methods] --> B[Material Selection]
    A --> C[Environmental Control]
    A --> D[Surface Coatings]
    A --> E[Design Modification]
    A --> F[Electrochemical Control]
    B --> B1[Noble alloys]
    B --> B2[Pure metals]
    C --> C1[Deaeration]
    C --> C2[Inhibitors]
    D --> D1[Paints]
    D --> D2[Platings]
    E --> E1[Avoid galvanic couples]
    E --> E2[Avoid crevices]
    F --> F1[Cathodic Protection]
    F --> F2[Anodic Protection]
    F1 --> G1[Sacrificial Anode CP - SACP]
    F1 --> G2[Impressed Current CP - ICCP]
    G1 --> H1[Active metal: Mg / Zn / Al]
    G1 --> H2[No external power]
    G2 --> I1[Inert anode: MMO Ti / Graphite / Pt]
    G2 --> I2[External DC rectifier]
```

## 4.2 Galvanic Series — Schematic Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N[NOBLE CATHODE END - Most Resistant] --> P1[Platinum]
    P1 --> P2[Gold]
    P2 --> P3[Titanium]
    P3 --> P4[Stainless Steel Passive]
    P4 --> P5[Copper Nickel Alloys]
    P5 --> P6[Copper Bronze Brass]
    P6 --> P7[Lead Tin]
    P7 --> P8[Cast Iron]
    P8 --> P9[Mild Steel]
    P9 --> P10[Aluminium Alloys]
    P10 --> P11[Zinc]
    P11 --> P12[Magnesium Alloys]
    P12 --> A[ACTIVE ANODE END - Most Corroded]
```

> **Reading the series:** When two metals are coupled, the **lower one** in the diagram corrodes (becomes anode) and the **higher one** is protected (becomes cathode).

## 4.3 Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) — System Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sacrificial Anode Zn Mg Al] -->|Oxidation ne- lost| B[Electrons flow through wire]
    B --> C[Protected Steel Structure - Cathode]
    C -->|Electrons consumed at surface| D[Oxygen Reduction Reaction]
    D -->|Forms OH- protective environment| E[Passive Film on Steel]
    A -->|Zn2+ ions released| F[Seawater Electrolyte]
    F -->|Conducts ions| C
    subgraph SAR[Anodic Reaction on Sacrificial Anode]
        A
    end
    subgraph CRR[Cathodic Reaction on Protected Structure]
        C
        D
        E
    end
```

### Electrode Reactions (Text Annotation)

**At the Anode (Zinc):**
$$
\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-
$$

**At the Cathode (Steel surface):**
$$
\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-
$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**
$$
2\text{Zn} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{Zn}^{2+} + 4\text{OH}^-
$$

> **Net effect:** Zinc dissolves. Steel is protected. The system continues until the anode is fully consumed.

## 4.4 Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) — System Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC[AC Mains Supply] --> R[DC Rectifier]
    R -->|Positive terminal| IA[Inert Anode MMO Ti or Graphite]
    IA -->|Current passes through electrolyte| ST[Protected Steel Structure - Cathode]
    R -->|Negative terminal| ST
    ST -->|Electrons supplied to surface| OF[Oxygen Reduction Forms OH-]
    IA -->|Oxidation reaction| OR[O2 evolution 2H2O to O2 + 4H + 4e-]
    OR --> IA
    subgraph RE[Rectifier and Power Module]
        AC
        R
    end
    subgraph AN[Inert Anode Module]
        IA
        OR
    end
    subgraph PR[Protected Structure Module]
        ST
        OF
    end
```

### Electrode Reactions (Text Annotation)

**At the Inert Anode:**
$$
2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 \uparrow + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-
$$
(Or in chloride environment: $2\text{Cl}^- \rightarrow \text{Cl}_2 \uparrow + 2e^-$)

**At the Cathode (Protected Steel):**
$$
\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-
$$

> **Net effect:** The rectifier pushes electrons from the anode to the structure. The structure is protected. The anode is not consumed; only water/chloride is oxidized.

## 4.5 Comparison Flowchart — When to Use SACP vs ICCP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Choose CP Method] --> Q1{Size of structure?}
    Q1 -->|Small to medium| Q2{Is external power available?}
    Q1 -->|Large and long| S2[Use ICCP]
    Q2 -->|No power grid| S1[Use SACP Mg or Al anodes]
    Q2 -->|Yes power grid| Q3{Soil electrolyte resistivity?}
    Q3 -->|Below 5000 ohm cm| S1
    Q3 -->|Above 5000 ohm cm| S2
    Q1 -->|Retrofit existing| S2
    Q1 -->|New installation small| S1
```

## 4.6 Protection Potential Monitoring Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Electrode CSE] --> RM[Reference Half Cell]
    ST[Protected Steel Structure] --> TM[Test Point Connection]
    RM --> V[High Impedance Voltmeter]
    TM --> V
    V --> D[Display -850 mV threshold check]
    D -->|Above 850 mV| E1[Increase current]
    D -->|Below 850 mV| E2[OK Protected]
    D -->|Below 1200 mV| E3[Warning Overprotection]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]

**Distinguish between Galvanic Series and Electrochemical Series (EMF Series).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Galvanic Series | EMF Series |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Empirically measured corrosion potentials in a specific environment (e.g., seawater) | Standard reduction potentials at 25 °C, 1 M concentration |
| **Environment** | Environment-specific (changes with electrolyte) | Independent of environment |
| **Lists** | Metals and **alloys** | Only pure elements |
| **Anomaly** | Tin and lead are *below* steel even though EMF suggests otherwise | Strict order based on $E^{\circ}$ |
| **Use** | Practical prediction of galvanic corrosion | Thermodynamic prediction of reaction spontaneity |

*(1 mark for each correct distinction; full table ⇒ 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]

**What is meant by "sacrificial anode" in cathodic protection? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A sacrificial anode is a metal that is **intentionally allowed to corrode** in order to supply electrons to the metal structure being protected. The sacrificial anode is more **electrochemically active** (anodic) than the protected metal, so it preferentially oxidizes, while the protected structure becomes the cathode. *(2 marks)*

**Examples** *(1 mark)*:
1. **Zinc** anodes used on ship hulls in seawater
2. **Magnesium** anodes used for buried pipelines in soil
3. **Aluminium alloys** (e.g., Al-Zn-In) for offshore platforms

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question has internal choice — answer **either Option A or Option B**. Each option is split into sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks.

---

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a) Explain the Galvanic Series of metals with a neat diagram. Discuss the significance of the relative position of metals in predicting galvanic corrosion.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Galvanic Series is an arrangement of metals and alloys in the order of their **measured corrosion potentials in a specific environment**, typically seawater at 25 °C. The most noble (cathodic) metals are at the top, and the most active (anodic) metals are at the bottom.

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the schematic in **Section 4.2** for the order from Platinum → Gold → Ti → Stainless Steel → Cu alloys → Steel → Al → Zn → Mg.

**Significance (4 marks):**

1. **Predicting Galvanic Corrosion Direction:** When two dissimilar metals are coupled in an electrolyte, the one lower in the series becomes the anode and corrodes. E.g., steel rivets in a copper plate → steel corrodes.

2. **Severity Estimation:** The greater the separation in the series, the higher the driving voltage and the more severe the galvanic corrosion. E.g., Mg–Pt couple is far more damaging than Zn–Fe couple.

3. **Selection of Materials for Coupled Systems:** In a single structure (e.g., pipe + flange), the two metals should be as close as possible in the series to minimize galvanic attack. Avoid combinations like Mg-Al, Zn-Cu.

4. **Choice of Sacrificial Anode:** The anode must be far more active than the protected structure. Hence, Mg, Zn, and Al are used for protecting steel.

5. **Area Effect Warning:** A small anodic area + large cathodic area → severe localized attack (worst case). A large anode + small cathode → mild, distributed corrosion (safer design).

**(b) With a neat sketch, explain the principle and working of Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP). List two commonly used sacrificial anode materials and their applications.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):** SACP uses a more electrochemically active metal (the sacrificial anode) electrically connected to the structure to be protected. The active metal corrodes preferentially, supplying electrons to the structure, making it the cathode. The sacrificial anode is "consumed" over time.

**Working with Sketch (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.3** for the schematic. 

- **Anode reaction:** $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$ (oxidation, dissolution)
- **Cathode reaction:** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ (oxygen reduction)
- **Net effect:** Zinc dissolves; steel is protected. The system is self-regulating because as the anode corrodes, its surface area decreases, and current output drops gradually.

**Anode Materials and Applications (2 marks):**

| Anode | Application |
|---|---|
| **Zinc** | Ship hulls, offshore platforms in seawater |
| **Magnesium (AZ63 alloy)** | Buried pipelines in soil, freshwater tanks |
| **Aluminium alloy (Al-Zn-In)** | Offshore platforms, hot marine structures |

**Advantages:** No external power, simple installation, self-regulating.

**Limitations:** Limited driving voltage, frequent replacement, not suitable for high-resistivity electrolytes.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Alternative Choice]

**(a) Explain the principle, working, and design procedure of Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) system. Compare its performance with Sacrificial Anode CP.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):** In ICCP, an external DC power source (rectifier connected to AC mains, or solar PV panel) is used. The **positive terminal** of the rectifier is connected to an **inert (impressed current) anode**, and the **negative terminal** is connected to the **structure to be protected**. Electrons are "impressed" (forced) from the rectifier into the structure, suppressing its anodic dissolution.

**Working with Sketch (2 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.4** for the schematic. The anodic reaction occurs at the inert anode (oxygen or chlorine evolution), and the cathodic reaction (oxygen reduction) occurs on the protected structure. The rectifier maintains a **constant protection potential of −0.85 V vs CSE** through automatic control.

**Design Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Determine total surface area $A$ of the structure.
2. Choose current density $i$ based on environment and coating condition.
3. Calculate $I_{\text{total}} = i \times A$.
4. Select rectifier rating ($V$, $A$).
5. Decide number and placement of inert anodes for uniform current distribution.
6. Install reference electrodes and monitoring system.

**Comparison with SACP (1 mark):**

| Aspect | SACP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | None | External DC |
| Driving voltage | ≤ 0.55 V | Up to 50 V |
| Anode life | Sacrificed | Inert, 20+ years |
| Best for | Small, low-resistivity | Large, high-resistivity |
| Cost | Low initial, high recurring | High initial, low recurring |

**(b) Calculate the number of zinc anodes required to protect a bare steel pipeline of length 1500 m and diameter 0.25 m in seawater for 5 years. Take current density = 120 mA/m², anode mass = 8 kg each, theoretical capacity = 820 A·h/kg, utilization factor = 0.85.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Surface area of pipeline (1 mark)**

$$
A = \pi D L = 3.14159 \times 0.25 \times 1500 = 1178.1 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Total current required (1 mark)**

$$
I_{\text{total}} = i \times A = 0.120 \text{ A/m}^2 \times 1178.1 \text{ m}^2 = 141.4 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3: Total charge required over 5 years (1 mark)**

$$
Q = I \times t = 141.4 \text{ A} \times 5 \times 8760 \text{ h} = 6{,}193{,}320 \text{ A·h}
$$

**Step 4: Practical anode capacity (1 mark)**

$$
C_{\text{practical}} = 820 \times 0.85 = 697 \text{ A·h/kg}
$$

**Step 5: Total anode mass required (1 mark)**

$$
m_{\text{total}} = \frac{Q}{C_{\text{practical}}} = \frac{6{,}193{,}320}{697} = 8885.7 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 6: Number of anodes (1 mark)**

$$
N = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{m_{\text{single}}} = \frac{8885.7}{8} = 1110.7
$$

**Step 7: Final answer (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{N = 1111 \text{ anodes (rounded up)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** Always convert current density to A/m² *before* multiplication with area. A common mistake is using 120 (forgetting to convert mA → A).
> 2. **Use practical capacity, not theoretical:** Multiplying theoretical 820 A·h/kg by utilization factor 0.85 is essential. Some students forget this and get inflated anode counts.
> 3. **Hours per year = 8760:** Forgetting to convert years → hours is a frequent error.
> 4. **Round up, not down:** Always round **up** the number of anodes to the next integer. Slight over-design is acceptable; under-design causes under-protection.
> 5. **Final units:** Mark the final answer with **units** (number of anodes is dimensionless, but explicitly stating "1111 anodes" earns full credit).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Galvanic Series** = empirical, environment-specific list of metals/alloys ranked by measured corrosion potential.
- [ ] **EMF Series** = theoretical, based on standard reduction potentials.
- [ ] **Top of Galvanic Series** = most noble (cathode) → bottom = most active (anode).
- [ ] **Cathodic Protection** = forcing a metal to act as a cathode to suppress anodic dissolution.
- [ ] **SACP** = uses an active metal (Mg, Zn, Al) as sacrificial anode; no external power.
- [ ] **ICCP** = uses external DC rectifier + inert anode (MMO/Ti, graphite, HSCI, Pb-Ag).
- [ ] **Protection criterion for steel** = **−0.85 V vs CSE** (or equivalent for other reference electrodes).
- [ ] **Theoretical capacity of Zn** = **820 A·h/kg**; of Al alloy = **2700 A·h/kg**; of Mg = **1230 A·h/kg**.
- [ ] **Utilization factor** $U$ accounts for non-uniform consumption; typical values: Zn ≈ 0.85, Mg ≈ 0.5, Al ≈ 0.7–0.9.
- [ ] **Total current** $I = i \times A$; **total charge** $Q = I \times t$; **anode mass** $m = Q / (C \times U)$.
- [ ] **Hours per year = 8760**; Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.
- [ ] **Overprotection** (below −1.20 V vs CSE for steel) → **hydrogen embrittlement** and **coating disbondment**.
- [ ] **Area effect**: small anode + large cathode = severe localized corrosion. **Avoid in design**.
- [ ] **SACP** is preferred for small structures, low-resistivity electrolytes, no-power-available situations.
- [ ] **ICCP** is preferred for large structures, high-resistivity soils, retrofit applications.
- [ ] **Cathode reaction** in CP: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ (oxygen reduction).
- [ ] **Anode reaction in SACP**: $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^-$ (metal dissolution).
- [ ] **Anode reaction in ICCP**: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$ (oxygen evolution at inert anode).
- [ ] **Tin plating on steel**: Once scratched, tin becomes cathode → accelerates steel corrosion (counter-intuitive but vital).
- [ ] **Anode efficiency** in seawater: highest for Al alloys; lowest for Mg (due to self-corrosion).
- [ ] **Reference electrodes** for field measurement: CSE (most common in soil), Ag/AgCl (seawater), SCE (laboratory).
- [ ] **KTU Exam Trap**: Galvanic Series is *not* universal — it changes with electrolyte. Always specify the environment in your answer.
- [ ] **Faraday's Law in CP design** is a must — practice at least 2 numerical problems from the KTU pattern.
- [ ] **Distinguish "cathodic protection" from "anodic protection"**: CP is for active metals (Fe, Zn); AP is for passive metals (stainless steel, Ti).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroplating of copper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electroplating of copper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electroplating** is an **electrolytic process** in which a thin, adherent, and uniform layer of a desired metal (such as **copper, gold, silver, chromium, or nickel**) is deposited onto the surface of a conductive substrate (the *cathode*) by passing a **direct electric current** through an electrolyte solution containing ions of the plating metal.

**Electroplating of copper** is a specific electrochemical deposition process in which a uniform, adherent coating of **copper (Cu)** is electrodeposited onto a metallic or conductive substrate (commonly **steel, iron, brass, or zinc die-castings**) using an aqueous electrolyte — most commonly the **acidic copper sulfate (CuSO₄·5H₂O) bath** — to improve electrical conductivity, corrosion resistance, solderability, and surface aesthetics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the **acid copper sulfate bath**, the composition is typically: **CuSO₄·5H₂O (200–250 g/L)**, **H₂SO₄ (40–60 g/L)**, with trace **chloride ions (60–100 ppm)**. Pure electrolytic copper is used as the **anode**, and the object to be plated serves as the **cathode**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have an old, rusty iron gate that you want to give a shiny, protective copper skin. You cannot simply paint copper onto it (paint flakes), and you cannot melt copper over it (the iron would warp). Instead, **electrochemistry gives you a magical "atomic paintbrush"**.

Think of it this way:
- **The electrolyte bath** is like a swimming pool filled with **copper atoms dissolved in acid** (Cu²⁺ ions floating in water).
- **The copper anode** is like a **"copper candy dispenser"** — it slowly dissolves and refills the pool with fresh Cu²⁺ ions.
- **The DC power supply** acts like a **magnet for ions**, pushing positive copper ions (Cu²⁺) toward the gate.
- **The iron gate (cathode)** receives these ions, which **grab electrons from the power supply and turn into solid copper atoms**, sticking firmly to the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Electroplating is essentially **"reverse corrosion"** — instead of metal dissolving, we *force* metal to deposit using external electricity.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Checklist

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Anode** | Positive electrode (where oxidation occurs; here, copper dissolves) |
| **Cathode** | Negative electrode (where reduction occurs; here, the object to be plated) |
| **Electrolyte** | Aqueous solution containing Cu²⁺ ions (CuSO₄ + H₂SO₄) |
| **Current density** | Current per unit area of cathode (typically **2–4 A/dm²** for acid copper bath) |
| **Throwing power** | Ability of the bath to deposit uniformly on irregular shapes |
| **Faraday's Constant (F)** | **96,485 C/mol** of electrons (≈ 96,500 C/mol) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Principle

Copper electroplating is governed by **two simultaneous half-reactions** occurring at the two electrodes, connected externally through a DC power source:

### 2.1.1 At the Anode (Oxidation) — Copper Dissolution

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cu (s)} &\longrightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} \text{(aq)} + 2e^{-}
\end{aligned}$$

**Standard potential:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34 \text{ V}$ vs. SHE

The anode is the **source of copper ions**. It continuously dissolves, replenishing the Cu²⁺ concentration in the bath. This is why a **pure copper anode (>99.9%)** is mandatory.

### 2.1.2 At the Cathode (Reduction) — Copper Deposition

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cu}^{2+} \text{(aq)} + 2e^{-} &\longrightarrow \text{Cu (s)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Why it sticks:** The freshly reduced Cu atoms nucleate on the cathode surface and form **metallic bonds** with the substrate, creating an adherent coating.

### 2.1.3 Overall Cell Reaction

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cu (anode)} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \text{(solution)} &\longrightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} \text{(solution)} + \text{Cu (cathode)}
\end{aligned}$$

The **net reaction** is essentially the **transfer of copper atoms** from anode to cathode through the solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the reaction "works" without a battery in theory:** The standard cell potential is **zero** ($E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = 0$) because both electrodes are the same metal. In practice, the cell has **internal resistance**, **overpotential**, and **concentration gradients**, so a small voltage (**1.5 – 6 V DC**) is applied to drive the reaction at a desired rate.

## 2.2 The Complete Electroplating Workflow (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1: Surface Preparation (Pre-treatment)** — The "clean canvas" stage
- **Degreasing** with alkaline cleaners (NaOH, Na₂CO₃) to remove oils and grease.
- **Pickling** with dilute H₂SO₄ or HCl to remove rust, scale, and oxides.
- **Rinsing** with deionized water to prevent contamination of the plating bath.

**Why?** Any contamination → **poor adhesion, pitting, or peeling** of the copper layer.

**Step 2: Strike / Undercoat (Optional but Recommended)**
- For difficult substrates (e.g., steel, zinc), a **thin copper strike** from a **cyanide copper bath** is applied first to ensure adhesion before switching to the acid bath.

**Step 3: Acid Copper Sulfate Plating**
- Substrate is immersed as **cathode** in the acid CuSO₄ bath.
- DC current applied at **2–4 A/dm²**, voltage **1.5–6 V**.
- Bath temperature: **25–50 °C**; mild air agitation is used.
- Duration depends on desired thickness.

**Step 4: Post-treatment**
- **Rinsing** → **Drying** → optional **passivation** or **lacquering** to prevent tarnishing.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Faraday's First Law** | $m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$ | $m$ = mass deposited (g), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $Z$ = electrochemical equivalent (g/C) | Calculate mass of Cu deposited |
| 2 | **Faraday's Second Law** | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | $E$ = equivalent weight (g/eq) | Compare deposition of different metals |
| 3 | **Electrochemical Equivalent (Z)** | $Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{E}{F}$ | $M$ = molar mass, $n$ = valency, $F$ = 96,485 C/mol | Find Z for Cu: $Z_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5 / (2 \times 96485) = 3.29 \times 10^{-4}$ g/C |
| 4 | **Mass Deposited (Copper)** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{\text{Cu}}}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot 63.5}{2 \times 96485}$ | All in SI units | Direct calculation for Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu |
| 5 | **Thickness of Deposit** | $t_{\text{depth}} = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$ | $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$ **g/cm³**, $A$ = area (cm²) | Convert mass to plating thickness |
| 6 | **Current Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in % | Real baths have $\eta$ ≈ 95–100% for acid Cu |
| 7 | **Nernst Equation (for Cu²⁺/Cu)** | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log[\text{Cu}^{2+}]$ | $E$ in volts at 25 °C | Predict cell voltage under non-standard conditions |
| 8 | **Current Density** | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | $A$ in dm² or m² | Operating parameter for bath control |
| 9 | **Plating Rate** | $R = \dfrac{J \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Thickness per unit time (μm/min) | Practical production rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numbers to Memorize for KTU:**
> - $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5$ **g/mol**
> - $n = 2$ (Cu²⁺)
> - $F = 96,485$ **C/mol** (use **96,500** in exams for cleaner numbers)
> - $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$ **g/cm³**
> - $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34$ **V**

## 2.4 Why the Acid Bath is Preferred over Cyanide Bath

| Property | Acid Copper Sulfate Bath | Cyanide Copper Bath |
|---|---|---|
| **Toxicity** | Low, eco-friendly | Highly toxic (HCN fumes) |
| **Throwing power** | Moderate (better with brighteners) | Excellent |
| **Current density** | High (up to 5 A/dm²) | Low (1–3 A/dm²) |
| **Deposition rate** | Fast | Slow |
| **Adhesion on steel** | Poor (needs strike) | Excellent (direct) |
| **Cost** | Low | High (waste treatment) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Reality:** Modern plants use a **dual-bath system**: cyanide strike → acid sulfate build-up. This combines the **adhesion of cyanide** with the **speed and brightness of acid bath**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **PCB (Printed Circuit Board) Manufacturing:** Copper plating through holes and surface traces.
- **Automotive Industry:** Decorative + corrosion-resistant coating on plastic parts (after electroless Cu).
- **EMI/RFI Shielding:** Copper-plated plastic enclosures for electronics.
- **Jewelry & Cutlery:** Decorative bright copper under-layer (often followed by nickel + chrome).
- **Electrical Connectors:** Improved conductivity and solderability.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Copper Deposited (Faraday's First Law Application)

**Problem Framework:** A current of **2.5 A** is passed through an acid copper sulfate bath for **1 hour 30 minutes**. Calculate the mass of copper deposited. Given: $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96,500$ C/mol.

**Step 1 — Identify the cathode reaction and number of electrons transferred.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cu}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cu}_{(s)}
\end{aligned}$$

Here, $n = 2$ electrons per Cu atom deposited.

**Step 2 — Recall Faraday's First Law.**

$$\begin{aligned}
m = Z \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{where} \quad Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and expand.**

$$\begin{aligned}
m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert time to seconds (SI unit).**

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= 1 \text{ h } 30 \text{ min} = 90 \text{ min} = 90 \times 60 \text{ s} = 5400 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{63.5 \times 2.5 \times 5400}{2 \times 96500}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate numerator.**

$$\begin{aligned}
63.5 \times 2.5 &= 158.75 \\
158.75 \times 5400 &= 857{,}250
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate denominator.**

$$\begin{aligned}
2 \times 96500 &= 193{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Final division.**

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{857{,}250}{193{,}000} = 4.442 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $m_{\text{Cu}} \approx \mathbf{4.44 \text{ g}}$ of copper is deposited.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thickness of Copper Layer on a Substrate

**Problem Framework:** The above 4.44 g of copper is deposited uniformly on a flat plate of area **250 cm²** on **one side**. Calculate the thickness of the copper layer. Given: $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Recall the geometric relation: Volume = Area × Thickness.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V = A \cdot d \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \dfrac{V}{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Volume from mass and density.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V = \dfrac{m}{\rho} = \dfrac{4.442 \text{ g}}{8.96 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 0.4958 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for thickness.**

$$\begin{aligned}
d = \dfrac{0.4958 \text{ cm}^3}{250 \text{ cm}^2} = 0.001983 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to micrometers (1 cm = 10,000 μm).**

$$\begin{aligned}
d = 0.001983 \times 10{,}000 = 19.83 \text{ μm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Thickness $\approx \mathbf{19.83 \ \mu m}$ (about **20 micrometers**).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time Required for a Given Thickness (Reverse Problem)

**Problem Framework:** A steel sheet of total area **400 cm²** is to be copper-plated to a thickness of **25 μm**. Using a current of **3.0 A** with **100% current efficiency**, calculate the time required. Given: $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$ g/cm³, $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5$ g/mol, $F = 96,500$ C/mol.

**Step 1 — Find the mass required.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= A \cdot d = 400 \text{ cm}^2 \times (25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}) = 400 \times 0.0025 = 1.0 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m = \rho \cdot V = 8.96 \times 1.0 = 8.96 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Use Faraday's Law in reverse.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{8.96 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5 \times 3.0}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator.**

$$\begin{aligned}
8.96 \times 2 = 17.92 \\
17.92 \times 96500 = 1{,}729{,}280
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate denominator.**

$$\begin{aligned}
63.5 \times 3.0 = 190.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final division.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{1{,}729{,}280}{190.5} = 9077.6 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to hours.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{9077.6}{3600} \approx 2.52 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Time required $\approx \mathbf{2.52 \text{ hours}}$ (or **2 h 31 min**).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Effect of Current Efficiency

**Problem Framework:** In the previous calculation, suppose the actual current efficiency of the bath is **95%** (some current is lost to side reactions like H₂ evolution). Calculate the new time required.

**Step 1 — Current efficiency relationship.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

Equivalently, the **effective current** for Cu deposition is:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{effective}} = \eta \cdot I = 0.95 \times 3.0 = 2.85 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Re-apply Faraday's Law with effective current.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I_{\text{effective}}} = \dfrac{8.96 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5 \times 2.85}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate denominator.**

$$\begin{aligned}
63.5 \times 2.85 = 180.975
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Final division.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{1{,}729{,}280}{180.975} = 9556.5 \text{ s} \approx 2.655 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** With 95% efficiency, time increases to $\approx \mathbf{2.66 \text{ hours}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 5% loss in efficiency leads to a **5% increase in time** (and energy cost). This is why industrial baths are carefully monitored with **Hull cells** and **ampere-hour meters**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Key Problem-Solving Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | To Find | Key Equation Chain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mass calculation | $I$, $t$ | $m$ | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ |
| Thickness | $m$, $A$, $\rho$ | $d$ | $d = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$ |
| Time for thickness | $d$, $A$, $I$, $\eta$ | $t$ | $t = \dfrac{\rho \cdot A \cdot d \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I \cdot \eta}$ |
| Current efficiency | $m_{\text{actual}}$, $m_{\text{theoretical}}$ | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theo}}} \times 100\%$ |
| Equivalent weight | Metal identity | $E$ | $E = M/n$ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define electroplating. List the essential components of an electroplating cell.**

**Model Answer:**
Electroplating is the process of depositing a thin, adherent layer of a metal onto a conductive substrate using an electric current passed through an electrolyte containing ions of the plating metal.

**Essential components of an electroplating cell:**
1. **Anode** — The metal to be deposited (pure copper in this case).
2. **Cathode** — The object to be plated (substrate).
3. **Electrolyte** — Aqueous solution of a copper salt (e.g., CuSO₄ in dilute H₂SO₄).
4. **DC Power Supply** — Provides the driving voltage (1.5–6 V for acid Cu bath).
5. **Connecting wires and tank** — Often with heating and agitation systems.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Write Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis. State its mathematical form and significance.**

**Model Answer:**
**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of a substance liberated or deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

**Mathematical form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m \propto Q \quad \text{or} \quad m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = Z \cdot Q
\end{aligned}$$

Where $m$ = mass deposited, $Z$ = electrochemical equivalent, $I$ = current, $t$ = time, $Q$ = charge.

**Significance in electroplating:** It allows the **calculation of plating thickness, time, and current requirements** in industrial processes, ensuring quality control and cost estimation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the **electroplating of copper** using the **acid copper sulfate bath**. Discuss the electrode reactions, bath composition, operating conditions, and the role of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A current of **5.0 A** is passed through an acid copper sulfate solution for **2 hours**. Calculate: **(i)** the mass of copper deposited, and **(ii)** the thickness of the deposit on a **flat plate of 500 cm² area (one side)**. Assume 100% current efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Bath Composition (Typical Industrial Acid Cu Bath):**

| Component | Concentration | Role |
|---|---|---|
| CuSO₄·5H₂O | 200–250 g/L | Source of Cu²⁺ ions |
| H₂SO₄ | 40–60 g/L | Improves conductivity, prevents Cu(OH)₂ precipitation |
| Chloride ions (Cl⁻) | 60–100 ppm | Aids anode dissolution, reduces anode passivation |
| Brighteners / Levelers | Trace (ppm) | Produce bright, level deposits |

**Electrode Reactions:**

At **Anode** (Oxidation, Cu dissolves):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cu (s)} \longrightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}
\end{aligned}$$

At **Cathode** (Reduction, Cu deposits):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cu}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cu (s)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Operating Conditions:**
- **Current density:** 2–4 A/dm²
- **Voltage:** 1.5–6 V DC
- **Temperature:** 25–50 °C
- **Agitation:** Mild air agitation
- **Anode:** Pure electrolytic copper (>99.9%)

**Pre-treatment Steps (must be completed before plating):**
1. Degreasing (alkaline cleaner)
2. Pickling (dilute H₂SO₄ to remove rust/scale)
3. Water rinsing
4. Optional **cyanide copper strike** for adhesion on steel

**Post-treatment:**
Rinsing → Drying → Optional anti-tarnish lacquer.

**Advantages of Acid Cu Bath:** High purity deposit, fast deposition, low toxicity, excellent for thick deposits (printed circuits, electroforming).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $I = 5.0$ A, $t = 2$ h = $2 \times 3600 = 7200$ s, $A = 500$ cm², $\eta = 100\%$, $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96,500$ C/mol, $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$ g/cm³.

**(i) Mass of Copper Deposited:**

Using Faraday's First Law:

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{63.5 \times 5.0 \times 7200}{2 \times 96500}
\end{aligned}$$

Numerator: $63.5 \times 5.0 = 317.5$; $317.5 \times 7200 = 2,286,000$
Denominator: $2 \times 96500 = 193,000$

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{2{,}286{,}000}{193{,}000} = 11.844 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

$\boxed{m_{\text{Cu}} \approx 11.84 \text{ g}}$

**(ii) Thickness of Deposit:**

Volume of copper:

$$\begin{aligned}
V = \dfrac{m}{\rho} = \dfrac{11.844}{8.96} = 1.3219 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

Thickness:

$$\begin{aligned}
d = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{1.3219}{500} = 0.002644 \text{ cm} = 26.44 \text{ μm}
\end{aligned}$$

$\boxed{d \approx 26.4 \ \mu m}$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** State and explain **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. How are they applied in calculating the **time required to deposit a 30 μm thick copper layer on a sheet of total area 800 cm²** using a current of 4.0 A and 95% current efficiency? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **industrial applications of copper electroplating**. Compare the **acid copper sulfate bath** with the **cyanide copper bath** in terms of bath composition, throwing power, and safety. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a) — 7 Marks

**Faraday's First Law:** Mass deposited ∝ charge passed.

$$\begin{aligned}
m = Z \cdot I \cdot t = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
\end{aligned}$$

**Faraday's Second Law:** When the same quantity of charge is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses deposited are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights.

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2} = \dfrac{M_1/n_1}{M_2/n_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Calculation:**

Given: $d = 30 \ \mu m = 30 \times 10^{-4}$ cm, $A = 800$ cm², $I = 4.0$ A, $\eta = 95\% = 0.95$.

**Step 1: Volume of Cu required.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V = A \cdot d = 800 \times 30 \times 10^{-4} = 2.4 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Mass required.**

$$\begin{aligned}
m = \rho \cdot V = 8.96 \times 2.4 = 21.504 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply Faraday's Law with efficiency.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I \cdot \eta} = \dfrac{21.504 \times 2 \times 96500}{63.5 \times 4.0 \times 0.95}
\end{aligned}$$

Numerator: $21.504 \times 2 = 43.008$; $43.008 \times 96500 = 4,150,272$
Denominator: $63.5 \times 4.0 \times 0.95 = 241.3$

$$\begin{aligned}
t = \dfrac{4{,}150{,}272}{241.3} = 17{,}200.6 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to hours:

$$\begin{aligned}
t = \dfrac{17{,}200.6}{3600} \approx 4.78 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

$\boxed{t \approx 4.78 \text{ hours (4 h 47 min)}}$

---

#### Model Solution for B(b) — 7 Marks

**Industrial Applications of Copper Electroplating:**
1. **Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs):** Through-hole plating and surface traces.
2. **Decorative coatings:** Under-layer for nickel + chromium on jewelry, cutlery.
3. **EMI/RFI Shielding:** Plastic enclosures for electronics.
4. **Automotive parts:** Trim, decorative items.
5. **Electroforming:** Manufacturing of precision metal parts and molds.
6. **Electrical industry:** Improving conductivity of aluminum busbars and connectors.
7. **Solderability enhancement:** On steel and brass components.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Acid Copper Sulfate Bath | Cyanide Copper Bath |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | CuSO₄ + H₂SO₄ + Cl⁻ | CuCN + NaCN + NaOH |
| **Toxicity** | Low (eco-friendly) | Very high (HCN fumes) |
| **Throwing power** | Moderate (improved with brighteners) | Excellent |
| **Adhesion on steel** | Poor — needs strike | Excellent — direct plating |
| **Current density** | High (2–4 A/dm²) | Low (1–3 A/dm²) |
| **Bath stability** | Stable | Complex, requires careful control |
| **Industrial use** | Heavy deposits, PCB | Strike + decorative |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the time conversion to seconds** (most common mistake — losing 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Using the wrong valency** of Cu (must be $n=2$, not 1).
> 3. **Ignoring current efficiency** when the problem states a value below 100%.
> 4. **Mixing up anode and cathode reactions** — always remember: **AN OX, CAT RED** (Anode Oxidation, Cathode Reduction).
> 5. **Not mentioning the need for pre-treatment** (degreasing, pickling) — examiners specifically look for this step.
> 6. **Confusing mass deposited with mass of anode dissolved** — both are equal *in theory*, but the **anode dissolves continuously** to replenish the bath.
> 7. **Using g/cm³ as density unit in SI calculations** — keep units consistent or convert thickness to cm before calculating volume.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Copper Electroplating**

- ☐ **Definition:** Electroplating = electrodeposition of a metal coating using DC current in an electrolyte.
- ☐ **Acid Cu Bath Composition:** CuSO₄·5H₂O (200–250 g/L) + H₂SO₄ (40–60 g/L) + Cl⁻ (60–100 ppm).
- ☐ **Anode:** Pure electrolytic copper (>99.9%); **Cathode:** Object to be plated.
- ☐ **Anode Reaction:** Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ (oxidation)
- ☐ **Cathode Reaction:** Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (reduction)
- ☐ **Standard Potential:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34$ V
- ☐ **Faraday's First Law:** $m = ZIt = \dfrac{MIt}{nF}$
- ☐ **Key Constants:** $M_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96,500$ C/mol, $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$ g/cm³.
- ☐ **Current Density:** 2–4 A/dm² for acid Cu bath.
- ☐ **Voltage Range:** 1.5–6 V DC.
- ☐ **Temperature:** 25–50 °C.
- ☐ **Pre-treatment:** Degreasing → Pickling → Rinsing → (Optional strike) → Plating.
- ☐ **Thickness Formula:** $d = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$ (always keep cm units for $d$ and $A$).
- ☐ **Current Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$
- ☐ **Adhesion Issue:** Acid bath does NOT adhere well directly on steel → use **cyanide strike** first.
- ☐ **Applications:** PCBs, decorative coatings, EMI shielding, electroforming, solderability.
- ☐ **Advantages of Acid Bath:** Fast, non-toxic, bright deposits, suitable for thick layers.
- ☐ **Disadvantage:** Poor throwing power without additives; requires strike on steel.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:** In Part B questions, **always draw a neat labeled diagram of the electroplating setup** (anode, cathode, electrolyte, power supply, ion flow arrows) — this often fetches **2–3 easy marks** even before you start calculations. Combine it with a **balanced chemical equation at each electrode** and a **flowchart of pre-treatment steps** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroless plating of copper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electroless plating of copper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electroless plating** is an *autocatalytic* (self-sustaining) chemical reduction process used to deposit a uniform, adherent metallic coating of copper on a solid substrate **without the application of an external electric current**. The deposition is driven entirely by a redox reaction in which a dissolved reducing agent (typically formaldehyde) supplies electrons to reduce metal ions (Cu²⁺) from the solution onto a catalytically active surface.

When the metal being deposited is **copper**, the process is specifically termed **Electroless Copper Plating (ELC)**, and it forms the technological backbone of modern **Printed Circuit Board (PCB) manufacturing** and semiconductor metallization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (GDCYT122 – Module 2):**
> Electroless plating of copper is studied under *surface engineering* and *thin-film chemistry*. Examiners frequently test the **anodic–cathodic half-reactions, bath composition, role of complexing agents, and the distinction from electroplating.**

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you want to "paint" a delicate, three-dimensional metal sculpture with copper — but you cannot reach inside its tiny crevices and holes with a brush, and you cannot connect wires to it. **Electroless plating solves this problem.**

- Think of the copper-plating bath as a **"chemical kitchen"** where copper "ingredients" (Cu²⁺ ions) are floating in a soup.
- A **"molecular chef"** — the reducing agent (formaldehyde) — is dissolved in the same soup. It voluntarily gives away electrons, converting Cu²⁺ ions into solid copper metal.
- These copper atoms then **rain down evenly** on every exposed surface of the submerged object, no matter how intricate the geometry. The process is *self-driving* — no external battery is needed, unlike electroplating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Electroplating:**
> - *Electroplating* → Uses an **external DC power source**; coating is uneven on irregular shapes.
> - *Electroless plating* → Uses **in-situ chemical reduction**; coating is **uniform on all surfaces** (including inside drilled holes, blind vias, and deep recesses).

## 1.3 Standard Process Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Operating Temperature | **40 – 60 °C** |
| Bath pH (strongly alkaline) | **11.5 – 13.0** |
| Deposition Rate | **2 – 8 µm / hour** |
| Standard Electrode Potential of Cu²⁺/Cu | **+ 0.34 V (vs. SHE)** |
| Common Reducing Agent | **Formaldehyde (HCHO, 37 % aq.)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry Behind Electroless Copper Deposition

Electroless copper deposition is governed by a **mixed-potential** model in which two simultaneous electrochemical half-reactions occur on the catalytically active substrate:

### (a) Cathodic Half-Reaction (Reduction of Cu²⁺)

The copper ions in solution gain electrons and deposit as solid copper on the substrate surface.

$$
Cu^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow Cu_{(s)}
$$

### (b) Anodic Half-Reaction (Oxidation of Formaldehyde)

In the strongly alkaline bath, formaldehyde (HCHO) is oxidized to formate ion (HCOO⁻), releasing two electrons per molecule.

$$
HCHO_{(aq)} + 3\,OH^{-}_{(aq)} \longrightarrow HCOO^{-}_{(aq)} + 2\,H_{2}O_{(l)} + 2e^{-}
$$

### (c) Overall Net Redox Reaction

Adding the two half-reactions and cancelling the electrons:

$$
Cu^{2+}_{(aq)} + HCHO_{(aq)} + 3\,OH^{-}_{(aq)} \longrightarrow Cu_{(s)} + HCOO^{-}_{(aq)} + 2\,H_{2}O_{(l)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrogen evolution as a competing reaction:**
> In real baths, hydrogen gas is also evolved:  $2H_{2}O + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_{2} + 2OH^{-}$
> This is why the net reaction is often written with H₂↑ in the products.

## 2.2 Role of Each Bath Constituent

| Constituent | Function | Common Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Copper Salt** | Source of Cu²⁺ ions | CuSO₄·5H₂O |
| **Reducing Agent** | Supplies electrons (anodic reaction) | Formaldehyde (HCHO) |
| **Complexing Agent** | Holds Cu²⁺ in solution at high pH; prevents Cu(OH)₂ precipitation | EDTA, Rochelle salt (KNaC₄H₄O₆), Quadrol |
| **Alkali** | Maintains alkaline pH; provides OH⁻ | NaOH / KOH |
| **Stabilizer** | Prevents spontaneous bath decomposition (homogeneous reduction) | 2,2′-dipyridyl, thiourea, MBT, KCN (trace) |
| **Surfactant / Wetter** | Reduces surface tension for uniform wetting | Polyethylene glycol (PEG) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is a complexing agent essential?**
