|------|----------------|--------------------|----------------|
| HCV  | Complete       | Condensed (liquid) | Sensible + Latent |
| LCV  | Complete       | Vapour (escaped)   | Only sensible   |

**Always remember:** LCV < HCV for the same fuel.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍕

Think of a **wood-fired pizza oven (LCV)** versus a **condensing boiler (HCV)**:

- A traditional wood oven releases hot exhaust gases carrying water vapor. The water vapor escapes, and with it goes the latent heat of condensation. This is **LCV** — what you practically feel as heat.
- A modern **condensing boiler** is engineered with a special heat exchanger that cools the flue gases below 100 °C, forcing the water vapor to **condense** and release its latent heat back into the heating loop. The extra recovered energy is exactly the difference between **HCV and LCV**.

So **HCV** is the *theoretical maximum*; **LCV** is the *practical reality* in most industrial furnaces, IC engines, and gas turbines.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Constants

The following constants are universally used in KTU board numericals:

- **Latent heat of vaporisation of water at 100 °C = 587 cal/g (≈ 2457 kJ/kg)**
- **Molar mass of water = 18 g/mol**, hydrogen = 2 g/mol, oxygen = 32 g/mol
- **1 g of H₂ (gas) on combustion produces 9 g of H₂O** (since 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O ⇒ 4 g H produces 36 g H₂O)
- **1 cal = 4.184 J** (thermochemical)
- **1 kcal/kg ≈ 1 BTU/lb**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### A. Why LCV is always smaller than HCV

The difference between HCV and LCV stems from a single source — the **latent heat carried away by water vapour** in the exhaust.

For every 1 g of hydrogen present in the fuel, **9 g of water** is generated on combustion.

$$\text{Mass of H}_2\text{O produced per gram of H} = \frac{18 \times 1}{2} = 9 \text{ g}$$

The latent heat lost from this 9 g of water = $9 \times 587 = 5283$ cal per gram of H.

If H is the **mass percentage of hydrogen** in the fuel, the latent heat lost per gram of fuel is:

$$\Delta H_{\text{latent}} = \frac{H}{100} \times 9 \times 587 \;\;\text{cal/g}$$

This leads to the master relationship:

$$\boxed{\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - \frac{9H}{100} \times 587 \;\;\text{cal/g}}$$

or equivalently:

$$\boxed{\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 0.09 \, H \times 587 \;\;\text{cal/g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In **SI units**, the same relation is written as:
> $$\text{LCV} \; (\text{kJ/kg}) = \text{HCV} \; (\text{kJ/kg}) - \frac{9H}{100} \times 2457$$

### B. Dulong's Formula — Theoretical HCV from Ultimate Analysis

When the elemental composition (C, H, O, S) of a solid fuel is known, the **Dulong's formula** predicts the HCV without performing any experiment:

$$\boxed{\text{HCV} = \frac{1}{100}\Big[8080\,C + 34500\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\Big] \;\;\text{cal/g}}$$

where C, H, O, S are **mass percentages** of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and sulphur respectively. The oxygen correction $(H - O/8)$ accounts for the fact that part of the hydrogen is already bound with oxygen (as in moisture or carbohydrates) and is therefore unavailable for heat release.

**Origin of constants in Dulong's formula:**

| Element | Heat released (cal/g) | Reasoning |
|---------|----------------------|-----------|
| C → CO₂ | **8080** | Standard enthalpy of combustion of carbon |
| H → H₂O (liquid) | **34 500** | Combustion of 1 g of H₂ yields 34 500 cal |
| S → SO₂ | **2240** | Combustion of 1 g of sulphur |
| O | (−1/8) factor | 1 g of O combines with 1/8 g of H₂, "locking" its heat |

## 2.2 Engineering Utility

| Field | Use of Calorific Value Data |
|-------|-----------------------------|
| Power Plants | Sizing coal-handling systems & boiler design |
| IC Engines | Octane/ Cetane rating, fuel-air mixture calculation |
| Domestic Fuels | LPG / PNG billing in BTU/therm |
| Metallurgy | Coke selection for blast furnaces |
| Aerospace | Specific energy of rocket propellants (kJ/kg) |
| Environmental | Estimating CO₂ per unit of energy produced |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------|------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | LCV from HCV | $\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 0.09\,H \times 587$ | cal/g | H = % H₂ in fuel |
| 2 | LCV in SI | $\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 0.09\,H \times 2457$ | kJ/kg | H = % H₂ in fuel |
| 3 | Dulong's HCV | $\text{HCV} = \frac{1}{100}[8080C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240S]$ | cal/g | From ultimate analysis |
| 4 | Dulong's LCV | Subtract 0.09 H × 587 from Dulong's HCV | cal/g | Two-step calculation |
| 5 | Bomb Calorimeter (HCV) | $\text{HCV} = \dfrac{(W + w) \cdot \Delta T}{m}$ | cal/g | W = water equivalent, w = water mass |
| 6 | Mass of H₂O per g H | $9 \text{ g}$ | — | Stoichiometric ratio |
| 7 | Calorie ↔ Joule | $1 \text{ cal} = 4.184 \text{ J}$ | — | Exact (thermochemical) |
| 8 | 1 kcal/kg | $\approx 1 \text{ BTU/lb}$ | — | Engineering conversion |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relation Between LCV and HCV

**Step 1: Write the balanced combustion reaction for hydrogen (the only element whose combustion produces water).**

$$2 H_2(g) + O_2(g) \longrightarrow 2 H_2O(l) \quad \Delta H = -136.8 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 2: Compute the heat released per gram of hydrogen.**

$$\text{Heat per g of H}_2 = \frac{136.8 \text{ kcal}}{4 \text{ g}} = 34.2 \text{ kcal/g} \approx 34500 \text{ cal/g}$$

**Step 3: Compute the mass of liquid water formed per gram of hydrogen.**

$$2 \text{ mol H}_2 \rightarrow 2 \text{ mol H}_2O \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 4 \text{ g H} \rightarrow 36 \text{ g H}_2O$$

$$\therefore \text{mass of H}_2O \text{ per g of H} = \frac{36}{4} = 9 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4: Compute the latent heat lost when 1 g of H is converted to water vapour instead of liquid.**

Per gram of fuel, if the H content is H% (by mass):

$$m_{\text{H}_2O} = \frac{9 H}{100} \text{ g per g of fuel}$$

$$q_{\text{latent lost}} = \frac{9 H}{100} \times 587 \text{ cal/g}$$

**Step 5: Equate the difference between HCV and LCV to this latent loss.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HCV} - \text{LCV} &= \frac{9 H}{100} \times 587 \\
\text{LCV} &= \text{HCV} - \frac{9 H}{100} \times 587 \\
\text{LCV} &= \text{HCV} - 0.09 \, H \times 587
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **master equation** every KTU examiner expects.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dulong's Formula (Logical Build-up)

**Step 1: Combustion of carbon to CO₂.**

$$C(s) + O_2(g) \longrightarrow CO_2(g) \quad \Delta H = -8080 \text{ cal/g of C}$$

Contribution to HCV = $8080 \, C / 100$ cal/g of fuel.

**Step 2: Combustion of hydrogen to liquid water.**

$$H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \longrightarrow H_2O(l) \quad \Delta H = -34500 \text{ cal/g of H}$$

Contribution to HCV = $34500 \, H / 100$ cal/g of fuel.

**Step 3: Correction for oxygen already present in the fuel.**

Oxygen in fuel exists as part of H₂O or as a bound oxide. **8 g of O combine with 1 g of H₂** to "lock" it. So the effective free hydrogen is:

$$H_{\text{eff}} = H - \frac{O}{8}$$

**Step 4: Combustion of sulphur to SO₂.**

$$S(s) + O_2(g) \longrightarrow SO_2(g) \quad \Delta H = -2240 \text{ cal/g of S}$$

Contribution to HCV = $2240 \, S / 100$ cal/g of fuel.

**Step 5: Sum all contributions.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HCV} &= \frac{1}{100}\Big[8080 \, C \;+\; 34500\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) \;+\; 2240 \, S\Big] \text{ cal/g} \\
\\
\text{LCV} &= \text{HCV} - 0.09 \, H \times 587 \text{ cal/g}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** Always express the final answer in **cal/g AND kcal/kg** (these are numerically identical). Add the unit **kJ/kg** by multiplying cal/g by 4.184 for full credit.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Working Equation of the Bomb Calorimeter

In a bomb calorimeter experiment, the heat released by the fuel sample raises the temperature of:
1. The water surrounding the bomb (mass = $w$ grams)
2. The bomb, stirrer, thermometer & accessories — lumped together as **water equivalent $W$** (grams)

Heat balance:

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = Q_{\text{water+accessories}}$$

$$m \cdot \text{HCV} = (W + w) \cdot \Delta T$$

Solving for HCV:

$$\boxed{\text{HCV} = \frac{(W + w) \cdot \Delta T}{m} \;\;\text{cal/g}}$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of fuel sample (g)
- $w$ = mass of water in the calorimeter (g)
- $W$ = water equivalent of bomb + stirrer + thermometer (g)
- $\Delta T = T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}}$ (°C or K)

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical (KTU-Style, 7 Mark)

**Problem:** A coal sample contains C = 80 %, H = 6 %, O = 10 %, S = 2 %, N = 2 %. Calculate its HCV and LCV using Dulong's formula. Also express LCV in kJ/kg.

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**

$$C = 80,\;\; H = 6,\;\; O = 10,\;\; S = 2,\;\; N = 2$$

**Step 2: Apply Dulong's formula for HCV.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HCV} &= \frac{1}{100}\Big[8080(80) + 34500\!\left(6 - \frac{10}{8}\right) + 2240(2)\Big] \\
&= \frac{1}{100}\Big[646400 + 34500 \times 4.75 + 4480\Big] \\
&= \frac{1}{100}\Big[646400 + 163875 + 4480\Big] \\
&= \frac{814755}{100} \\
&= 8147.55 \text{ cal/g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apply the LCV–HCV relation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LCV} &= \text{HCV} - 0.09 \times H \times 587 \\
&= 8147.55 - 0.09 \times 6 \times 587 \\
&= 8147.55 - 0.54 \times 587 \\
&= 8147.55 - 316.98 \\
&= 7830.57 \text{ cal/g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Convert LCV to kJ/kg.**

$$\text{LCV} = 7830.57 \times 4.184 = 32763.10 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Final Result (Box-Answer Format for KTU scripts):**

$$\text{HCV} = 8147.55 \text{ cal/g} = 34080.18 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\text{LCV} = 7830.57 \text{ cal/g} = 32763.10 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bomb Calorimeter — Labelled Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oxygen Cylinder 25 to 30 atm] --> B[Steel Bomb Crucible Assembly]
    C[Fuel Pellet 0.9 to 1.1 g with ignition cotton] --> B
    B --> D[Water Jacket 1500 to 2000 g water]
    D --> E[Stirrer Mechanism]
    D --> F[Precision Beckmann Thermometer]
    D --> G[Ignition Electrode Leads]
    G --> H[External 6 to 12 V DC Supply]
    H -- Electric Spark --> B
    B -- Combustion Heat --> D
    D -- Temperature Rise deltaT --> F
    F --> I[Readings every 30 s for 25 min]
    I --> J[Plot Temperature versus Time]
    J --> K[Apply Regnault Pfaundler Correction for Radiation]
    K --> L[Compute HCV using m x HCV equals W plus w x deltaT]
```

**Component-by-Component Function Table**

| Node | Component | Functional Role |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| A | Oxygen Cylinder | Supplies O₂ at 25–30 atm pressure for complete combustion |
| B | Steel Bomb + Crucible | Sealed vessel where combustion occurs at constant volume |
| C | Fuel Pellet | Sample (≈ 1 g) pelletised with benzoic acid / ignition cotton |
| D | Water Jacket | Absorbs liberated heat (calorimetric medium) |
| E | Stirrer | Maintains uniform temperature inside the jacket |
| F | Beckmann Thermometer | Measures ΔT with 0.01 °C resolution |
| G | Electrode Leads | Carry DC current into the bomb |
| H | DC Supply | Provides the spark that ignites the cotton thread |
| I–J | Time–Temp Data | Used to plot heating and cooling curves |
| K | Pfaundler Correction | Corrects ΔT for heat lost to surroundings during the run |
| L | Final Calculation | $\text{HCV} = (W+w)\Delta T / m$ |

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Calorific Value Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Solid Fuel Sample] --> S2[Ultimate Analysis CHNS and O]
    S1 --> S3[Bomb Calorimetry Experiment]
    S2 --> S4{Choose Method}
    S3 --> S4
    S4 -- Experimental --> S5[Apply m x HCV equals W plus w x deltaT]
    S4 -- Theoretical --> S6[Apply Dulong Formula]
    S5 --> S7[HCV measured]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Compute LCV using LCV equals HCV minus 0.09 H times 587]
    S8 --> S9[Compare HCV and LCV]
    S9 --> S10[Decide Fuel Quality for Industrial Use]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture Insight:** Notice that the experimental path (Bomb Calorimeter) and theoretical path (Dulong's Formula) both **converge into the same HCV value**, after which a single conversion step yields LCV. This dual-pathway approach is a hallmark of KTU lab-practical + theory integration.

## 4.3 HCV vs LCV — Energy Distribution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    E[Total Heat of Combustion equals HCV] --> P1[Sensible Heat of Gases]
    E --> P2[Latent Heat of Condensing Water]
    P1 --> F[Heat Actually Recovered equals LCV]
    P2 --> L[Heat Lost in Flue Gases]
    E --> F
    E --> L
```

**Interpretation:** The **HCV** is the *total heat blob*; it is split into (a) the **sensible heat** of dry exhaust gases (which is recovered) and (b) the **latent heat** of water vapor (which is lost unless condensation is engineered in). The sum of (a) is the **LCV**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the terms (i) Calorific Value, (ii) Higher Calorific Value (HCV), and (iii) Lower Calorific Value (LCV).**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Calorific Value:** It is the amount of heat energy liberated (in cal/g, kcal/kg, or kJ/kg) when **one gram (or one kg) of a fuel is completely burnt** in excess of oxygen (or air) under standard conditions.

(ii) **Higher Calorific Value (HCV) / Gross Calorific Value (GCV):** It is the **total heat released** when unit mass of fuel is completely combusted, and the combustion products are cooled back to the original temperature (25 °C), with the **water vapour formed condensed to liquid**. Hence, the latent heat of condensation of water is **recovered** here.

(iii) **Lower Calorific Value (LCV) / Net Calorific Value (NCV):** It is the **net usable heat** released when unit mass of fuel is completely combusted, and the products escape as hot gases (water remaining as vapour). Here, the latent heat of condensation is **NOT recovered**.

> **HCV > LCV** for the same fuel. Numerical difference = $(0.09 \times H \times 587)$ cal/g.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between HCV and LCV with the help of a numerical example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | HCV (Gross) | LCV (Net) |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| Combustion products | Cooled to 25 °C | Allowed to escape at 100 °C+ |
| Water state in products | **Liquid** | **Vapour** |
| Latent heat recovered | Yes (counted) | No (lost) |
| Numerical value | Higher | Lower (by $0.09 H \times 587$) |
| Used in | Condensing boilers, theoretical calcs | IC engines, industrial furnaces |

**Numerical Illustration:** A coal with HCV = 8200 cal/g and H = 5 % has

$$\text{LCV} = 8200 - 0.09 \times 5 \times 587 = 8200 - 264.15 = 7935.85 \text{ cal/g}$$

Thus, HCV is about 264 cal/g higher than LCV for this particular fuel.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (Choice A or B, 14 Marks Each)

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **experimental determination of calorific value of a solid fuel** using a **Bomb Calorimeter** with a neat labelled diagram. Mention the **Regnault–Pfaundler correction** for heat loss to surroundings. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis: C = 72 %, H = 5 %, O = 14 %, S = 1.5 %, N = 7.5 %. Calculate the **HCV and LCV** using Dulong's formula. Express LCV in kJ/kg. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 🟦 Model Solution — Q A(a)

**1. Principle:**
A known mass of fuel is burnt completely in excess O₂ inside a sealed steel **bomb** at constant volume. The heat released is absorbed by a known mass of water surrounding the bomb. The temperature rise (ΔT) of water is measured with a **Beckmann thermometer**, and HCV is calculated from the heat balance:

$$\text{HCV} = \frac{(W + w) \cdot \Delta T}{m} \;\;\text{cal/g}$$

**2. Construction (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):**
- **Bomb**: Heavy-walled stainless steel crucible (≈ 300 mL), leak-proof, fitted with oxygen inlet valve and two electrodes.
- **Crucible**: A small silica/quartz cup holding the fuel pellet.
- **Water Jacket**: Surrounds the bomb; filled with a known mass ($w$, typically 1500–2000 g) of water.
- **Stirrer**: Ensures uniform water temperature.
- **Thermometer**: Beckmann thermometer (resolution 0.01 °C) or digital PT-100 probe.
- **Ignition Circuit**: 6–12 V DC supply connected across the electrodes with a thin iron/constantan ignition wire dipped in the fuel.

**3. Procedure:**
1. Weigh accurately **≈ 1 g** of fuel pellet (record mass $m$).
2. Place the pellet in the crucible. Attach the ignition cotton thread to the electrodes.
3. Close the bomb and fill it with **oxygen at 25–30 atm** pressure.
4. Immerse the bomb in the water jacket (containing known $w$ g of water).
5. Set up the stirrer and Beckmann thermometer; record the **initial temperature $T_i$** (after the "preliminary period" of ~5 min).
6. Close the ignition circuit → spark ignites the cotton → fuel burns.
7. Record temperature **every 30 s** for ~25 min (heating + cooling periods).
8. Plot the temperature–time graph and obtain the **corrected ΔT** using the **Regnault–Pfaundler method**.

**4. Regnault–Pfaundler Correction (Simplified):**
Because heat leaks to surroundings during the experiment, the observed ΔT is slightly inaccurate. The corrected rise is:

$$\Delta T_{\text{corr}} = \Delta T_{\text{obs}} + C$$

where $C$ is the cooling correction computed by extrapolating the pre- and post-combustion temperature slopes to the **midpoint of the combustion period**. The standard Pfaundler formula is:

$$C = \frac{m_1 + m_2}{2} \times \Delta t + \frac{m_2 - m_1}{2} \times (T_{\text{mid}} - T_{\text{room}})$$

Here, $m_1$ and $m_2$ are the rates of temperature change per minute before and after combustion; $\Delta t$ is the time in minutes between $m_1$ and $m_2$; $T_{\text{mid}}$ is the mean of max and min temperatures, and $T_{\text{room}}$ is ambient.

**5. Calculation:**
After correction, the **HCV is computed** as given above. The experiment yields **HCV only**; **LCV must be derived** from HCV using the hydrogen content (Dulong's formula or standard correction).

> **Standard lab values of W (water equivalent of bomb)** are usually **450 – 650 g**, calibrated beforehand using **benzoic acid (HCV = 6324 cal/g)** as a standard.

---

### 🟦 Model Solution — Q A(b)

**Given:** C = 72 %, H = 5 %, O = 14 %, S = 1.5 %, N = 7.5 %.

**Step 1: Dulong's Formula for HCV.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HCV} &= \frac{1}{100}\Big[8080(72) + 34500\!\left(5 - \frac{14}{8}\right) + 2240(1.5)\Big] \\
&= \frac{1}{100}\Big[581760 + 34500(5 - 1.75) + 3360\Big] \\
&= \frac{1}{100}\Big[581760 + 34500 \times 3.25 + 3360\Big] \\
&= \frac{1}{100}\Big[581760 + 112125 + 3360\Big] \\
&= \frac{1}{100}\Big[697245\Big] \\
&= 6972.45 \text{ cal/g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: LCV from HCV.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LCV} &= 6972.45 - 0.09 \times 5 \times 587 \\
&= 6972.45 - 0.45 \times 587 \\
&= 6972.45 - 264.15 \\
&= 6708.30 \text{ cal/g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Convert LCV to kJ/kg.**

$$\text{LCV} = 6708.30 \times 4.184 = 28067.53 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

### ✅ **Final Boxed Answer — Q A(b)**

$$\boxed{\text{HCV} = 6972.45 \text{ cal/g} = 29172.49 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{LCV} = 6708.30 \text{ cal/g} = 28067.53 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Derive the **relationship between HCV and LCV** of a fuel. Mention its significance in engineering applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a Bomb Calorimeter experiment, **0.95 g** of a coal sample caused a **temperature rise of 2.8 °C**. The water equivalent of the calorimeter is **550 g** and the calorimeter contains **1200 g of water**. The coal contains **5.4 % hydrogen** and **0.8 % sulphur**. Calculate the **HCV and LCV** of the coal. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 🟦 Model Solution — Q B(a)

**Derivation:**

**Step 1: Hydrogen combustion stoichiometry.**

$$2 H_2 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2 H_2O(l) \quad \Delta H = -136.8 \text{ kcal}$$

So, 4 g H₂ → 36 g H₂O(l), i.e., **1 g of hydrogen** produces **9 g of liquid water**.

**Step 2: Heat released per gram of H (condensed to liquid).**

$$q_H = \frac{136800 \text{ cal}}{4 \text{ g}} = 34200 \text{ cal/g} \approx 34500 \text{ cal/g}$$

**Step 3: Difference (HCV − LCV) arises ONLY from latent heat of the water that does NOT condense in the LCV scenario.**

If a fuel contains H% of hydrogen by mass, then per gram of fuel, water produced = $9H/100$ g.

Latent heat lost per gram of fuel = $(9H/100) \times L_v$, where $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporisation of water = **587 cal/g**.

**Step 4: Final derived relation.**

$$\boxed{\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - \frac{9H}{100} \times 587 \;\;\text{cal/g}}$$

**Engineering Significance:**

- **IC Engine Design:** Engines exhaust hot gases; hence engineers use **LCV** for thermal efficiency calculations.
- **Boiler Design (Non-Condensing):** Industrial boilers typically lose latent heat → use **LCV**.
- **Condensing Boilers (Domestic Heating):** Use **HCV** for higher efficiency (up to 98 % vs. 80 %).
- **Fuel Cost Estimation:** Natural gas is sold on **HCV** basis in many countries, but air-conditioning and gas turbine design use **LCV**.
- **Rocket Propellants:** The **specific impulse** uses LCV (since exhaust gases are hot vapour).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B(a):** Students often forget to write the **9 H/100 factor** derivation. They directly jump to the formula and lose 2–3 marks. Always show: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O stoichiometry first!

---

### 🟦 Model Solution — Q B(b)

**Given:** $m = 0.95$ g, $\Delta T = 2.8$ °C, $W = 550$ g, $w = 1200$ g, H = 5.4 %, S = 0.8 %.

**Step 1: Apply the Bomb Calorimeter equation.**

$$\text{HCV} = \frac{(W + w) \cdot \Delta T}{m}$$

$$\text{HCV} = \frac{(550 + 1200) \times 2.8}{0.95} = \frac{1750 \times 2.8}{0.95} = \frac{4900}{0.95} = 5157.89 \text{ cal/g}$$

**Step 2: Correct for sulphur → SO₃ (acid correction).**
Many KTU problems include this. The acid (H₂SO₄ from SO₂ → SO₃) formation releases additional heat that is wrongly absorbed in the bomb. The standard correction is:

$$\text{Correction} = \frac{22.5 \times S}{m} \text{ cal/g} \quad \text{(or 0.13 \% × 2200 cal/g for S) — typically subtracted.}$$

If asked to apply: $\Delta_{\text{acid}} = (0.8 / 100) \times 22.5 / 0.95 = 0.19$ cal/g. Often **negligible** in 7-mark problems; if not explicitly demanded, **skip it**.

**Step 3: Compute LCV.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LCV} &= \text{HCV} - 0.09 \times H \times 587 \\
&= 5157.89 - 0.09 \times 5.4 \times 587 \\
&= 5157.89 - 0.486 \times 587 \\
&= 5157.89 - 285.28 \\
&= 4872.61 \text{ cal/g}
\end{aligned}$$

### ✅ **Final Boxed Answer — Q B(b)**

$$\boxed{\text{HCV} = 5157.89 \text{ cal/g} = 21580.66 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{LCV} = 4872.61 \text{ cal/g} = 20386.99 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B(b):** The most common mistake is **forgetting to add $W + w$** in the numerator. Some students use only the water mass (1200 g) and miss the water equivalent (550 g), losing **3 marks instantly**. Also remember to **convert cal/g → kcal/kg → kJ/kg** for full credit; showing only cal/g is often worth partial credit only.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your last-day revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Calorific value = heat released per unit mass (or volume) of fuel on complete combustion** (units: cal/g, kcal/kg, kJ/kg, BTU/lb).
- ✅ **HCV / GCV:** Water from combustion is condensed → latent heat **recovered**.
- ✅ **LCV / NCV:** Water leaves as vapour → latent heat **lost** in exhaust.
- ✅ **Master relation:** $\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 0.09 \times H \times 587$ (cal/g). Always.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric magic number:** **1 g of H → 9 g of H₂O.** Memorise.
- ✅ **Latent heat of vaporisation of water** at 100 °C = **587 cal/g** (or 2457 kJ/kg). Memorise.
- ✅ **Dulong's formula:** $\text{HCV} = \frac{1}{100}[8080C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240S]$ cal/g.
- ✅ **Dulong's constant 8080** is for carbon; **34 500** for H₂; **2240** for S; **O/8** is the oxygen correction.
- ✅ **Bomb calorimeter is for SOLID & liquid fuels**; **Boy's calorimeter is for GASEOUS fuels** (rarely asked).
- ✅ **Bomb calorimeter measures HCV only**; LCV must be computed from HCV using the formula.
- ✅ **Bomb calorimeter formula:** $\text{HCV} = \dfrac{(W + w) \cdot \Delta T}{m}$. **Always add $W + w$** in the numerator.
- ✅ **Regnault–Pfaundler correction** accounts for heat leakage to surroundings during the experiment.
- ✅ **Water equivalent (W) of bomb** is calibrated using **benzoic acid** (HCV = 6324 cal/g).
- ✅ **Sulphur correction:** Acid formation in bomb gives an extra heat → must subtract ≈ $22.5 \times S$ cal/g (in advanced problems).
- ✅ **Conversion:** $1 \text{ cal/g} = 1 \text{ kcal/kg} = 4.184 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 1.8 \text{ BTU/lb}$.
- ✅ **Industrial relevance:** IC engines and gas turbines use **LCV**; condensing boilers and rocket propulsion use **HCV**.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** Combustion is exothermic (heat released), so $\Delta H$ is negative; magnitude is what we call calorific value.
- ✅ **Common Examiner Traps:** (1) Confusing HCV with LCV, (2) using 1 g of H → 18 g of H₂O (WRONG, it's 9 g), (3) forgetting O/8 correction in Dulong's, (4) not converting units at the end.
- ✅ **Bonus fact for viva:** The **highest HCV of a common fuel is H₂ (~34 500 cal/g)**, followed by methane (~13 160 cal/g) and gasoline (~11 000 cal/g).
- ✅ **Memory trick:** **HCV = 1**, **LCV = L** (Lower — last to recover, less heat). Mnemonic: *"**H**eavy **C**arries **V**apour, **L**ight **C**arries **V**apour — but **H**eavy keeps all heat!"*

---

> 🟢 **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Notes — Module 3 — Topic: Calorific Value (HCV/LCV) of Solid Fuels**
> **All KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (COs) and Bloom's Taxonomy levels (L1 Remember → L4 Analyse → L5 Evaluate) have been addressed.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biofuels: Biodiesel and Green Hydrogen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Biofuels: Biodiesel and Green Hydrogen # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Biofuels? A **biofuel** is a renewable fuel produced from biological feedstocks (plant biomass, animal fats, algae, or organic waste) that can replace or supplement conventional fossil fuels (petrol, diesel, natural gas). They are **carbon-neutral in net life-cycle analysis** because the CO₂ released on combustion is reabsorbed by the next crop cycle through photosynthesis. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** *"Biofuels are liquid, solid or gaseous fuels derived from recently dead biological material (biomass), distinguishable from fossil fuels which are derived from long-dead biomass."* ### Classification of Biofuels (Generations) | Generation | Feedstock | Examples | Land-Use Concern | |------------|-----------|----------|------------------| | 1st Gen | Sugar, starch, vegetable oils, animal fats | Bioethanol, Biodiesel | High (food vs fuel) | | 2nd Gen | Lignocellulosic biomass, waste | Cellulosic ethanol | Medium | | 3rd Gen | Algae | Algal biodiesel, bio-H₂ | Low | | 4th Gen | Engineered organisms | Synthetic biology fuels | Very Low | --- ## 1.2 Biodiesel — The Diesel Substitute **Biodiesel** is defined as the **mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids** derived from vegetable oils, animal fats, or microbial lipids. The standard industrial product is **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)**, produced via the **transesterification** of triglycerides with methanol in the presence of a base catalyst (NaOH or KOH). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Global Standard (EN 14214 / ASTM D6751):** Biodiesel is officially called **B100** (100% FAME). When blended with petro-diesel, it is denoted **B20** (20% biodiesel, 80% diesel), **B5**, etc. **Intuitive Analogy — "The Molecular Tailor":** Think of a triglyceride (the bulky oil molecule) as an overstuffed three-seater sofa — too big to fit through a narrow doorway (the engine's combustion chamber). Transesterification acts like a master carpenter who disassembles the sofa into three identical single chairs (FAME molecules) plus a small wooden frame (glycerol). These smaller chairs now fit easily through the door, burn cleanly, and leave the leftover frame as a valuable by-product (used in soaps, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals). --- ## 1.3 Green Hydrogen — The Fuel of the Future **Green Hydrogen** is hydrogen gas (H₂) produced exclusively by **electrolysis of water powered by renewable electricity** (solar, wind, hydro, geothermal). It emits **zero CO₂ at the point of use** — its only by-product on combustion (or in fuel cells) is **pure water**. The "color" classification of hydrogen (a KTU-favorite exam topic): | Color | Production Route | CO₂ Footprint | |-------|------------------|---------------| | **Grey H₂** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | ~9–10 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | | **Blue H₂** | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) | ~1–3 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | | **Green H₂** | Electrolysis powered by renewables | **0 kg CO₂/kg H₂** | | **Pink H₂** | Electrolysis powered by nuclear | ~0 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Only hydrogen produced via renewable-powered electrolysis qualifies as "**Green Hydrogen**" under the National Green Hydrogen Mission of India (launched 2023, target 5 MMT production by 2030). **Intuitive Analogy — "The Battery in Gaseous Form":** Green hydrogen is to renewable electricity what a rechargeable battery is to solar panels. Solar and wind power are **intermittent** (the sun sets, the wind stops). You cannot store MW of electricity cheaply in a wire, but you can use that surplus electricity to split water, store the resulting H₂ in tanks or underground caverns, and then release that energy later via a fuel cell or turbine. It is a **molecular time-shift of energy**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Biodiesel — Transesterification Mechanism ### 2.1.1 The Governing Reaction The overall reversible reaction: $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{catalyst}]{\text{NaOH/KOH}} 3\,\text{R-COO-CH}_3 + \text{Glycerol}$$ In chemical shorthand: $$\text{TG} + 3\,\text{MeOH} \rightarrow 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{GLY}$$ This reaction proceeds in **three sequential, reversible steps**, each producing a diglyceride, then monoglyceride, then glycerol, with one FAME released at each step. ### 2.1.2 Why Methanol? * **Short-chain alcohol** → easy separation of glycerol (immiscible, denser phase settles down). * **Low cost** and **easily recoverable** (BP = 64.7 °C). * **Forms smaller, less viscous ester** molecules → better cold-flow properties. > [!TIP] > **Why 3:1 molar ratio in practice?** Although the stoichiometric methanol-to-oil ratio is **3:1**, an excess of **6:1 to 12:1** is used industrially to drive the equilibrium forward and compensate for losses during recovery. ### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Why Base Catalysis Works 1. **OH⁻ (from NaOH)** deprotonates methanol → forms **methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻)**. 2. The methoxide attacks the **carbonyl carbon (C=O)** of the triglyceride — nucleophilic addition. 3. A **tetrahedral intermediate** forms. 4. The alkoxide (–O–) leaves, breaking the C–O bond, releasing **diglyceride + FAME**. 5. Steps 1–4 repeat twice more → final products **3 FAME + glycerol**. ### 2.1.4 Critical Process Parameters | Parameter | Optimal Range | Effect of Deviation | |-----------|---------------|---------------------| | **Methanol : Oil molar ratio** | 6:1 to 12:1 | <6:1 → incomplete conversion; >12:1 → glycerol recovery hard | | **Catalyst (NaOH) loading** | 0.5 – 1.5 wt% of oil | Excess → saponification (soap formation) | | **Reaction Temperature** | 55 – 65 °C | >65 °C → methanol boils (BP 64.7 °C), reaction stops | | **Reaction Time** | 30 – 90 min | Insufficient → low yield | | **Water content of oil** | < 0.06 wt% | Water → soap formation (saponification side reaction) | | **Free Fatty Acid (FFA) value** | < 2 mg KOH/g | High FFA → acid-catalysed pre-esterification needed | ### 2.1.5 By-product: Glycerol ~10 wt% of input oil becomes **crude glycerol (80% purity)**. After refining to **pharmaceutical grade (99.7%)**, it is used in cosmetics, food, explosives (nitroglycerin), and as a feedstock for 1,3-propanediol. --- ## 2.2 Green Hydrogen — Electrolysis Chemistry ### 2.2.1 The Governing Reaction (Overall) $$2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \rightarrow 2\,\text{H}_{2(g)} + \text{O}_{2(g)} \quad \Delta H^\circ = +571.6\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ In two half-reactions: **Cathode (Reduction):** $$4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4\,e^- \rightarrow 2\,\text{H}_2 + 4\,\text{OH}^-$$ **Anode (Oxidation):** $$2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\,\text{H}^+ + 4\,e^-$$ ### 2.2.2 Electrolyzer Technologies | Technology | Electrolyte | Operating T | Efficiency | Cost (USD/kW) | |------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|---------------| | **Alkaline (ALK)** | KOH (25–30%) | 60–80 °C | 60–70% | 500–1000 | | **PEM** | Polymer membrane | 50–80 °C | 70–85% | 1100–1800 | | **Solid Oxide (SOEC)** | YSZ ceramic | 700–850 °C | 75–85% | 2000–3000 | > [!NOTE] > **Thermodynamic minimum voltage** to split water = **1.23 V**. In practice, commercial electrolyzers operate at **1.8 – 2.2 V** per cell due to overpotentials (kinetic + ohmic + concentration). ### 2.2.3 Energy Content of Green Hydrogen * **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** = 142 MJ/kg * **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** = **120 MJ/kg** (used in fuel cell industry) * **Energy required to produce 1 kg H₂** ≈ **50 – 55 kWh** of electricity * **Density** (at STP) = 0.0899 kg/m³ → must be **compressed (350–700 bar)** or **liquefied (–253 °C)** for storage. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Key Constants & Units | |---------|---------|------------------------| | Stoichiometric Molar Ratio | $n_{\text{MeOH}} : n_{\text{oil}} = 3 : 1$ | Excess used: 6:1 – 12:1 | | Biodiesel Yield | $\%\,\text{Yield} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100$ | Ideal ≈ 100% if pure TG | | Theoretical Glycerol Mass | $m_{\text{GLY}} = \dfrac{92 \times m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{oil}}}$ | 92 = MW glycerol, $M_{\text{oil}}$ ≈ 884 g/mol (for soy) | | Calorific Value (Biodiesel) | $Q_{\text{biodiesel}} \approx 37.2\ \text{MJ/L}$ | Petro-diesel ≈ 35.7 MJ/L | | Density of Biodiesel | $\rho_{\text{B100}} \approx 0.88\ \text{g/cm}^3$ | Lower than petro-diesel (0.85) | | Cetane Number (CN) | $\text{CN}_{\text{biodiesel}} = 45 - 70$ | Higher than petro-diesel (~50) → better ignition | | Faraday's Law of Electrolysis | $m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$, $n = 2$ for H₂ | | Volume of H₂ (STP) | $V_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot 22.4}{2 \times 96485}\ \text{L}$ | At $0\ ^\circ\text{C}$, 1 atm | | Energy in H₂ | $E_{\text{H}_2} = m_{\text{H}_2} \times 120\ \text{MJ/kg}$ | LHV basis | | Electrolyzer Energy Input | $E_{\text{input}} = V_{\text{cell}} \times I \times t$ | $V_{\text{cell}} \approx 1.8 - 2.2\ \text{V}$ | | Thermoneutral Voltage | $V_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{n \cdot F} = 1.48\ \text{V}$ | 1.23 V is reversible voltage | | Efficiency of Electrolyzer | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{LHV of H}_2\ \text{produced}}{\text{Electrical input}}$ | Typical 60 – 75% | > [!IMPORTANT] > For any exam problem: $\text{mass of 1 mol H}_2 = 2\ \text{g}$; $\text{mass of 1 mol H}_2\text{O} = 18\ \text{g}$; **22.4 L of any ideal gas at STP (273 K, 1 atm)**. For hydrogen at NTP (25 °C, 1 atm) use **24.5 L/mol**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Problem Solving ## 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation: Biodiesel Yield from a Given Oil Mass > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** 1000 kg of soybean oil (average molecular weight 884 g/mol, 100% triglyceride) is reacted with excess methanol and 1% NaOH catalyst. Assuming 98% conversion, calculate: (a) mass of FAME produced, (b) mass of glycerol by-product, (c) volume of methanol consumed (density 0.792 g/mL). ### Step 1: Moles of Triglyceride in the Feed $$n_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{oil}}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 10^3\ \text{g}}{884\ \text{g/mol}}$$ $$n_{\text{oil}} = 1131.22\ \text{mol}$$ **Logic:** Dividing total mass by molecular weight gives the number of triglyceride molecules, each containing 3 fatty acid chains. ### Step 2: Theoretical Moles of FAME Produced From stoichiometry (1 mol TG → 3 mol FAME): $$n_{\text{FAME, theo}} = 3 \times n_{\text{oil}} = 3 \times 1131.22 = 3393.66\ \text{mol}$$ ### Step 3: Actual Moles of FAME (98% Conversion) $$n_{\text{FAME, act}} = 0.98 \times 3393.66 = 3325.79\ \text{mol}$$ ### Step 4: Mass of FAME Produced Average molecular weight of soy FAME ≈ 292.4 g/mol. $$m_{\text{FAME}} = n_{\text{FAME, act}} \times M_{\text{FAME}}$$ $$m_{\text{FAME}} = 3325.79 \times 292.4 = 9.724 \times 10^5\ \text{g} \approx 972.4\ \text{kg}$$ **Logic check:** Yield on mass basis = $\dfrac{972.4}{1000} \times 100 = 97.24\%$, consistent with the 98% molar conversion (small difference due to leaving group mass). ### Step 5: Mass of Glycerol Produced From stoichiometry (1 mol TG → 1 mol glycerol, MW = 92.09 g/mol): $$n_{\text{GLY, theo}} = n_{\text{oil}} = 1131.22\ \text{mol}$$ $$n_{\text{GLY, act}} = 0.98 \times 1131.22 = 1108.59\ \text{mol}$$ $$m_{\text{GLY}} = 1108.59 \times 92.09 = 1.021 \times 10^5\ \text{g} \approx 102.1\ \text{kg}$$ ### Step 6: Volume of Methanol Consumed Stoichiometric methanol needed: $$n_{\text{MeOH}} = 3 \times n_{\text{oil}} = 3 \times 1131.22 = 3393.66\ \text{mol}$$ Mass of methanol: $$m_{\text{MeOH}} = 3393.66 \times 32.04 = 1.087 \times 10^5\ \text{g} = 108.7\ \text{kg}$$ Volume: $$V_{\text{MeOH}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{MeOH}}}{\rho_{\text{MeOH}}} = \dfrac{108.7 \times 10^3\ \text{g}}{0.792\ \text{g/mL}}$$ $$V_{\text{MeOH}} = 1.373 \times 10^5\ \text{mL} = 137.3\ \text{L}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Excess Methanol:** If a 6:1 molar ratio is used (typical industrial), the actual methanol charged is 6 × 1131.22 = 6787.32 mol ≈ 274.6 L. The unreacted methanol is recovered and recycled (~70–80% recovery is common). --- ## 3.2 Worked-Out Derivation: Green Hydrogen Production via Electrolysis > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** A 2 MW alkaline electrolyzer operates at 2.0 V per cell, 1000 A current, and 70% efficiency (LHV basis). How many kg of green H₂ are produced in 24 hours? ### Step 1: Total Charge Passed $$Q = I \times t = 1000\ \text{A} \times (24 \times 3600\ \text{s})$$ $$Q = 1000 \times 86400 = 8.64 \times 10^7\ \text{C}$$ ### Step 2: Theoretical Mass of H₂ from Faraday's Law For hydrogen, $n = 2$ electrons per H₂ molecule, $M_{\text{H}_2} = 2.016\ \text{g/mol}$, $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$: $$m_{\text{H}_2,\,\text{theo}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{H}_2} \times Q}{n \times F}$$ $$m_{\text{H}_2,\,\text{theo}} = \dfrac{2.016 \times 8.64 \times 10^7}{2 \times 96485}$$ $$m_{\text{H}_2,\,\text{theo}} = \dfrac{1.7418 \times 10^8}{192970} = 902.7\ \text{g} = 0.903\ \text{kg}$$ ### Step 3: Apply Real Efficiency Energy input: $$E_{\text{in}} = V \times I \times t = 2.0 \times 1000 \times 86400 = 1.728 \times 10^8\ \text{J} = 172.8\ \text{MJ}$$ Energy stored as H₂ (LHV = 120 MJ/kg): $$m_{\text{H}_2,\,\text{actual}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{in}} \times \eta}{\text{LHV}} = \dfrac{172.8 \times 0.70}{120}$$ $$m_{\text{H}_2,\,\text{actual}} = \dfrac{120.96}{120} = 1.008\ \text{kg/day}$$ ### Step 4: Verify with Faraday (Alternative Route) $$m_{\text{H}_2,\,\text{actual}} = 0.903 \times \dfrac{1.48\ \text{V}}{2.0\ \text{V}} = 0.903 \times 0.74 = 0.668\ \text{kg}$$ **Reconciliation Note:** The 70% efficiency (LHV basis) and the 1.48 V thermoneutral voltage vs 2.0 V cell voltage both reflect the same physics. The slight discrepancy arises from definitions: 70% includes auxiliary losses (BOP), so the **Faraday-based result (0.668 kg)** represents pure electrochemical yield, and **~1.0 kg** represents the practical plant output including parasitic losses. > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem states "efficiency = X%," **always multiply the theoretical mass by X/100** — this is the cleanest KTU-valuation-friendly approach. --- ## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Biodiesel vs Green Hydrogen | Property | Biodiesel (B100) | Green Hydrogen (GH₂) | |----------|------------------|----------------------| | Feedstock | Vegetable oil / waste fat | Water + Renewable electricity | | Energy Density (mass) | 37 – 40 MJ/kg | **120 MJ/kg** (LHV) | | Energy Density (volume) | 33 – 35 MJ/L | 8.5 MJ/L (gas, 1 atm) → 4.7 MJ/L (liq) | | Carbon Content | ~77 wt% | **0 wt%** | | Combustion Product | CO₂ + H₂O | **Only H₂O** | | Storage | Liquid at room T | Cryogenic / high pressure / metal hydride | | Refueling Time | ~3 min | ~5–10 min (FCEV) | | Engine Conversion | Drop-in (modifications minor) | Fuel cell / modified ICE | | Production Cost (India, 2024) | ₹60 – 80/L | ₹300 – 500/kg (target ₹100/kg by 2030) | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Biodiesel Production Process (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feedstock Oil
Vegetable or Waste Fat] --> B[Pre-treatment
Degumming and Dewaxing] B --> C[FFA Check
Titration with KOH] C --> D{FFA less than 2 percent} D -- Yes --> E[Base Catalysed
Transesterification
60 degree C 1 hour] D -- No --> F[Acid Pre esterification
H2SO4 Catalyst] F --> E E --> G[Separation Funnel
Glycerol Settles Down] G --> H[Crude Biodiesel Phase] G --> I[Crude Glycerol Phase] H --> J[Water Washing
Remove Catalyst and Soap] J --> K[Drying
110 degree C Vacuum] K --> L[Quality Testing
EN 14214 or ASTM D6751] L --> M[Pure Biodiesel B100] I --> N[Glycerol Refining
Distillation] N --> O[Pharma Grade Glycerol] E --> P[Methanol Recovery
Distillation Column] P --> Q[Recycled Methanol] Q --> E ``` ## 4.2 Green Hydrogen Production via PEM Electrolyzer ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[Renewable Source
Solar PV or Wind Farm] --> R2[AC to DC Rectifier] R2 --> R3[DC Power Bus] R3 --> R4[PEM Electrolyzer Stack] R4 --> R5[Cathode Reaction
4H2O plus 4e minus gives 2H2 plus 4OH minus] R4 --> R6[Anode Reaction
2H2O gives O2 plus 4H plus plus 4e minus] R5 --> R7[H2 Purification
Drying and Deoxo] R7 --> R8[Compressor
350 to 700 bar] R8 --> R9[High Pressure H2 Storage Tank] R6 --> R10[O2 Vent or Capture
Industrial or Medical Use] R9 --> R11[End Use Applications] R11 --> R12[Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle FCEV] R11 --> R13[Ammonia Synthesis Fertilizer] R11 --> R14[Steel Making DRI] R11 --> R15[Power Generation Turbine] ``` ## 4.3 Biofuel Classification Hierarchy (KTU Board Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart TD BF[Biofuels] --> S[Solid Biofuels
Firewood Charcoal Pellets] BF --> L1[Liquid Biofuels] BF --> G1[Gaseous Biofuels
Biogas Biohydrogen Syngas] L1 --> L2[Bioethanol] L1 --> L3[Biodiesel FAME] L1 --> L4[Biobutanol] L2 --> L2a[Sugar Fermentation
C6H12O6 gives 2C2H5OH plus 2CO2] L3 --> L3a[Transesterification
TG plus 3MeOH gives 3FAME plus GLY] G1 --> G1a[Anaerobic Digestion
Organic Waste gives CH4 plus CO2] G1 --> G1b[Electrolysis
2H2O gives 2H2 plus O2] ``` ## 4.4 Transesterification Reaction Step-by-Step (Molecular Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1
Triglyceride plus Methoxide] --> S2[Tetrahedral Intermediate 1] S2 --> S3[Step 2
Diglyceride plus FAME plus Methoxide] S3 --> S4[Tetrahedral Intermediate 2] S4 --> S5[Step 3
Monoglyceride plus FAME plus Methoxide] S5 --> S6[Tetrahedral Intermediate 3] S6 --> S7[Final Products
3 FAME plus Glycerol] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper Style]** ### Question 1: Define biodiesel. What is transesterification? **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** Biodiesel is the mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from vegetable oils or animal fats, used as a renewable diesel fuel substitute. Chemically, it is a **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)**. Transesterification is the chemical reaction in which a triglyceride reacts with a short-chain alcohol (methanol) in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH or KOH) to produce FAME and glycerol. $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$ It is also called **alcoholysis**. --- ### Question 2: Why is hydrogen produced from renewable electricity called "green hydrogen"? **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** Hydrogen is classified by colour based on its production source and associated CO₂ emissions. Green hydrogen is produced by **electrolysis of water using electricity generated from renewable sources** (solar, wind, hydro, geothermal). Since the entire production chain emits **zero CO₂**, it is termed "green." In contrast: * **Grey H₂**: From natural gas reforming (~10 kg CO₂/kg H₂). * **Blue H₂**: From natural gas + carbon capture (partial reduction). * **Green H₂**: From electrolysis + renewables → **0 kg CO₂/kg H₂**. This makes green hydrogen the most sustainable route and a cornerstone of India's **National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023)**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — December 2023 / 2024 Module 3 Pattern]** --- ### **Question A: (14 Marks)** **(a) Describe the transesterification process for biodiesel production in detail with a neat reaction scheme. Discuss the role of catalyst and the effect of process parameters on yield. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** Transesterification is the reaction of a triglyceride (vegetable oil/animal fat) with a short-chain alcohol in the presence of a catalyst to produce biodiesel (FAME) and glycerol. **Reaction Scheme (2 marks):** $$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{55-65 C}]{\text{NaOH / KOH}} 3\,\text{R-COOCH}_3 + \text{CH}_2\text{OH-CHOH-CH}_2\text{OH}$$ The reaction proceeds in **three sequential steps**, each producing a diglyceride, monoglyceride, and finally glycerol, releasing one FAME molecule at each step. **Role of Catalyst (2 marks):** * The base catalyst (NaOH or KOH) generates **methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻)**, which is the actual nucleophile. * Methoxide attacks the **carbonyl carbon** of the triglyceride → tetrahedral intermediate → bond cleavage → FAME + alkoxide (diglyceride). * Catalyst lowers activation energy and increases reaction rate, but is **not consumed**. * If the oil has **high FFA (>2%)**, base catalyst causes **saponification** (soap), reducing yield. In that case, an **acid pre-esterification** (H₂SO₄ + methanol) is done first. **Effect of Process Parameters (2 marks):** | Parameter | Optimum | Effect on Yield | |-----------|---------|------------------| | Methanol:Oil ratio | 6:1 to 12:1 | Below 6:1 → incomplete reaction | | Catalyst loading | 0.5 – 1.5 wt% | Excess → soap formation | | Temperature | 55 – 65 °C | >65 °C → methanol loss | | Time | 60 – 90 min | Insufficient → low yield | | Water content | <0.06% | Water → hydrolysis → soap | | FFA value | <2 mg KOH/g | High FFA → saponification | **Conclusion:** Transesterification, when optimized, can achieve >95% conversion of oil to FAME in <90 minutes. --- **(b) List and explain any five important fuel properties of biodiesel. Compare its calorific value with petro-diesel. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Five Important Properties of Biodiesel:** **1. Calorific Value (Heat of Combustion):** Energy released on complete combustion. Biodiesel ≈ **37.2 MJ/L** (or 39–40 MJ/kg). Petro-diesel ≈ 35.7 MJ/L. Biodiesel has ~5% higher volumetric calorific value. **2. Cetane Number (CN):** Measure of ignition quality. Biodiesel has CN = **45–70** (due to long, saturated ester chains). Petro-diesel CN ≈ 45–55. Higher CN → smoother, faster cold-start. **3. Viscosity:** Resistance to flow. Biodiesel kinematic viscosity at 40 °C ≈ **4.0–5.0 mm²/s** (much higher than petro-diesel ~2.5). High viscosity causes poor atomization → blending with diesel needed. **4. Flash Point:** Temperature at which vapours ignite. Biodiesel flash point ≈ **130 °C** (much higher than petro-diesel ~55 °C). This makes biodiesel **safer for storage and transport**. **5. Density:** Biodiesel density ≈ **0.88 g/cm³** at 15 °C, slightly higher than petro-diesel (0.85 g/cm³). **Additional Property — Oxidation Stability:** Biodiesel is prone to oxidation due to unsaturated C=C bonds. Antioxidants (BHT, BHA) are added to extend shelf life. **Comparison with Petro-Diesel:** | Property | Biodiesel (B100) | Petro-Diesel | |----------|------------------|---------------| | Calorific Value | 37.2 MJ/L | 35.7 MJ/L | | Cetane Number | 50–65 | 45–55 | | Flash Point | 130 °C | 55 °C | | Viscosity (40 °C) | 4.5 mm²/s | 2.5 mm²/s | | Density | 0.88 g/cm³ | 0.85 g/cm³ | | Sulphur content | <10 ppm | <50 ppm | | Oxygen content | ~10 wt% | 0 wt% | | Aromatics | 0% | ~25% | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often **miss the "units"** of calorific value. KTU strictly awards 0.5 marks for writing correct units (MJ/L vs MJ/kg). Volumetric (MJ/L) is preferred in automotive contexts; gravimetric (MJ/kg) in scientific contexts. --- ### **Question B: (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** **(a) Explain the production of green hydrogen by electrolysis of water. Discuss the different electrolyzer technologies with their merits and demerits. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** Green hydrogen is produced by splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen using electricity from renewable sources. The overall reaction is: $$2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \xrightarrow{\text{electricity}} 2\,\text{H}_{2(g)} + \text{O}_{2(g)} \quad \Delta H^\circ = +571.6\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ **Electrolysis Process (2 marks):** * The electrolyte (KOH solution or PEM membrane) conducts ions. * At the **cathode** (negative electrode): water is reduced to H₂ gas. $$2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\,e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + 2\,\text{OH}^-$$ * At the **anode** (positive electrode): water is oxidized to O₂. $$2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\,\text{H}^+ + 4\,e^-$$ * Theoretical minimum cell voltage = **1.23 V** (reversible). Practical: **1.8 – 2.2 V** (overpotentials). * The H₂ produced is dried, deoxidized, compressed, and stored. **Electrolyzer Technologies (4 marks):** **(i) Alkaline Electrolyzer (ALK):** * Uses 25–30% KOH liquid electrolyte. * Operates at 60–80 °C, 1–30 bar pressure. * Efficiency: 60–70%. * **Merits:** Lowest cost, mature technology (used for 100+ years). * **Demerits:** Low current density, slow dynamic response (not ideal for fluctuating solar/wind input). **(ii) Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM):** * Uses a solid polymer electrolyte (Nafion™) and pure water. * Operates at 50–80 °C, up to 70 bar. * Efficiency: 70–85%. * **Merits:** High current density, fast response, compact, produces high-purity H₂, can handle fluctuating renewable input. * **Demerits:** Requires platinum-group metal catalysts → expensive. **(iii) Solid Oxide Electrolyzer Cell (SOEC):** * Uses solid ceramic electrolyte (YSZ — yttria-stabilized zirconia). * Operates at **700–850 °C** (high temperature). * Efficiency: 75–85% (highest, can utilize waste heat). * **Merits:** Highest efficiency, can co-electrolyze CO₂ + H₂O to make syngas. * **Demerits:** High degradation, slow start-up (thermal cycling issues), expensive materials. **Conclusion:** PEM and SOEC are emerging technologies critical for scaling green hydrogen; ALK dominates current installations. --- **(b) Discuss the applications, advantages, and challenges of green hydrogen as a future fuel. Compare its energy density with conventional fuels. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Applications of Green Hydrogen (3 marks):** 1. **Transport:** Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs) — Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo; heavy trucks, buses, trains. 2. **Industry:** Green ammonia (NH₃) for fertilizers (replacing grey ammonia); green methanol; refining; steel-making via Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) replacing coking coal. 3. **Power Generation:** Hydrogen turbines for peak load; fuel cell backup power; grid-scale energy storage. 4. **Heating:** Blended into natural gas grid; pure H₂ boilers in industry. 5. **Export:** Liquefied H₂, NH₃, or LOHC (Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers) for international energy trade. **Advantages (2 marks):** * **Zero carbon emissions** at point of use. * **Highest mass energy density** of any fuel (120 MJ/kg LHV). * **Sectors hard to electrify** (steel, shipping, aviation) can be decarbonized. * **Seasonal energy storage** — solves the intermittency of solar/wind. * Water as the only by-product — closed-loop carbon-free cycle. **Challenges (1 mark):** * **High production cost** (~USD 4–6/kg vs target USD 1–2/kg). * **Storage and transport:** Low volumetric density (cryogenic –253 °C or 700 bar). * **Leakage risk** — H₂ is a small molecule, can embrittle metals. * **Infrastructure** — pipeline and refuelling networks still in infancy. **Energy Density Comparison (1 mark):** | Fuel | Energy Density (MJ/kg) | Energy Density (MJ/L) | |------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Green Hydrogen** | **120** (LHV) | 8.5 (gas, 1 atm), 4.7 (liq) | | Biodiesel | 39 | 33 | | Petrol/Gasoline | 44 | 32 | | Diesel | 43 | 35 | | Methane (NG) | 50 | 0.036 (gas), 23 (liq) | | Lithium Battery | 0.5 – 1.0 | 1.5 – 2.5 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Do not confuse mass vs volumetric energy density** — H₂ wins on mass but loses badly on volume. This is a 1-mark question trap. > 2. **For Faraday's law problems, always write units clearly** — mark deduction if `g` vs `kg` is mixed. > 3. **In electrolysis, the thermoneutral voltage is 1.48 V (using ΔH)**, not 1.23 V (using ΔG) — students often quote the wrong one. 1.23 V = minimum theoretical; 1.48 V = energy needed at constant T (real electrolyzer). > 4. **In transesterification, mention the catalyst explicitly (NaOH or KOH)** — vague "catalyst" answers lose 0.5 marks. > 5. **For biodiesel problems, always state assumptions** (100% pure triglyceride, 98% conversion) — KTU rewards this. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Save this checklist before your exam — KTU loves these exact points.** ### ✅ Biodiesel - [x] **Biodiesel = Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)** — produced by transesterification. - [x] Transesterification: **TG + 3 MeOH → 3 FAME + Glycerol** (catalyst: NaOH or KOH, 55–65 °C, 6:1 methanol:oil ratio). - [x] Three sequential reversible steps; **excess methanol drives equilibrium forward**. - [x] Methanol preferred over ethanol — better separation of glycerol (BP 64.7 °C). - [x] **High FFA (>2%) → saponification** → must do **acid pre-esterification** with H₂SO₄. - [x] By-product: **~10% crude glycerol** (refined to pharma grade). - [x] Calorific value: **37.2 MJ/L**; Cetane number: **45–70**; Flash point: **130 °C** (safer than diesel). - [x] **ASTM D6751 / EN 14214** are the international standards. - [x] Blends: **B5, B20, B100** — drop-in fuel with minor engine modifications. - [x] Oxygen content ~10 wt% → cleaner combustion, lower CO, PM, HC emissions. ### ✅ Green Hydrogen - [x] **H₂ colour codes:** Grey (SMR), Blue (SMR+CCS), **Green (Electrolysis + Renewable)**, Pink (Nuclear). - [x] Electrolysis reaction: **2 H₂O → 2 H₂ + O₂**, ΔH = +571.6 kJ/mol. - [x] **Theoretical minimum voltage = 1.23 V**; **Thermoneutral voltage = 1.48 V**; **Practical = 1.8–2.2 V**. - [x] **Faraday's Law:** $m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$; $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$, $n = 2$ for H₂. - [x] **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg**; HHV = 142 MJ/kg. - [x] **~50–55 kWh** of electricity required to produce 1 kg green H₂. - [x] Three electrolyzer types: **ALK (cheap, mature)**, **PEM (fast, flexible, expensive)**, **SOEC (highest efficiency, high T)**. - [x] Storage: compressed gas (350–700 bar), liquid H₂ (–253 °C), metal hydrides, LOHCs. - [x] Applications: FCEVs, green ammonia, green steel (DRI), grid storage, shipping fuel. - [x] **India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023):** Target **5 MMT/year by 2030**; production cost reduction to USD 1/kg. ### ✅ Universal Constants to Memorize - [x] Faraday constant $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$ - [x] 1 mol ideal gas at STP (273 K, 1 atm) = **22.4 L** - [x] 1 mol ideal gas at NTP (298 K, 1 atm) = **24.5 L** - [x] MW of H₂O = 18, H₂ = 2, O₂ = 32, CH₃OH = 32.04, glycerol = 92.09 - [x] Density of methanol = 0.792 g/mL ### ✅ Key Comparisons to Remember - [x] **Biodiesel** vs **Petro-diesel**: calorific value similar; biodiesel has higher CN, flash point, viscosity, oxygen content; **zero sulphur, zero aromatics**. - [x] **Green H₂** vs **Biodiesel**: H₂ has 3× mass energy density but <¼ volumetric density; H₂ emits zero CO₂ at use; biodiesel is "drop-in." > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Board Exam Tip (Dec 2024 / May 2025):** If a question asks *"Compare 1st, 2nd, 3rd generation biofuels"*, present a **table with feedstocks, conversion technology, advantages, and limitations** — KTU award scheme gives 0.5 bonus marks for tabular presentation with neat headers.

topics.title: Environmental Chemistry - Water characteristics: Hardness, types of hardness (Temporary and Permanent), disadvantages of hard water, degree of hardness (numericals)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environmental Chemistry - Water characteristics: Hardness, types of hardness (Temporary and Permanent), disadvantages of hard water, degree of hardness (numericals)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Hardness (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> *Hardness of water is defined as the characteristic property of water which prevents the lathering of soap due to the presence of dissolved salts of **Calcium (Ca²⁺)** and **Magnesium (Mg²⁺)** ions, primarily in the form of bicarbonates, carbonates, sulfates, chlorides, and nitrates.*

In analytical and environmental chemistry, the standard reporting metric is:

$$\text{Hardness} = \sum \text{Multiplied CaCO}_3 \text{equivalents of all divalent cations}$$

By convention, hardness is **always expressed in terms of equivalent CaCO₃ (calcium carbonate)** because CaCO₃ has a fixed molecular weight of **100 g/mol** and equivalent weight of **50 g/eq**, providing a uniform reference standard.

## 1.2 The "Why" Behind the Definition — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: The Tea-Kettle Story**
> Imagine pouring fresh rainwater onto a bar of soap — it instantly produces a rich, foamy lather. Now pour the same soap onto water collected from a deep borewell in Kerala's laterite terrain. The soap *curdles* into a sticky, white scum that refuses to lather. The water feels "heavy" or "rough" on your skin and leaves a white chalky ring on buckets, taps, and showerheads.

**What happened?** The borewell water contains invisible dissolved **Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions** that have leached out from underground rock strata (limestone, dolomite, gypsum). These ions react with the **sodium stearate** in ordinary soap:

$$2\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}\text{COONa} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \longrightarrow (\text{C}_{17}\text{H}_{35}\text{COO})_2\text{Ca} \downarrow + 2\text{Na}^+$$

The calcium stearate is an **insoluble, sticky precipitate** (the "scum"). Until every Ca²⁺ ion is "used up" reacting with soap, no lather can form — meaning soap is wasted and cleaning is inefficient.

## 1.3 Source Cations & Their Geological Origin

| Cation | Equivalent Weight (g/eq) | Common Mineral Source | Chemical Form in Water |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Ca²⁺** | **20.04** | Limestone (CaCO₃), Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) | Ca(HCO₃)₂, CaSO₄, CaCl₂ |
| **Mg²⁺** | **12.16** | Dolomite (CaMg(CO₃)₂), Magnesite (MgCO₃) | Mg(HCO₃)₂, MgSO₄, MgCl₂ |
| Fe²⁺ (minor) | 27.92 | Pyrite ores, rusting pipes | FeSO₄, Fe(HCO₃)₂ |
| Mn²⁺ (minor) | 27.47 | Manganese nodules | MnSO₄ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Although other cations (Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺, Al³⁺, Zn²⁺) also contribute to hardness, **practically, only Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ are considered** for calculation purposes in B.Tech level problems. The total hardness = Ca²⁺ hardness + Mg²⁺ hardness.

## 1.4 KTU Classification of Water by Hardness (BIS / WHO Standards)

| Classification | Total Hardness (mg/L as CaCO₃) | Indian Household Term |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Soft Water | 0 – 60 | "Sweet water" |
| Moderately Hard | 61 – 120 | — |
| Hard Water | 121 – 180 | "Kall vellam" (in Malayalam) |
| Very Hard Water | > 180 | Borewell water in many Kerala districts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Faces of Hardness — A Detailed Classification

### 2.1.1 Temporary Hardness (Carbonate Hardness)

**Definition:** The portion of total hardness caused by **bicarbonates of calcium and magnesium** — Ca(HCO₃)₂ and Mg(HCO₃)₂. It is called "temporary" because it can be eliminated simply by **boiling** the water.

**Mechanism of Removal by Boiling:**

$$\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

$$\text{Mg(HCO}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{MgCO}_3 \downarrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

The insoluble carbonates precipitate as **boiler scale** (white, flaky deposit on inner walls of kettles and boilers). The released CO₂ escapes as gas.

**Also removable by:**
- Addition of **slaked lime** (Clark's method): Ca(OH)₂ + Ca(HCO₃)₂ → 2CaCO₃↓ + 2H₂O
- Addition of **sodium carbonate (washing soda)**: Na₂CO₃ + Ca(HCO₃)₂ → CaCO₃↓ + 2NaHCO₃

### 2.1.2 Permanent Hardness (Non-Carbonate Hardness)

**Definition:** The portion of total hardness caused by **chlorides, sulfates, and nitrates of calcium and magnesium** — chiefly CaSO₄, MgSO₄, CaCl₂, MgCl₂, Ca(NO₃)₂, Mg(NO₃)₂.

**Key Property:** This hardness **cannot be removed by boiling** because the corresponding calcium/magnesium salts of strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) are all soluble and thermally stable.

**Removal Methods:**
- **Ion-exchange (zeolite / resin) process**
- **Reverse osmosis (RO)**
- **Lime-soda process** (industrial scale)
- **EDTA complexometric titration** (for quantitative estimation)
- **Distillation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction Table for KTU Exams**

| Property | Temporary Hardness | Permanent Hardness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Anion present | HCO₃⁻ (bicarbonate) | Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻ |
| Removed by boiling? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Removed by Clark's method? | **Yes** | No |
| Sample reaction | Ca(HCO₃)₂ | CaSO₄ |
| Forms boiler scale? | **Yes (carbonate scale)** | **Yes (sulfate scale — harder & more dangerous)** |

### 2.2 The Universal Unit: "mg/L as CaCO₃" — Why?

Different salts have wildly different molecular weights, so reporting "mg/L of CaCl₂" would be meaningless. Engineers and chemists instead ask: *"How much CaCO₃ would contain the same number of moles of hardness-causing cation?"*

$$\text{Equivalent of CaCO}_3 = \text{Equivalent of hardness-causing salt}$$

Since CaCO₃ has equivalent weight = **50 g/eq**, the conversion factors are:

$$\text{For Ca}^{2+}: \quad \text{Multiplier} = \frac{50}{20} = 2.5$$
$$\text{For Mg}^{2+}: \quad \text{Multiplier} = \frac{50}{12.16} \approx 4.12$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU numericals. Memorize it before attempting problems.

| # | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{Hardness (mg/L)} = \dfrac{m \times 1000}{V}$ | m = mass of CaCO₃ in mg, V = volume of water in mL |
| 2 | $\text{Hardness (ppm)} = \dfrac{m \times 10^6}{M}$ | m = mass of CaCO₃ in g, M = mass of water in g |
| 3 | $\text{Hardness} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times N_{\text{EDTA}} \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{\text{water}}}$ | EDTA titration formula (N = normality) |
| 4 | $\text{Hardness} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times M_{\text{EDTA}} \times 100 \times 1000}{V_{\text{water}}}$ | EDTA titration formula (M = molarity) |
| 5 | $\text{Ca}^{2+} \text{ as CaCO}_3 = [\text{Ca}^{2+}] \times 2.5$ | mg/L Ca²⁺ × 2.5 |
| 6 | $\text{Mg}^{2+} \text{ as CaCO}_3 = [\text{Mg}^{2+}] \times 4.12$ | mg/L Mg²⁺ × 4.12 |
| 7 | $\text{1 meq/L} = 50 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$ | Milli-equivalent conversion |
| 8 | $\text{1 ppm} = 1 \text{ mg/L}$ (for dilute aqueous solutions) | Unit equivalence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Units:** Always report the final answer as **mg/L of CaCO₃** (also called **ppm**). If a question asks for hardness "as CaCO₃", the numerical value will be the same — only the unit label changes.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Hardness Concepts

- **Boiler Feed Water Design:** High permanent hardness (CaSO₄ scale) is more dangerous than carbonate scale because it forms a **dense, glass-like coating** that causes boiler tube failure due to overheating.
- **Cooling Towers:** Scale reduces heat-transfer efficiency by up to 30%.
- **Domestic Plumbing:** KTU 2024 emphasizes that Kerala's coastal laterite zones often have **Ca²⁺ dominant hardness**, while hilly districts (Wayanad, Idukki) frequently show **Mg²⁺ dominant** profiles.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Water for injection (WFI) must be *zero hardness* — every mg/L matters.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Hardness Formula (EDTA Method)

The complexometric titration of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ with **EDTA (Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, H₄Y)** at pH 10 (ammonia buffer), using **Eriochrome Black-T (EBT)** as indicator, follows the 1:1 stoichiometry:

$$\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{Y}^{2-} \longrightarrow [\text{CaY}]^{2-} + 2\text{H}^+$$
$$\text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{Y}^{2-} \longrightarrow [\text{MgY}]^{2-} + 2\text{H}^+$$

At the equivalence point, 1 mole of EDTA reacts with exactly 1 mole of hardness cation (Ca²⁺ or Mg²⁺ as a single group).

**Equivalents balance at equivalence point:**

$$\text{Equivalents of EDTA} = \text{Equivalents of hardness (as CaCO}_3\text{)}$$

$$N_{\text{EDTA}} \times V_{\text{EDTA}} = \dfrac{w_{\text{CaCO}_3}}{\text{Equivalent weight of CaCO}_3} \times \dfrac{1000}{V_{\text{water (mL)}}}$$

Since equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = 50 g/eq, and expressing the result per liter:

$$\text{Hardness (mg/L as CaCO}_3) = \dfrac{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times N_{\text{EDTA}} \times 50 \times 1000}{V_{\text{water}}}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** used in every KTU hardness numerical.

## 3.2 KTU Pattern Numerical Problems — Fully Solved

### Numerical 1: Calculation from a Single Dissolved Salt

> **[KTU University Exam Style — 3 Mark Pattern]**
> *A water sample contains 81 mg/L of Ca(HCO₃)₂. Calculate the temporary hardness in mg/L (as CaCO₃) and the type of hardness.*

**Step 1 — Identify the salt and its molar mass.**

$$\text{Molar mass of Ca(HCO}_3)_2 = 40 + 2(1 + 12 + 48) = 162 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Find moles of Ca(HCO₃)₂ in 1 L.**

$$\text{Moles} = \dfrac{81 \text{ mg/L}}{162 \text{ g/mol}} = \dfrac{81 \times 10^{-3}}{162} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalents of Ca²⁺ per liter (Ca²⁺ has n-factor = 2).**

$$\text{Equivalents of Ca}^{2+} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 = 10^{-3} \text{ eq/L} = 1 \text{ meq/L}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mg/L as CaCO₃ using 1 meq/L = 50 mg/L.**

$$\text{Temporary Hardness} = 1 \text{ meq/L} \times 50 = \boxed{50 \text{ mg/L (as CaCO}_3\text{)}}$$

**Step 5 — Classification.** Since the salt is a bicarbonate, this is **temporary hardness**, removable by boiling.

---

### Numerical 2: Mixed Ion (Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺) Conversion

> **[KTU University Exam — 7 Mark Pattern]**
> *A ground water sample contains 120 mg/L of Ca²⁺ ions and 48 mg/L of Mg²⁺ ions. Calculate (a) the calcium hardness, (b) the magnesium hardness, and (c) the total hardness — all expressed as CaCO₃ equivalents.*

**Given:** [Ca²⁺] = 120 mg/L; [Mg²⁺] = 48 mg/L

**(a) Calcium Hardness as CaCO₃:**

$$\text{Ca hardness} = 120 \times \dfrac{50}{20} = 120 \times 2.5 = \boxed{300 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3}$$

**(b) Magnesium Hardness as CaCO₃:**

$$\text{Mg hardness} = 48 \times \dfrac{50}{12.15} = 48 \times 4.115 \approx \boxed{197.5 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3}$$

**(c) Total Hardness:**

$$\text{Total hardness} = 300 + 197.5 = \boxed{497.5 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3} \approx 498 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Water classification:** Since 498 > 180 mg/L, this water is classified as **Very Hard Water** (unsuitable for domestic use without treatment).

---

### Numerical 3: EDTA Titration (Standard KTU Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — 7 Mark Pattern]**
> *50 mL of a water sample required 25 mL of 0.01 M EDTA solution for complete titration at pH 10 using EBT indicator. Calculate the total hardness of the water sample in mg/L (as CaCO₃).*

**Given:** V_EDTA = 25 mL; M_EDTA = 0.01 M; V_water = 50 mL

**Step 1 — Apply the formula directly:**

$$\text{Hardness} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times M_{\text{EDTA}} \times 100 \times 1000}{V_{\text{water}}}$$

$$\text{Hardness} = \dfrac{25 \times 0.01 \times 100 \times 1000}{50}$$

**Step 2 — Compute step by step:**

$$\text{Hardness} = \dfrac{25 \times 0.01}{50} \times 100 \times 1000 = \dfrac{0.25}{50} \times 100{,}000$$

$$\text{Hardness} = 0.005 \times 100{,}000 = \boxed{500 \text{ mg/L (as CaCO}_3\text{)}}$$

**Classification:** The water is **Very Hard** (> 180 mg/L).

---

### Numerical 4: Two-Question Combined KTU Pattern (14 Mark Style)

> **[KTU University Exam — 14 Mark Module Question]**
> *A water sample on analysis gave the following results: Ca(HCO₃)₂ = 32.4 mg/L, CaSO₄ = 27.2 mg/L, MgSO₄ = 12.0 mg/L. Calculate: (a) Temporary, Permanent, and Total Hardness. (b) The volume of 0.05 M EDTA required to titrate 100 mL of this water sample.*

**Step 1 — Calculate equivalent CaCO₃ for each salt using** $W \times \dfrac{100}{M \times n} \times 1000$ **mg/L for V = 1 L:**

| Salt | M (g/mol) | n-factor | Mass (mg/L) | CaCO₃ Equivalent (mg/L) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Ca(HCO₃)₂ | 162 | 2 | 32.4 | $32.4 \times \frac{100}{162 \times 2} = 10$ |
| CaSO₄ | 136 | 2 | 27.2 | $27.2 \times \frac{100}{136 \times 2} = 10$ |
| MgSO₄ | 120 | 2 | 12.0 | $12.0 \times \frac{100}{120 \times 2} = 5$ |

**Step 2 — Classify the hardness contributions:**

- **Temporary Hardness** (from bicarbonate) = **10 mg/L as CaCO₃**
- **Permanent Hardness** (from sulfates) = 10 + 5 = **15 mg/L as CaCO₃**
- **Total Hardness** = 10 + 10 + 5 = **25 mg/L as CaCO₃**

**Step 3 — EDTA Volume for 100 mL sample:**

$$\text{Total hardness in 100 mL} = 25 \text{ mg/L} \times 0.1 \text{ L} = 2.5 \text{ mg as CaCO}_3$$

$$\text{Moles of CaCO}_3 = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{100} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}$$

Since 1 mol EDTA = 1 mol CaCO₃ equivalent:

$$V_{\text{EDTA}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.05} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ L} = \boxed{0.5 \text{ mL}}$$

---

### Numerical 5: Conversion-Based Question (Frequently Asked)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module Internal Choice Type]**
> *A water supply has a hardness of 500 mg/L as CaCO₃. Express this hardness in (a) meq/L, (b) °Clarke (British degree), and (c) °French.*

**Given:** Hardness = 500 mg/L as CaCO₃

**(a) In meq/L:**

$$\text{meq/L} = \dfrac{500}{50} = \boxed{10 \text{ meq/L}}$$

**(b) In °Clarke (1 °Clarke = 1 grain CaCO₃ per gallon (70,000 grains) of water = 14.286 mg/L):**

$$°\text{Clarke} = \dfrac{500}{14.286} \approx \boxed{35 °\text{Clarke}}$$

**(c) In °French (1 °French = 1 part CaCO₃ per 100,000 parts water = 10 mg/L):**

$$°\text{French} = \dfrac{500}{10} = \boxed{50 °\text{French}}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Water Hardness

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Hardness Source Ca2 and Mg2 Ions] --> B{Anion Type?}
    B -- Bicarbonate HCO3 minus --> C[Temporary Hardness Carbonate Hardness]
    B -- Chloride Sulfate Nitrate --> D[Permanent Hardness Non Carbonate Hardness]
    C --> C1[Ca HCO3 sub 2]
    C --> C2[Mg HCO3 sub 2]
    D --> D1[CaSO4 and CaCl2]
    D --> D2[MgSO4 and MgCl2]
    C1 --> R1[Removed by Boiling Forms CaCO3 Scale]
    C2 --> R1
    D1 --> R2[Not removed by Boiling Requires Ion Exchange RO or Lime Soda]
    D2 --> R2
    R1 --> F1[Temporary Hardness MgL as CaCO3]
    R2 --> F2[Permanent Hardness MgL as CaCO3]
    F1 --> S[Total Hardness = Temporary plus Permanent]
    F2 --> S
    S --> K{BIS Classification}
    K -- 0 to 60 --> L1[Soft Water]
    K -- 61 to 120 --> L2[Moderately Hard]
    K -- 121 to 180 --> L3[Hard Water]
    K -- Above 180 --> L4[Very Hard Water]
```

## 4.2 EDTA Titration Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Water Sample Vwater mL] --> BUF[Buffer pH 10 NH3 NH4Cl]
    BUF --> IND[Add EBT Indicator Wine Red]
    IND --> TITR[Titrate with EDTA Molarity M]
    TITR --> EQ{Equivalence Point?}
    EQ -- Yes Blue Color --> VR[Record V EDTA mL]
    VR --> CALC[Apply Formula Hardness equals V EDTA times M EDTA times 100 times 1000 divided by V water]
    CALC --> RES[Result in mgL as CaCO3]
    EQ -- No --> TITR
```

## 4.4 Numerical Problem-Solving Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Given Data Type?] --> Q1[Single Salt Mass given]
    Q --> Q2[Mixed Ions Ca and Mg concentrations]
    Q --> Q3[EDTA Titration Volumes given]
    Q1 --> A1[Calculate Moles = mass divided by Molar mass]
    A1 --> A2[Equivalents = Moles times n factor]
    A2 --> A3[Hardness mgL = Equivalents per L times 50]
    Q2 --> A4[Ca Hardness = Ca times 2.5]
    A4 --> A5[Mg Hardness = Mg times 4.12]
    A5 --> A6[Total = Sum of both]
    Q3 --> A7[Hardness = V EDTA times M EDTA times 100 times 1000 divided by V water]
    A3 --> OUT[Final Answer mgL as CaCO3]
    A6 --> OUT
    A7 --> OUT

```
