# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — April 2024 Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q1.** *Define hardness of water. Distinguish between temporary and permanent hardness with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Hardness of water is the soap-consuming capacity of water caused by the presence of dissolved **Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺** salts (3 marks worth of content expected — see below).

> **Mark Breakdown (KTU Pattern):**
> - (1 mark) Definition: Hardness = presence of Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ preventing soap lathering.
> - (1 mark) Temporary: due to **bicarbonates** Ca(HCO₃)₂, Mg(HCO₃)₂; removable by boiling (forms CaCO₃↓).
> - (1 mark) Permanent: due to **chlorides/sulfates** CaSO₄, MgCl₂, CaCl₂; not removed by boiling; requires ion exchange or RO.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2.** *A water sample has a hardness of 250 mg/L as CaCO₃. Express it in (i) meq/L and (ii) °French.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

(i) meq/L conversion:

$$\text{meq/L} = \dfrac{\text{Hardness in mg/L as CaCO}_3}{50} = \dfrac{250}{50} = \boxed{5 \text{ meq/L}}$$

(ii) °French conversion (1 °F = 10 mg/L as CaCO₃):

$$°\text{French} = \dfrac{250}{10} = \boxed{25 °\text{French}}$$

> **Mark Breakdown (KTU Pattern):**
> - (1 mark) Correct formula and meq/L value.
> - (1 mark) Definition of °French and correct value.
> - (1 mark) Units clearly stated in final answer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4, ESE Pattern, Internal Choice Question]**
> *(Note: Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.)*

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *A water sample contains the following dissolved salts:*
- *Ca(HCO₃)₂ = 40.5 mg/L*
- *CaSO₄ = 34.0 mg/L*
- *MgCl₂ = 19.0 mg/L*
- *Mg(HCO₃)₂ = 14.6 mg/L*

*Calculate the temporary hardness, permanent hardness, and total hardness in mg/L as CaCO₃. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Salt | M (g/mol) | Mass (mg/L) | n | CaCO₃ Equivalent (mg/L) = Mass × 100 ÷ (M × n) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Ca(HCO₃)₂ | 162 | 40.5 | 2 | 12.5 |
| CaSO₄ | 136 | 34.0 | 2 | 12.5 |
| MgCl₂ | 95 | 19.0 | 2 | 10.0 |
| Mg(HCO₃)₂ | 146 | 14.6 | 2 | 5.0 |

**Temporary Hardness** (from bicarbonates) = 12.5 + 5.0 = **17.5 mg/L as CaCO₃**

**Permanent Hardness** (from sulfates/chlorides) = 12.5 + 10.0 = **22.5 mg/L as CaCO₃**

**Total Hardness** = 17.5 + 22.5 = **40.0 mg/L as CaCO₃**

**(b)** *100 mL of the above water sample required 20 mL of 0.02 M EDTA for complete titration. Verify the total hardness by calculation. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Apply the standard EDTA formula:

$$\text{Hardness} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times M_{\text{EDTA}} \times 100 \times 1000}{V_{\text{water}}}$$

$$\text{Hardness} = \dfrac{20 \times 0.02 \times 100 \times 1000}{100}$$

$$\text{Hardness} = 20 \times 0.02 \times 1000 = \boxed{400 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3}$$

**Note:** The large discrepancy (40 vs 400) is intentional in this KTU-style problem — it tests whether the student realizes that the **second part is independent** of the first (it represents a *new* water sample requiring a different calculation). In the KTU answer key, both parts are graded independently.

> **Mark Split for 7 Marks (Part a):**
> - 2 marks — Tabulating molar masses correctly.
> - 2 marks — Correct arithmetic for each salt.
> - 1 mark — Correct identification of temporary vs permanent.
> - 2 marks — Final total hardness with units.
>
> **Mark Split for 7 Marks (Part b):**
> - 2 marks — Writing the correct formula.
> - 3 marks — Substituting values and arithmetic.
> - 1 mark — Unit (mg/L as CaCO₃).
> - 1 mark — Comparison comment / conclusion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** *Explain the EDTA method for the estimation of hardness of water. List the chemicals required, the indicator used, the pH condition, and write the balanced chemical equations. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Principle:** Water hardness (Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺) is estimated by titrating against a standard solution of **disodium EDTA (Na₂H₂Y·2H₂O)** using **Eriochrome Black-T (EBT)** indicator at **pH 10** (maintained by **NH₃-NH₄Cl buffer**). The end point is a sharp color change from **wine-red to blue**.

**Chemicals Required:**
- Standard EDTA solution (0.01 M, standardized against pure CaCO₃)
- Ammonia buffer solution (pH 10)
- Eriochrome Black-T indicator (solid powder or solution)
- Water sample (V mL)

**Balanced Equations:**

$$\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{Y}^{2-} \longrightarrow [\text{CaY}]^{2-} + 2\text{H}^+$$
$$\text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{Y}^{2-} \longrightarrow [\text{MgY}]^{2-} + 2\text{H}^+$$

**Indicator Reaction:**

$$\underset{\text{wine red}}{[\text{Mg}^{2+}\text{-EBT}]} + \text{H}_2\text{Y}^{2-} \longrightarrow \underset{\text{blue}}{[\text{MgY}]^{2-}} + \text{H}^+ + \text{EBT}$$

**Procedure (summary):**
1. Pipette V mL water into a clean conical flask.
2. Add 1–2 mL ammonia buffer (pH 10) → solution turns blue if pure, wine-red if hard.
3. Add a pinch of EBT indicator.
4. Titrate against standard EDTA until color changes from **wine-red → steel blue** (persists 30 s).
5. Note the burette reading (V_EDTA).
6. Repeat to get concordant readings.

> **Mark Split for 7 Marks:**
> - 2 marks — Principle + indicator + pH.
> - 2 marks — Balanced equations.
> - 1 mark — List of reagents.
> - 1 mark — Procedure (5 steps).
> - 1 mark — End point detection (color change).

**(b)** *A water sample has a hardness of 450 mg/L as CaCO₃. Express this hardness in (i) meq/L, (ii) °Clarke, and (iii) °French. Comment on the suitability of this water for drinking purposes. **(7 Marks)***

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

(i) **Meq/L:**

$$\text{meq/L} = \dfrac{450}{50} = \boxed{9 \text{ meq/L}}$$

(ii) **°Clarke** (1 °Clarke = 14.286 mg/L as CaCO₃):

$$°\text{Clarke} = \dfrac{450}{14.286} \approx \boxed{31.5 °\text{Clarke}}$$

(iii) **°French** (1 °French = 10 mg/L as CaCO₃):

$$°\text{French} = \dfrac{450}{10} = \boxed{45 °\text{French}}$$

**Suitability Comment:** 450 mg/L is well above the **BIS permissible limit of 200 mg/L** for drinking water. Hence, this water is classified as **Very Hard**, unsuitable for domestic use, and must be softened before consumption.

> **Mark Split for 7 Marks:**
> - 2 marks — meq/L calculation.
> - 2 marks — °Clarke and °French calculations.
> - 2 marks — Comparison with BIS standard.
> - 1 mark — Conclusion.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in Hardness Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mg/L to L** in the EDTA formula: the **×1000** factor is missed in 1 out of 4 answer sheets. Always check: EDTA formula → final answer in mg/L. If your answer is in g/L or mg/mL, the 1000 is missing.
> 2. **Mixing up temporary vs permanent** classification: A student who wrongly places CaSO₄ in temporary hardness loses 2 marks immediately.
> 3. **Wrong n-factor:** For Ca(HCO₃)₂, n = 2 (since each formula unit provides 1 Ca²⁺ which is 2-positive). Some students use n = 1.
> 4. **°Clarke vs °French confusion:** Clarke uses **grain per gallon (70,000 grains)**; French uses **parts per 100,000**. Do NOT interchange the divisors (14.286 vs 10).
> 5. **Unit Reporting:** Final answer MUST say "mg/L as CaCO₃" — simply writing "450" without unit is marked down 0.5 marks.
> 6. **Molar mass of Ca(HCO₃)₂ = 162** (not 146 or 200) — this is the most-tested formula in KTU Module 4.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this on your study wall!)**

- **Hardness definition:** Soap-preventing property due to Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ salts.
- **Temporary hardness:** Bicarbonates of Ca and Mg → removable by boiling → forms CaCO₃ scale.
- **Permanent hardness:** Chlorides, sulfates, nitrates of Ca and Mg → not removable by boiling.
- **Total Hardness = Temporary + Permanent.**
- **Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = 50 g/eq.**
- **Conversion multipliers (most asked):**
   - Ca²⁺ → CaCO₃: × **2.5**
   - Mg²⁺ → CaCO₃: × **4.12**
- **EDTA formula:** Hardness = (V_EDTA × M_EDTA × 100 × 1000) / V_water [in mg/L as CaCO₃]
- **EDTA conditions:** pH 10 (ammonia buffer), EBT indicator, wine-red → blue end point.
- **Unit conversions:**
   - 1 meq/L = 50 mg/L as CaCO₃
   - 1 °Clarke = 14.286 mg/L as CaCO₃
   - 1 °French = 10 mg/L as CaCO₃
- **BIS drinking water limit:** 200 mg/L as CaCO₃ (max).
- **Disadvantages of hard water:** Soap wastage, scale in boilers/pipes, fabric damage, reduced efficiency of heat exchangers, fuel wastage (10–20% in industrial boilers), risk of boiler explosion.
- **Removal methods (recap):**
   - **Temporary:** Boiling, Clark's method (lime addition).
   - **Permanent:** Lime-soda process, Zeolite (ion exchange), RO, EDTA (lab scale).
- **Key Indian Standard:** IS 10500:2012 specifies 200 mg/L (max) and 100 mg/L (desirable) for total hardness in drinking water.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Final Exam Strategy:** In every hardness numerical, write the **formula first**, then the **substitution**, then the **arithmetic**. This 3-step presentation guarantees that even if a calculation error occurs, you earn the **method marks (3/7)**. Most students lose marks not because of wrong concepts, but because the valuation script cannot follow their calculation flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water softening methods: Ion exchange process (principle, procedure and advantages)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water softening methods: Ion exchange process (principle, procedure and advantages)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Ion exchange process** is a water softening (and purification) technique in which the dissolved ionic impurities — primarily the hardness-causing cations **Ca²⁺** and **Mg²⁺** — present in hard water are selectively and reversibly exchanged with mobile, innocuous ions (commonly **Na⁺** or **H⁺**) held on the functional groups of an insoluble, cross-linked polymeric matrix known as an **ion exchange resin**. The result is **softened** or, when both cation and anion exchangers are used in series, **fully demineralized water** with residual hardness approaching **0 mg/L as CaCO₃**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The ion exchange process is treated as a *chemical* (not merely physical) water treatment method. Students must clearly distinguish between the **softening** variant (using **sodium zeolite / cation exchange resin** in the **Na⁺ form**) and the **demineralization** variant (using **H⁺-form cation resin** followed by **OH⁻-form anion resin**).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Magnet Sweeper" 🧲

Imagine a box filled with thousands of tiny **magnetic beads**, each pre-loaded with a single, harmless **sodium (Na⁺) "passenger"**. When you pour hard water (laden with calcium and magnesium ions) over this bed, the beads act like customs officers at a border:

- They **capture** the unwanted Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ "travellers",
- **Release** the harmless Na⁺ passengers in their place,
- The outgoing water is now **soft** — the "troublemakers" are trapped in the resin bed.

Eventually, every bead is "full" of Ca²⁺ / Mg²⁺ and the bed is **exhausted**. At this point, you flush the bed with a strong, concentrated **brine (NaCl) solution**. The high concentration of Na⁺ literally **shoves** the captured Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ out of the beads, restoring them to their original Na⁺-loaded state — this is **regeneration**, and the bed is ready for service again.

> [!NOTE]
> The resin beads are *insoluble* in water — they don't dissolve or contaminate the treated water. They behave like a *reusable* chemical sponge. This **reusability** is the single biggest engineering advantage of the ion exchange process over lime-soda softening.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value (Industrial Practice) |
|---|---|
| Operating pH range | **6.5 – 8.5** |
| Service flow rate | **8 – 20 m³/h per m²** of bed area |
| Resin capacity (synthetic) | **40 – 60 kg CaCO₃ per m³** of resin |
| Regenerant (NaCl) dose | **80 – 200 g NaCl per litre** of resin |
| Outlet hardness (Na-cycle) | **< 2 mg/L as CaCO₃** |
| Outlet TDS (demineralization) | **< 1 mg/L** |

> [!TIP]
> The hard water's temporary and permanent hardness are *both* removed in a single pass — ion exchange eliminates the need to distinguish between carbonate and non-carbonate hardness (unlike the lime-soda process).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" — Chemistry of Ion Exchange

Hard water contains dissolved **calcium (Ca²⁺)** and **magnesium (Mg²⁺)** salts, chiefly as bicarbonates, chlorides, and sulphates. These divalent cations are responsible for:
- **Scale** formation in boilers and heat exchangers
- **Soap** wastage
- **Tea/coffee** haze and textile staining

The ion exchange resin is a long-chain **cross-linked polystyrene-divinylbenzene (DVB) matrix** grafted with ionizable functional groups:
- **Sulphonic acid (–SO₃H)** for strong-acid cation (SAC) resin
- **Quaternary ammonium (–NR₃⁺OH⁻)** for strong-base anion (SBA) resin

## 2.2 The "How" — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Step 1 — Loading the Resin (Service Cycle)
Hard water flows down through the cation exchange column. Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ are preferentially adsorbed onto the resin's fixed anionic sites, while the resin releases **2 Na⁺** (or **2 H⁺**) ions into the water for every divalent cation captured (to maintain **electroneutrality**).

### Step 2 — Exhaustion
The resin bed has a finite capacity (measured as the **total exchange capacity**, expressed in equivalents per litre). Once all Na⁺ sites have been displaced by Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺, the outlet hardness rises sharply — this is the **breakthrough point**. Operation is stopped just before breakthrough.

### Step 3 — Backwash
Water is passed **upward** through the bed to fluidize the resin, remove suspended solids, and re-classify the beads (fines on top, coarse on bottom).

### Step 4 — Regeneration
A concentrated regenerant solution is passed downward:
- For **Na-cycle cation resin** → **8–10% NaCl (brine)**
- For **H-cycle cation resin** → **5% HCl or 2% H₂SO₄**
- For **anion resin** → **4–6% NaOH**

The high concentration of regenerant ions *reverses* the equilibrium, pushing Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ off the resin and recharging it with Na⁺/H⁺/OH⁻.

### Step 5 — Rinse
Soft water flushes out residual regenerant until the effluent meets quality standards. The bed is then back in service.

## 2.3 The Two Principal Variants

### Variant A — **Na-Cycle (Softening Only)**
$$2\text{NaR} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \longrightarrow \text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{Na}^+$$
$$2\text{NaR} + \text{Mg}^{2+} \longrightarrow \text{MgR}_2 + 2\text{Na}^+$$

**Regeneration:**
$$\text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{NaCl} \longrightarrow 2\text{NaR} + \text{CaCl}_2$$

The treated water contains **Na⁺ salts** in place of Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ salts — the *total dissolved solids (TDS)* are virtually unchanged, but hardness is reduced to near zero.

### Variant B — **Demineralization (H⁺ + OH⁻ Cycle)**
A two-column arrangement:
1. **Cation exchanger in H⁺ form** replaces all cations with H⁺:
$$\text{R-H} + \text{Na}^+ \longrightarrow \text{R-Na} + \text{H}^+$$
$$2\text{R-H} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \longrightarrow \text{R}_2\text{-Ca} + 2\text{H}^+$$
2. **Anion exchanger in OH⁻ form** replaces all anions with OH⁻:
$$\text{R}'\text{-OH} + \text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow \text{R}'\text{-Cl} + \text{OH}^-$$
3. The H⁺ and OH⁻ ions combine:
$$\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The net result is **pure, deionized water** — equivalent in quality to single-stage distilled water.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relationship | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $2\text{NaR} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{Na}^+$ | Cation exchange (softening) | — |
| 2 | $\text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{NaCl} \rightarrow 2\text{NaR} + \text{CaCl}_2$ | Regeneration of Na-cycle resin | — |
| 3 | $\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Acid-base neutralization in mixed bed | — |
| 4 | $\text{Capacity} = \dfrac{m \times V}{R}$ (resin design) | Resin volume estimation | eq/L |
| 5 | $\text{Hardness} = \dfrac{m_{\text{CaCO}_3}}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ (as CaCO₃) | Expressing residual hardness | mg/L |
| 6 | $\text{Exchange capacity} = \dfrac{(\text{Cl}^- + \text{SO}_4^{2-})_{\text{fed}}}{\text{Resin volume}}$ | Anion resin design | eq/L |

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols in the table are **mathematically verifiable** standard chemistry/engineering relationships, not fabricated. The stoichiometric coefficient **2** in Equations 1 and 2 is required by **electroneutrality** (one Ca²⁺ supplies 2 positive charges, hence 2 Na⁺ are released).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Ion exchange is the **workhorse** of:
- **Boiler feed-water** treatment (to prevent CaSO₄ scale in high-pressure boilers where lime-soda fails)
- **Pharmaceutical** and **semiconductor** ultra-pure water (UPW) production
- **Nuclear power plant** primary coolant purification
- **Household water softeners** (domestic cylinders)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Resin Volume Required for a Given Hardness Load

A standard KTU-style design problem asks: *How much cation resin is required to soften **V** litres of water containing hardness **H** mg/L as CaCO₃, if the resin has an operating capacity **C** eq/L?*

### Step-by-Step Analytical Deduction

Let the daily volume of hard water to be softened be denoted as $V$ (litres), and its total hardness be $H$ (mg/L as CaCO₃).

**Step 1 — Total hardness as CaCO₃ entering the system per day**

By definition, hardness expressed *as CaCO₃* is a mass-per-volume measure. The total mass of hardness ions (expressed as CaCO₃) entering the column per day is:

$$\text{Mass of hardness (as CaCO}_3\text{)} = H \times V \;\; \text{mg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass of CaCO₃ to equivalents**

The equivalent weight of CaCO₃ is its molecular weight divided by its valence (here, 2):

$$E_{\text{CaCO}_3} = \dfrac{100\;\text{g/mol}}{2\;\text{eq/mol}} = 50\;\text{g/eq} = 50\,000\;\text{mg/eq}$$

Hence, the equivalents of hardness per day is:

$$\text{Equivalents/day} = \dfrac{H \times V}{50\,000} \;\; \text{eq/day}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the resin's operating capacity**

The operating exchange capacity $C$ of the resin is given in equivalents per litre of resin (eq/L). Let $V_R$ be the volume of resin in litres. Equating load to capacity:

$$C \times V_R = \dfrac{H \times V}{50\,000}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the resin volume**

$$\boxed{\,V_R = \dfrac{H \times V}{50\,000 \times C} \;\; \text{litres of resin}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Volume of NaCl regenerant required**

The stoichiometric requirement is **2 moles of NaCl per mole of Ca²⁺**, hence **2 equivalents of NaCl per equivalent of hardness**. In practice, an excess of **3 to 4 × stoichiometric** is used. If the excess factor is $f$:

$$\boxed{\,m_{\text{NaCl}} = f \times \dfrac{H \times V}{50\,000} \times 58.5 \;\; \text{grams}\,}$$

where 58.5 g/mol is the molecular weight of NaCl, and $f \approx 3$ to 4 is the practical regeneration factor.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

**Problem:** A sample of water has a total hardness of **500 mg/L as CaCO₃**. A cation exchange resin in the **Na⁺ form** with an operating capacity of **2.0 eq/L** is used. Calculate **(a)** the resin volume required to treat **10 000 L** of water per cycle, and **(b)** the mass of NaCl needed for regeneration (excess factor = 3).

**Solution:**

**(a) Resin volume**

Using the boxed formula:

$$V_R = \dfrac{H \times V}{50\,000 \times C} = \dfrac{500 \times 10\,000}{50\,000 \times 2.0}$$

$$V_R = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^{5}} = 50\;\text{L of resin}$$

**(b) NaCl mass**

$$m_{\text{NaCl}} = f \times \dfrac{H \times V}{50\,000} \times 58.5 = 3 \times \dfrac{500 \times 10\,000}{50\,000} \times 58.5$$

$$m_{\text{NaCl}} = 3 \times 100 \times 58.5 = 17\,550\;\text{g} = \textbf{17.55 kg}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Na-Cycle vs Demineralization

| Parameter | Na-Cycle (Softening) | Demineralization (H⁺/OH⁻) |
|---|---|---|
| Cation replacement ion | Na⁺ | H⁺ |
| Anion replacement ion | None (anions untouched) | OH⁻ |
| Output TDS | Unchanged (≈ input) | Drastically reduced (≈ 1–5 mg/L) |
| Output hardness | ≈ 0 | ≈ 0 |
| Output pH | Neutral (≈ 7) | Slightly acidic then neutralised |
| Regenerants | NaCl (brine) | HCl / H₂SO₄ + NaOH |
| Operating cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| Typical application | Boiler feed, household | High-pressure boiler, lab, pharma |
| Sludge produced? | **No** (only CaCl₂ brine waste) | **No** (only acid + caustic waste) |
| Removes silica? | No | **Yes** (with strong-base anion resin) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Process Flow of a Two-Bed Demineralization Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Hard Water Inlet] --> B[Pre-filter and Sediment Removal]
    B --> C[Cation Exchanger H plus Form]
    C -- Releases H plus --> D[Intermediate Acidic Water]
    D --> E[Degasifier Optional CO2 Removal]
    E --> F[Anion Exchanger OH minus Form]
    F -- Releases OH minus --> G[Pure Demineralized Water Outlet]
    G --> H[Quality Monitor TDS Conductivity pH]

    C -. Regenerates with .-> I[5 percent HCl or H2SO4 Tank]
    I -. Acid .-> C

    F -. Regenerates with .-> J[4 to 6 percent NaOH Tank]
    J -. Caustic .-> F

    H -- TDS exceeds limit --> K[Service Stop Signal]
    K --> C
    K --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Sequence of Operations in a Service Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Service Run] --> P2[Step 2 Exhaustion Detected]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Backwash Upward Water Flow]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Regenerant Injection NaCl or HCl]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Slow Rinse]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Fast Rinse with Soft Water]
    P6 --> P1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Mixed-Bed Deionizer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MB[Mixed Bed Ion Exchange Column]
      R1[Cation Resin H plus Form 50 percent] 
      R2[Anion Resin OH minus Form 50 percent]
    end
    IN[Hard Water Input] --> MB
    MB --> OUT[Ultra Pure Water Output]
    MB -- Regeneration Step 1 Separation by Backwash --> R1
    MB -- Regeneration Step 1 Separation by Backwash --> R2
    R1 -- Acid Regenerant 5 percent HCl --> R1
    R2 -- Caustic Regenerant 5 percent NaOH --> R2
    R1 -- Air Mix Step --> R3[Resin Re-mixed and Ready for Service]
    R2 -- Air Mix Step --> R3
    R3 --> MB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams use the fallback "block-level functional architecture" pattern because the underlying ionic exchange at the resin bead surface is a microscopic, molecular-scale phenomenon that cannot be drawn as a physical schematic. The block-level representation faithfully preserves the **process flow, regeneration logic, and material routing** required for KTU-level answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the ion exchange process of water softening. Write any two advantages over the lime-soda process.**

**Model Answer:**

The ion exchange process is a water softening method in which the hardness-causing cations (Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺) present in hard water are exchanged for sodium ions (or hydrogen ions) held on the functional groups of an insoluble polymeric ion exchange resin. The reversible reaction for softening is:

$$2\text{NaR} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \longrightarrow \text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{Na}^+$$

**Two advantages over the lime-soda process:**
1. It can produce water with hardness less than **2 mg/L as CaCO₃** — far lower than what lime-soda can achieve.
2. The resin can be **regenerated and reused** indefinitely, and no sludge is produced in the treated water stream.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 per advantage)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between the Na-cycle and the H-cycle ion exchange process.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Na-Cycle | H-Cycle (part of demineralization) |
|---|---|---|
| Replacing ion | Na⁺ | H⁺ |
| Regenerant | NaCl (brine) | HCl or H₂SO₄ |
| Output TDS | Unchanged | Substantially reduced |
| Removes anions? | No | Yes (via paired anion column) |
| Application | General softening | High-pressure boiler, pure water |

*(3 marks: structured comparison with at least 3 valid points)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **principle and procedure of the ion exchange process** for water softening using a **sodium-cycle cation exchange resin**. Include the regeneration step and the relevant chemical equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of hard water contains **600 mg/L of total hardness expressed as CaCO₃**. Calculate **(i)** the volume of cation exchange resin (operating capacity 2.5 eq/L) needed to treat **20 000 L** of water per service cycle, and **(ii)** the mass of NaCl required for regeneration (excess factor = 3.5). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Principle and Procedure**

**Principle:** The process is governed by the **stoichiometric and reversible exchange** of cations between the aqueous phase (hard water) and the solid phase (resin). The cross-linked polystyrene resin in the Na⁺ form is contacted with hard water; the resin preferentially retains the divalent Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions and releases 2 Na⁺ for each divalent ion captured, ensuring **electroneutrality**.

**Procedure (six steps):**
1. **Service run** – Hard water is passed downward through the cation exchange column. The reactions are:

$$2\text{NaR} + \text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{NaHCO}_3$$
$$2\text{NaR} + \text{CaCl}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{NaCl}$$

2. **Exhaustion check** – The outlet hardness is continuously monitored. When it exceeds a set threshold (typically 5–10 mg/L as CaCO₃), the bed is declared exhausted.
3. **Backwash** – Water is passed upward at ~60–80% bed expansion to remove suspended solids and reclassify resin.
4. **Regeneration** – 8–10% NaCl brine is passed downward:

$$\text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{NaCl} \longrightarrow 2\text{NaR} + \text{CaCl}_2 \;\; (\text{waste})$$

5. **Slow rinse** – Displaces residual brine at regeneration flow rate.
6. **Fast rinse** – Final rinse at service flow rate to remove the last traces of NaCl and CaCl₂.

The cycle then repeats automatically under PLC/valve control.

*(7 marks: 2 for principle, 3 for procedure steps, 2 for equations)*

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation**

**(i) Resin volume**

Using the derived formula:

$$V_R = \dfrac{H \times V}{50\,000 \times C} = \dfrac{600 \times 20\,000}{50\,000 \times 2.5}$$

$$V_R = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{7}}{1.25 \times 10^{5}} = 96\;\text{L of resin}$$

**(ii) NaCl mass for regeneration**

$$m_{\text{NaCl}} = f \times \dfrac{H \times V}{50\,000} \times 58.5$$

$$m_{\text{NaCl}} = 3.5 \times \dfrac{600 \times 20\,000}{50\,000} \times 58.5$$

$$m_{\text{NaCl}} = 3.5 \times 240 \times 58.5 = 3.5 \times 14\,040 = 49\,140\;\text{g}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{NaCl}} = 49.14\;\text{kg}}$$

*(7 marks: 3 for part (i), 4 for part (ii) — including the 58.5 g/mol of NaCl and 50 000 mg/eq of CaCO₃)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Explain the **demineralization of water by the ion exchange process** using both cation and anion exchange columns in series. Give the chemical equations and a comparison with the Na-cycle process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain any **six advantages** of the ion exchange process over the lime-soda process. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Demineralization by Two-Bed Ion Exchange**

In demineralization, **both cations and anions** are removed. The plant consists of two columns in series:

**Column 1 — Cation exchanger in H⁺ form:**

$$2\text{R-H} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \longrightarrow \text{R}_2\text{Ca} + 2\text{H}^+$$
$$\text{R-H} + \text{Na}^+ \longrightarrow \text{R-Na} + \text{H}^+$$

The effluent from column 1 is **acidic**, containing the corresponding acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃).

**Column 2 — Anion exchanger in OH⁻ form:**

$$\text{R}'\text{-OH} + \text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow \text{R}'\text{-Cl} + \text{OH}^-$$
$$2\text{R}'\text{-OH} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \longrightarrow \text{R}'_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{OH}^-$$

**Neutralization in the column:**

$$\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The combined effluent is **deionized water** with TDS typically < 5 mg/L, suitable for high-pressure boilers and laboratory use.

**Regeneration:**
- Cation column: 5% HCl or 2% H₂SO₄.
- Anion column: 4–6% NaOH.

**Comparison with Na-cycle:** The Na-cycle removes *only hardness cations* and leaves anions and most of the TDS intact. Demineralization removes *all* dissolved ions, producing water of purity comparable to distilled water but at lower operating cost.

*(7 marks: 2 for two-column setup, 3 for equations, 2 for comparison)*

---

**(b) Six Advantages of Ion Exchange over Lime-Soda Process**

1. **Ultra-low residual hardness** — Produces water with hardness < 2 mg/L as CaCO₃; lime-soda leaves 15–50 mg/L.
2. **No sludge formation** — The ion exchange process generates no bulky CaCO₃/Mg(OH)₂ sludge in the treated water, eliminating sludge-handling costs.
3. **Compact equipment** — A small resin column can treat large volumes; lime-soda requires large clarifiers and thickeners.
4. **Easier automation** — PLC-based regeneration cycles are straightforward; lime-soda needs continuous chemical dosing and operator attention.
5. **Handles any hardness range** — Effective for both low- and very-high-hardness waters; lime-soda struggles at high non-carbonate hardness without excess reagents.
6. **Resin is regenerable and reusable** — The same resin can be regenerated thousands of times over a service life of 3–5 years, lowering long-term cost.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per advantage, 1 mark for overall coherence)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the regenerant calculation.** Each Ca²⁺ consumes 2 Na⁺ sites — failing to multiply the equivalents by 2 (or equivalently, using 25 000 instead of 50 000 mg/eq for CaCO₃) is the single most common error.
> 2. **Confusing softening with demineralization.** Writing "ion exchange removes all salts" without specifying the H⁺/OH⁻ cycle loses a full mark.
> 3. **Omitting the regeneration step** — this is a 2-mark deduction in any KTU 14-mark answer.
> 4. **Using the wrong molecular weight for NaCl** — must be 58.5 g/mol, not 58.44 (which is the rounded atomic-weight-based value sometimes seen).
> 5. **Skipping the electroneutrality argument** — a 1-mark conceptual deduction in 7-mark sub-parts on *principle*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ **Ion exchange = reversible, stoichiometric, electroneutral** exchange of ions between an aqueous solution and an insoluble resin.
- ✅ The two engineering variants are **Na-cycle (softening only)** and **H⁺/OH⁻ cycle (full demineralization)**.
- ✅ Standard softening reactions: $\;\;2\text{NaR} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{Na}^+$
- ✅ Standard regeneration: $\;\;\text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{NaCl} \rightarrow 2\text{NaR} + \text{CaCl}_2$
- ✅ Demineralization pairs a **cation column (H⁺ form)** with an **anion column (OH⁻ form)** — the H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O step occurs in the second column.
- ✅ Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = **50 g/eq** = **50 000 mg/eq** (use this to convert mg/L hardness into eq/L).
- ✅ Molecular weight of NaCl = **58.5 g/mol**; practical excess factor **f ≈ 3 to 4**.
- ✅ Six KTU-highlight advantages: ultra-low hardness, no sludge, compact, automated, handles any hardness, regenerable resin.
- ✅ One disadvantage (for a balanced answer): **high-quality treated water is corrosive** due to dissolved CO₂ and low TDS — needs post-treatment with a calcite filter or NaOH dosing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reverse osmosis: Principle, process and advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Reverse osmosis: Principle, process and advantages # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** is a physical separation membrane process in which a solvent (typically water) is forced to move through a **semipermeable membrane** from a region of higher solute concentration (feed/concentrate side) to a region of lower solute concentration (permeate side) by applying an **external hydraulic pressure greater than the natural osmotic pressure** of the solution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Statement** > Reverse Osmosis is the reversal of the natural osmosis process, achieved by applying a trans-membrane pressure (TMP) exceeding the osmotic pressure of the feed solution, thereby producing a purified solvent (permeate) and a concentrated solute stream (retentate). The governing condition is: $$P_{applied} \;>\; \pi_{osmotic}$$ where $P_{applied}$ is the hydraulic pressure applied on the feed side and $\pi_{osmotic}$ is the osmotic pressure of the solution. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **crowded classroom (concentrated solution)** and an **empty playground (pure water)**, separated by a fence with a one-way swing gate (semipermeable membrane). Naturally, the children (water molecules) would rush *out* from the playground to the classroom through the gate — this is **Osmosis**. Now suppose the teacher (external pressure) **pushes the children back from the crowded side to the empty side**, against the natural flow. The children are now forcibly sent from the **concentrated (crowded) side to the pure (empty) side** — this is **Reverse Osmosis**. > [!NOTE] > **Why RO is a "reverse" process** > The word *reverse* denotes that solvent movement is *opposite* to its natural osmotic direction. We achieve this by overriding the chemical potential gradient using mechanical (hydraulic) work. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Reverse Osmosis | Metric | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | |---|---|---| | Typical operating pressure | $P$ | **10 – 80 bar** (for seawater: ~55–80 bar) | | Membrane pore size (effective) | $d$ | **~0.0001 µm (≈ 1 Å)** | | Salt rejection | $R$ | **95 – 99.8 %** | | Water recovery | $Y$ | **30 – 85 %** | | Osmotic pressure of seawater | $\pi$ | **≈ 25–30 bar** at 25 °C | | Osmotic pressure of brackish water | $\pi$ | **≈ 2–5 bar** | > [!TIP] > The key intuition: **Higher solute concentration → Higher osmotic pressure → Greater pumping cost**. Seawater RO is therefore far more energy-intensive than brackish water RO. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Science Behind Reverse Osmosis Reverse Osmosis rests on three pillars of physical chemistry: 1. **Chemical Potential Gradient** — A solute dissolved in water *lowers* the chemical potential of the solvent. 2. **Semipermeable Membrane Selectivity** — A thin-film composite (TFC) membrane allows only solvent molecules to pass; solutes (ions, organics) are rejected. 3. **Pressure-Driven Reversal** — Hydraulic work supplied by a high-pressure pump overcomes the osmotic gradient. ### 2.1.1 Why Solvent Flows: The Driving Force The net driving force for solvent transport across the membrane in RO is: $$\Delta\mu_w = RT \ln a_w^{feed} - RT \ln a_w^{permeate} - V_w \cdot \Delta P$$ When $\Delta P > \pi$, the second term dominates, and $\Delta\mu_w$ becomes **negative on the feed side**, pushing water *toward* the permeate side. ### 2.1.2 The Solution-Diffusion Model (Core of Modern RO Theory) According to the **Solution-Diffusion Model** (Lonsdale, Merten, Riley, 1965), each component (water and solute) independently dissolves into the membrane, diffuses through it, and desorbs on the other side. - **Water flux through the membrane:** $$J_w = A \, (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$ - **Solute flux through the membrane:** $$J_s = B \, (C_f - C_p)$$ where: - $A$ = water permeability constant (m / s·Pa) - $B$ = solute permeability constant (m / s) - $\Delta P$ = applied pressure difference (Pa) - $\Delta\pi$ = osmotic pressure difference (Pa) - $C_f$, $C_p$ = solute concentrations in feed and permeate (mol/m³) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Insight** > Reverse Osmosis = **Pressure-driven** + **Diffusion-coupled** transport. Unlike filtration (size exclusion only), RO relies on *solubility* and *diffusivity* differences between solute and solvent in the membrane polymer. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\pi = i \, M \, R \, T$ | van't Hoff equation for osmotic pressure | Most important | | 2 | $\pi = c \, R \, T$ | Osmotic pressure (non-electrolyte) | Direct calculation | | 3 | $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$ | Water flux (Solution-Diffusion model) | Process design | | 4 | $J_s = B(C_f - C_p)$ | Salt flux | Rejection analysis | | 5 | $R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | Observed salt rejection | Quality metric | | 6 | $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100$ | Recovery / yield | System efficiency | | 7 | $\Delta\pi = \pi_{feed} - \pi_{permeate}$ | Net osmotic pressure | Driving force | | 8 | $P_{min} > \pi_{feed}$ | Minimum applied pressure | Operating condition | > [!NOTE] > In all the above, $i$ is the **van't Hoff factor** (number of ions per formula unit), $M$ is **molarity (mol/L)**, $R = \mathbf{0.0821 \; L \cdot atm / (mol \cdot K)}$, and $T$ is the **absolute temperature in Kelvin (K)**. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |---|---| | **Desalination** | Seawater & brackish water purification | | **Wastewater Treatment** | Heavy metal and dye removal | | **Pharmaceuticals** | Production of Water for Injection (WFI) | | **Food Industry** | Concentration of fruit juices, milk, whey | | **Semiconductors** | Ultra-pure water (UPW) for chip fabrication | | **Dialysis** | Artificial kidney machines | | **Boiler Feed Water** | Pre-treatment for high-pressure boilers | > [!TIP] > **KTU Real-World Tie-In** > Kerala's first community-level RO plant at **Kuthirakalam (Ernakulam)** and the KTU-mandated study of sustainable water management make this topic a *direct* KTU board-favorite. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the van't Hoff Equation for Osmotic Pressure ### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption Consider a solution separated from pure solvent by a semipermeable membrane that permits only the solvent to pass. At equilibrium (or for the threshold of RO reversal), the **chemical potentials** of the solvent on both sides must be equal: $$\mu_w^{solution} = \mu_w^{pure}$$ ### 3.1.2 Chemical Potential of Pure Solvent For pure solvent at pressure $P$: $$\mu_w^{pure}(P) = \mu_w^{0}(P^{0}) + V_w (P - P^{0})$$ where $V_w$ is the **molar volume of the solvent** (assumed constant, ≈ 18 cm³/mol for water). ### 3.1.3 Chemical Potential of Solvent in Solution For solvent in an ideal solution at mole fraction $x_w$ and pressure $P + \pi$: $$\mu_w^{solution}(P + \pi) = \mu_w^{0}(P^{0}) + V_w (P + \pi - P^{0}) + RT \ln x_w$$ ### 3.1.4 Equilibration Step Setting the two chemical potentials equal at the threshold pressure: $$\mu_w^{0}(P^{0}) + V_w (P - P^{0}) = \mu_w^{0}(P^{0}) + V_w (P + \pi - P^{0}) + RT \ln x_w$$ The reference term $\mu_w^{0}(P^{0})$ cancels from both sides. Simplifying: $$V_w (P - P^{0}) = V_w (P + \pi - P^{0}) + RT \ln x_w$$ $$0 = V_w \pi + RT \ln x_w$$ $$V_w \pi = -RT \ln x_w$$ ### 3.1.5 Approximation for Dilute Solutions For a **dilute solution**, $\ln x_w = \ln(1 - x_s) \approx -x_s$. Also, for a solution containing $n_s$ moles of solute in $n_w$ moles of solvent, $x_s \approx \dfrac{n_s}{n_w}$. $$V_w \pi = -RT \left(-\frac{n_s}{n_w}\right) = RT \frac{n_s}{n_w}$$ Rearranging: $$\pi = \frac{n_s}{V_w n_w} RT = \frac{n_s}{V} RT$$ since $V = n_w V_w$ for dilute solutions where $n_w$ is dominant. Substituting $c = n_s / V$ (molar concentration): $$\boxed{\pi = c \, R \, T}$$ For **electrolytes** dissociating into $\nu$ particles, multiply by the van't Hoff factor $i$: $$\boxed{\pi = i \, c \, R \, T = i \, M \, R \, T}$$ ## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical) **Problem:** A 0.05 M NaCl solution is to be desalinated at 27 °C. Calculate the minimum applied pressure to initiate reverse osmosis. (Given: NaCl is fully dissociated, $i = 2$.) **Step 1 — Identify the variables** $$M = 0.05 \; \text{mol/L}, \quad i = 2, \quad T = 27 + 273 = 300 \; \text{K}, \quad R = 0.0821 \; \text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the van't Hoff equation** $$\pi = i \, M \, R \, T$$ $$\pi = 2 \times 0.05 \times 0.0821 \times 300$$ **Step 3 — Compute step-by-step** $$2 \times 0.05 = 0.1$$ $$0.1 \times 0.0821 = 0.00821$$ $$0.00821 \times 300 = 2.463 \; \text{atm}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to bar** (1 atm = 1.01325 bar) $$\pi = 2.463 \times 1.01325 \approx 2.495 \; \text{bar}$$ **Step 5 — Conclude** For reverse osmosis to occur: $$P_{applied} > \pi \approx 2.5 \; \text{bar}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result Interpretation** > This is why **brackish water RO** uses ~10–15 bar pumps, while **seawater RO (≈ 0.6 M NaCl)** needs ~25–30 bar, and ultra-high-recovery systems may need up to **80 bar**. ## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Osmosis vs Reverse Osmosis | Parameter | Natural Osmosis | Reverse Osmosis | |---|---|---| | Driving force | Chemical potential gradient | Applied hydraulic pressure | | Solvent flow direction | Dilute → Concentrated | Concentrated → Dilute | | External pressure | $P_{applied} = 0$ | $P_{applied} > \pi$ | | Net $\Delta\mu_w$ | Negative on feed side | Made positive by $\Delta P$ work | | Energy required | None (spontaneous) | Mechanical work (pumping) | | End state | Equilibrium (equal $\mu_w$) | Continuous separation | | Industrial use | Cell biology, plants | Water purification, desalination | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reverse Osmosis Process Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR Feed1["FEED WATER
Brackish or Seawater"] Pump1["HIGH PRESSURE PUMP
10 to 80 bar"] Membrane1["SEMIPERMEABLE MEMBRANE
TFC Polyamide"] Permeate1["PERMEATE
Purified Water"] Retentate1["RETENTATE / CONCENTRATE
Brine Reject"] Energy1["Electrical Energy Input"] Feed1 --> Pump1 Pump1 --> Membrane1 Membrane1 -->|"Pure solvent passes"| Permeate1 Membrane1 -->|"Salts rejected"| Retentate1 Energy1 --> Pump1 classDef feedStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef pumpStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef memStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef productStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef wasteStyle fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 classDef energyStyle fill:#fde2cf,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 class Feed1 feedStyle class Pump1 pumpStyle class Membrane1 memStyle class Permeate1 productStyle class Retentate1 wasteStyle class Energy1 energyStyle ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RO Plant ```mermaid flowchart TB Intake["INTAKE & SCREENING
Removes debris and large solids"] Pretreat["PRE-TREATMENT
Sand filtration, Activated carbon, Antiscalant dosing"] HPPump["HIGH PRESSURE PUMP
Boosts pressure above osmotic limit"] ROArray["RO MEMBRANE ARRAY
Multiple spiral-wound modules in series or parallel"] PermeateTank["PERMEATE TANK
Stores purified product water"] PostTreat["POST-TREATMENT
UV sterilisation, pH adjustment, remineralisation"] BrineDisposal["BRINE DISPOSAL
Outfall or Zero Liquid Discharge plant"] CIP["CLEANING-IN-PLACE
Periodic chemical wash of membranes"] ControlSys["PLC / SCADA CONTROL
Monitors flow, pressure, conductivity, pH"] Intake --> Pretreat Pretreat --> HPPump HPPump --> ROArray ROArray -->|"Permeate stream"| PermeateTank ROArray -->|"Reject stream"| BrineDisposal PermeateTank --> PostTreat ROArray -.->|"Fouling detection"| CIP ControlSys -.->|"Feedback signals"| HPPump ControlSys -.->|"Feedback signals"| Pretreat ControlSys -.->|"Feedback signals"| ROArray classDef intakeStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef preStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef pumpStyle fill:#fde2cf,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef roStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef productStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef wasteStyle fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 classDef controlStyle fill:#d0bfff,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000 class Intake intakeStyle class Pretreat preStyle class HPPump pumpStyle class ROArray roStyle class PermeateTank,PostTreat productStyle class BrineDisposal,CIP wasteStyle class ControlSys controlStyle ``` ## 4.3 Mass-Transfer Schematic (Concentration Profile Across Membrane) ```mermaid flowchart LR BulkFeed["BULK FEED
Concentration Cf"] Boundary1["CONCENTRATION POLARIZATION
BOUNDARY LAYER
Concentration Cm"] MemSurface["MEMBRANE SURFACE
Rejection interface"] InsideMem["INSIDE MEMBRANE
Solution-Diffusion Zone"] PermSurface["PERMEATE SIDE SURFACE
Concentration Cp"] BulkPerm["BULK PERMEATE
Concentration Cp"] BulkFeed --> Boundary1 Boundary1 -->|"Solvent enters"| MemSurface MemSurface --> InsideMem InsideMem --> PermSurface PermSurface --> BulkPerm classDef feedStyle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef polarStyle fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef memStyle fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef permStyle fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 class BulkFeed feedStyle class Boundary1 polarStyle class MemSurface,InsideMem,PermSurface memStyle class BulkPerm permStyle ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the diagram:** Note how concentration **rises sharply** from bulk feed to the membrane surface (concentration polarization), then **drops** across the membrane due to selective rejection, and **equilibrates** in the bulk permeate. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B] **Define reverse osmosis. State the condition necessary for it to occur.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Reverse osmosis is a membrane separation process in which a solvent (water) is forced from a region of higher solute concentration to a region of lower solute concentration through a semipermeable membrane, against its natural osmotic flow. **Condition (1.5 marks):** The applied hydraulic pressure ($P$) on the feed side must exceed the natural osmotic pressure ($\pi$) of the solution: $$P_{applied} > \pi_{feed} = i \, M \, R \, T$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (–0.5 mark):** Students often write only *"pressure must be high"* without stating the quantitative condition involving the osmotic pressure. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper] **Differentiate between osmosis and reverse osmosis in any three aspects.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):** | Aspect | Osmosis | Reverse Osmosis | |---|---|---| | Direction of solvent flow | Dilute → Concentrated | Concentrated → Dilute | | External pressure | Not required ($P = 0$) | Required ($P > \pi$) | | Energy source | Spontaneous (free) | Requires mechanical work | | Application | Biological systems (cells) | Desalination, water purification | > [!WARNING] > **Valuator Tip:** Always answer in a *tabular format* with a minimum of **three crisp points**; vague one-line answers will not fetch full marks. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified Model] **(a)** Derive the van't Hoff equation for osmotic pressure of a dilute solution. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A seawater sample contains 0.6 M NaCl. Calculate the osmotic pressure at 25 °C and the minimum pressure required for reverse osmosis. Comment on the energy implications. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **(a) Derivation of the van't Hoff Equation (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Setting up the equilibrium condition (1 mark):** At osmotic equilibrium, the chemical potential of solvent on the pure side equals that on the solution side. $$\mu_w^{pure}(P) = \mu_w^{solution}(P + \pi)$$ **Step 2 — Writing the chemical potential expressions (2 marks):** $$\mu_w^{pure}(P) = \mu_w^{0}(P^{0}) + V_w(P - P^{0})$$ $$\mu_w^{solution}(P + \pi) = \mu_w^{0}(P^{0}) + V_w(P + \pi - P^{0}) + RT \ln x_w$$ **Step 3 — Equating and simplifying (2 marks):** $$V_w(P - P^{0}) = V_w(P + \pi - P^{0}) + RT \ln x_w$$ $$0 = V_w \pi + RT \ln x_w$$ $$V_w \pi = -RT \ln x_w$$ **Step 4 — Applying the dilute solution approximation (1 mark):** For dilute solutions, $\ln x_w = \ln(1 - x_s) \approx -x_s$, and $x_s \approx n_s/n_w$: $$V_w \pi = RT \cdot \frac{n_s}{n_w}$$ **Step 5 — Final expression (1 mark):** Since $V = n_w V_w$ in a dilute solution: $$\pi = \frac{n_s}{V} RT = c \, R \, T$$ For electrolytes: $\pi = i \, c \, R \, T$ **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Given Data (1 mark):** $M = 0.6$ mol/L, $T = 25 + 273 = 298$ K, $i = 2$ (NaCl fully dissociates), $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K) **Step 2 — Applying van't Hoff equation (1 mark):** $$\pi = i \, M \, R \, T = 2 \times 0.6 \times 0.0821 \times 298$$ **Step 3 — Calculation (2 marks):** $$2 \times 0.6 = 1.2$$ $$1.2 \times 0.0821 = 0.09852$$ $$0.09852 \times 298 = 29.36 \; \text{atm}$$ **Step 4 — Conversion to bar (1 mark):** $1 \; \text{atm} = 1.01325 \; \text{bar}$ $$\pi = 29.36 \times 1.01325 \approx 29.75 \; \text{bar}$$ **Step 5 — Minimum pressure for RO and energy comment (2 marks):** $$P_{min} > \pi \approx 29.75 \; \text{bar} \approx 30 \; \text{bar}$$ **Energy Implications (1 mark):** Operating at ≥ 30 bar requires substantial pumping power (≈ 3–6 kWh per m³ of freshwater produced). Hence, **seawater RO is energy-intensive**, justifying the global push toward energy-recovery devices and high-efficiency membranes. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified] **(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the principle, process, and mechanism of reverse osmosis. **(7 marks)** **(b)** List any **seven advantages** of reverse osmosis over conventional separation methods like distillation or ion exchange. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **(a) Principle, Process, and Mechanism of RO (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Definition and principle (2 marks):** Reverse osmosis is the pressure-driven flow of solvent through a semipermeable membrane from a concentrated solution to a dilute solution/pure solvent side, against the natural osmotic gradient, when the applied pressure exceeds the osmotic pressure of the feed. **Step 2 — Mechanism (Solution-Diffusion Model) (3 marks):** - **Sorption:** Water molecules dissolve into the dense polymer matrix of the membrane on the feed side. - **Diffusion:** Water molecules diffuse through the membrane down their chemical potential gradient. - **Desorption:** Water molecules are released on the permeate side as essentially pure solvent. The mathematical form is: $$J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$ $$J_s = B(C_f - C_p)$$ **Step 3 — Process description with reference to diagram (2 marks):** - Feed water is pressurized to 10–80 bar by a high-pressure pump. - It enters the RO module (typically a spiral-wound element). - Permeate (purified water) exits at low pressure. - Retentate (concentrate/brine) is discharged at high pressure. --- **(b) Seven Advantages of Reverse Osmosis (7 marks — 1 mark each)** 1. **Energy efficient** compared to thermal desalination (distillation). RO consumes 3–6 kWh/m³ vs. 10–15 kWh/m³ for multi-stage flash distillation. 2. **High rejection rate** of ions, organics, microorganisms, and pyrogens (> 99 % for salts in seawater RO). 3. **No phase change** is involved — water remains in the liquid state, avoiding latent heat losses. 4. **Modular and scalable** — capacity can be increased by simply adding more membrane modules. 5. **Continuous operation** with relatively simple control and minimal moving parts. 6. **Compact footprint** — occupies far less space than distillation columns of equivalent capacity. 7. **Environmentally cleaner** — no combustion of fossil fuels, no thermal pollution of effluent streams. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning** > - Students often *forget to draw a labeled diagram* in part (a). Drawing a **membrane with feed, permeate, and concentrate** arrows is *mandatory* and carries **2 marks by itself**. > - In part (b), listing **generic advantages** like "good quality water" will not fetch full marks. Use **numerical values or comparative terms** (e.g., "99 % salt rejection", "3–6 kWh/m³ energy") to demonstrate depth. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - **RO** = pressure-driven membrane separation; *opposite* of natural osmosis. - **Driving force condition:** $P_{applied} > \pi_{osmotic}$ - **van't Hoff equation (master equation):** $\pi = i \, M \, R \, T$ - **For non-electrolytes:** $i = 1$; for **NaCl:** $i = 2$; for **CaCl₂:** $i = 3$. - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 0.0821 \; \text{L·atm/(mol·K)} = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$ - **Membrane material:** Typically **thin-film composite (TFC) polyamide** on a polysulfone support. - **Water flux equation:** $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$ (Solution-Diffusion model). - **Salt flux equation:** $J_s = B(C_f - C_p)$. - **Salt rejection formula:** $R = 1 - C_p / C_f$ - **Recovery:** $Y = Q_p / Q_f \times 100 \; \%$ - **Seawater RO pressure:** 55–80 bar; **Brackish water RO pressure:** 10–25 bar. - **Energy consumption:** 3–6 kWh/m³ (modern SWRO with energy recovery). - **Key disadvantage:** Membrane fouling & scaling → requires pre-treatment and CIP. - **KTU hot topic:** Numerical problems on osmotic pressure + comparison tables between osmosis and reverse osmosis appear *every* semester. - **Mnemonic:** "**P**umps **P**ush **P**ermeate" — Pressure, Pump, Permeate. - **Common confusion:** RO ≠ Ultrafiltration. RO works at molecular/ionic level; UF works on suspended particles. - **Industrial symbol:** $Q_f = Q_p + Q_r$ (feed flow = permeate + retentate). > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU 2024 ESE** > Practice deriving the van't Hoff equation at least **three times** before the exam. Board examiners award full marks to students who **show every algebraic step explicitly** — never skip a single line in the derivation.

topics.title: Water disinfection methods: Chlorination, break point chlorination, ozone and UV irradiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water disinfection methods: Chlorination, break point chlorination, ozone and UV irradiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Water Disinfection?

**Water disinfection** is the deliberate, controlled process of eliminating, inactivating, or removing pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, protozoa, cysts) from drinking water to render it safe for human consumption and prevent water-borne diseases such as cholera, typhoid, and dysentery. Unlike **sterilization** (which destroys *all* microbial life), disinfection targets only the harmful, disease-causing fraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wordbank):**
> *Disinfection* is the partial destruction/chemical or physical inactivation of disease-producing microorganisms from water, achieved by adding chemical agents (chlorine, ozone) or by physical means (UV radiation), so that the water becomes microbiologically safe without producing adverse physiological effects in the consumer.

## 1.2 The Four Major Disinfection Methods Under KTU Syllabus

| Method | Category | Active Agent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Chlorination** | Chemical | HOCl / OCl⁻ / Cl₂ |
| **Break-Point Chlorination** | Chemical (advanced) | Free residual Cl₂ |
| **Ozonation** | Chemical (oxidative) | O₃ (ozone) |
| **UV Irradiation** | Physical | 254 nm photons |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Sanitizer"

Imagine you are about to drink from a glass that has been used by many people, and you want to make it safe. You have three options:

- **Chlorination (Add Bleach):** You pour a small measured amount of dilute bleach (HOCl) into the glass. It kills the germs chemically and continues to protect the water even after you've poured it (a "residual" shield).
- **Ozonation (Blast with Reactive Oxygen):** You pass a strong, short-lived oxidizing gas through the glass. It destroys germs powerfully and quickly, but once the gas escapes, the protection vanishes (no residual).
- **UV Irradiation (Hold under a UV Lamp):** You place the glass under a germicidal UV lamp. The radiation damages the germ's DNA so it cannot reproduce. No chemical is added, but no residual protection remains either.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Chlorine = "long-acting bodyguard", Ozone = "powerful but short-lived assassin", UV = "DNA-disabling flashlight". Each has engineering trade-offs that the next sections will dissect.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

- Public Health Engineering (Civil Engineering): Municipal water treatment plants in Kerala (e.g., Kerala Water Authority) routinely use these methods.
- Environmental Chemistry: Disinfection by-products (DBPs) like trihalomethanes (THMs) are a major research area.
- Biotechnology & Pharmacy: UV is the standard for sterilizing purified water in pharmaceutical manufacturing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Chlorination

### 2.1.1 Chemistry of Chlorine in Water

When chlorine gas (Cl₂) or its salts (bleach: NaOCl, bleaching powder: Ca(OCl)₂) are dissolved in water, they form an equilibrium mixture known as **free chlorine**:

$$Cl_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HOCl + H^+ + Cl^-$$

The hypochlorous acid (HOCl) then dissociates based on pH:

$$HOCl \rightleftharpoons H^+ + OCl^-$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HOCl is ~80–100 times more effective as a germicide than OCl⁻.** Hence chlorination is most effective at **pH < 7.5**, where HOCl dominates (pKa ≈ 7.5 at 25 °C).

### 2.1.2 Mechanism of Disinfection

HOCl is a small, electrically neutral molecule. It **diffuses through the bacterial cell wall** and oxidizes the enzyme systems (especially the sulfhydryl –SH groups of vital enzymes) and nucleic acids, causing cell death.

$$R\text{-}SH + HOCl \rightarrow R\text{-}S\text{-}Cl + H_2O$$

### 2.1.3 Forms of Chlorine Applied

| Form | Formula | Common Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Liquid Chlorine | Cl₂ | Large municipal plants |
| Bleaching Powder | Ca(OCl)₂ | Small communities, wells |
| Sodium Hypochlorite | NaOCl | Domestic/industrial |

## 2.2 Method 2 — Break-Point Chlorination

### 2.2.1 The Problem of Ammonia

Natural waters contain **ammonia (NH₃)** and ammonium ions (NH₄⁺) from organic decay, sewage, and agricultural runoff. When chlorine is added to such water, it does **not** form free residual chlorine immediately. Instead, it forms **chloramines** (combined chlorine):

$$NH_3 + HOCl \rightarrow NH_2Cl \text{ (monochloramine)} + H_2O$$

$$NH_2Cl + HOCl \rightarrow NHCl_2 \text{ (dichloramine)} + H_2O$$

$$NHCl_2 + HOCl \rightarrow NCl_3 \text{ (nitrogen trichloride)} + H_2O$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Chloramines are weak disinfectants** (about 25 times weaker than HOCl) and produce taste/odour problems. They are also persistent.

### 2.2.2 Definition of Break-Point

The **break-point** is the exact chlorine dose at which all the combined chlorine (chloramines) and ammonia have been completely oxidized, after which any further added chlorine appears as **free residual chlorine**.

The reactions beyond the break-point that destroy chloramines are:

$$2 NH_2Cl + HOCl \rightarrow N_2 \uparrow + 3 HCl + H_2O$$

$$NHCl_2 + HOCl \rightarrow NCl_3 + H_2O \text{ (then } NCl_3 \text{ hydrolyses)}$$

Overall destruction reaction at the break-point:

$$2 NH_3 + 3 Cl_2 \rightarrow N_2 \uparrow + 6 HCl$$

This is the **breakpoint reaction** in its simplest stoichiometric form.

## 2.3 Method 3 — Ozonation (O₃)

### 2.3.1 Generation of Ozone

Ozone is generated **on-site** (it cannot be stored) by passing dry air or oxygen through a high-voltage electric discharge (corona discharge), simulating the natural process during lightning:

$$3 O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{electric discharge}} 2 O_3 \quad \Delta H = +285 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### 2.3.2 Mechanism of Disinfection

Ozone is one of the **strongest oxidizing agents** known (oxidation potential = **+2.07 V**, higher than chlorine's +1.36 V). It kills microorganisms by:

1. **Direct oxidation** of the cell wall (lipids and proteins).
2. Generation of **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)** as it decomposes in water — these are even more reactive and attack intracellular DNA/enzymes.

$$O_3 + H_2O \rightarrow 2 \cdot OH + O_2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Fact:** Ozone is **unstable** (half-life ≈ 20 min in water). It provides **NO residual disinfection** in the distribution network. Hence ozonation is often paired with a small post-chlorine dose to maintain a residual.

## 2.4 Method 4 — UV Irradiation

### 2.4.1 Germicidal Wavelength

Ultraviolet light in the **UV-C band (200–280 nm)**, with peak germicidal action at **λ = 254 nm** (emitted by low-pressure mercury vapour lamps), is used.

### 2.4.2 Mechanism

UV photons at 254 nm are absorbed by the microbial DNA/RNA bases, particularly causing the formation of **pyrimidine dimers** (most commonly thymine dimers). These dimers distort the DNA double helix, preventing replication and transcription:

$$T + T \xrightarrow{\text{UV (254 nm)}} T\widehat{\;}T \text{ (cyclobutane thymine dimer)}$$

The organism becomes incapable of reproducing and thus dies out. UV is **physical**, adds **no chemicals**, and forms **no disinfection by-products (DBPs)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** UV is ineffective in turbid (cloudy) water because suspended particles block and scatter the photons. No residual is produced, so a secondary disinfectant may be needed downstream.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Reaction | Key Constant | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Chlorine hydrolysis | $Cl_2 + H_2O \rightarrow HOCl + H^+ + Cl^-$ | — | Initial Cl₂ chemistry |
| HOCl dissociation | $HOCl \rightleftharpoons H^+ + OCl^-$ | pKa $\approx$ 7.5 at 25 °C | pH optimization |
| Chloramine formation | $NH_3 + HOCl \rightarrow NH_2Cl + H_2O$ | — | Why combined Cl forms |
| Breakpoint stoichiometry | $2NH_3 + 3Cl_2 \rightarrow N_2 + 6HCl$ | — | Stoichiometric Cl₂ demand |
| Ozone formation | $3O_2 \rightarrow 2O_3$ | $\Delta H = +285$ kJ/mol | Generator design |
| Ozone redox potential | $O_3 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow O_2 + H_2O$ | $E^\circ = +2.07$ V | Comparison with Cl₂ |
| Chlorine redox potential | $HOCl + H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow Cl^- + H_2O$ | $E^\circ = +1.49$ V | Comparison with O₃ |
| UV germicidal peak | — | $\lambda = 254$ nm | Lamp specification |
| Typical dose (Cl₂) | — | **1 to 16 mg/L** | Municipal practice |
| Typical dose (O₃) | — | **0.5 to 2 mg/L** | Ozonation plants |
| Typical UV dose | — | **30 to 40 mJ/cm²** | Disinfection standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oxidation Power Order (descending):** $O_3 > HOCl > NH_2Cl > OCl^-$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Break-Point Chlorination Curve — Complete Analytical Walkthrough

The break-point chlorination phenomenon is best understood by plotting **residual chlorine (y-axis) vs. chlorine dose added (x-axis)** for a water sample containing a fixed amount of ammonia. The graph has **four distinct regions**.

### Step 1 — Identify the four regions

**(a) Region I — Initial Demand (Zone of Incomplete Reaction):**
As chlorine is added, free chlorine reacts with ammonia to form monochloramine. So almost all added chlorine is consumed. The residual is very low (combined chlorine, weak).

**(b) Region II — Monochloramine Peak:**
At a chlorine dose of roughly **5:1** Cl₂ : NH₃-N mass ratio (or ~7.6:1 molar ratio), monochloramine concentration reaches a maximum. Total residual chlorine peaks.

**(c) Region III — Chloramine Destruction / Dip:**
As more chlorine is added beyond the monochloramine peak, dichloramine and nitrogen trichloride form, which are unstable and **decompose** into N₂ gas, HCl, and water. The residual chlorine therefore **falls** to a minimum. This minimum is the **break-point**.

**(d) Region IV — Free Residual Chlorine Region:**
After the break-point, all ammonia has been oxidized to N₂. Any additional chlorine now appears as free residual chlorine (HOCl/OCl⁻) and the curve rises linearly with added dose.

### Step 2 — Stoichiometric Verification at the Break-Point

The theoretical chlorine demand to reach the break-point is given by the equation:

$$2 NH_3 + 3 Cl_2 \rightarrow N_2 \uparrow + 6 HCl$$

**Molar mass check:**

$$M(NH_3) = 14 + 3(1) = 17 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$M(Cl_2) = 2(35.5) = 71 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Molar ratio Cl₂ : NH₃** = $3 : 2$ = **1.5 : 1**

**Mass ratio Cl₂ : NH₃** = $(3 \times 71) : (2 \times 17) = 213 : 34 \approx$ **6.26 : 1**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** Some textbooks use the approximate **5:1 mass ratio** (or ~7.6:1 molar × 0.5 chlorine atoms). This is because of the **5 atoms of Cl consumed per 2 atoms of N** in the practical breakpoint mechanism (3 Cl₂ = 6 Cl atoms; 2 NH₃ = 2 N atoms). Therefore the **Cl-atom : N-atom ratio = 3:1** and the **Cl₂-molecule : NH₃-molecule ratio = 1.5:1**. Memorise: **Cl₂ demand = 1.5 × [NH₃-N as Cl₂ equivalent] + any other demand.**

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A water sample contains **2.0 mg/L** of ammonia (as NH₃-N). Calculate the theoretical chlorine dose (in mg/L) required to reach the break-point, ignoring other chlorine demands.

**Solution:**

From the breakpoint reaction:

$$2 NH_3 + 3 Cl_2 \rightarrow N_2 + 6 HCl$$

Step 1 — Calculate moles of NH₃ per litre:

$$n_{NH_3} = \frac{2.0 \text{ mg}}{17 \text{ g/mol}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g}}{17 \text{ g/mol}} = 1.176 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$

Step 2 — Apply the stoichiometric ratio 3 Cl₂ : 2 NH₃:

$$n_{Cl_2} = \frac{3}{2} \times n_{NH_3} = \frac{3}{2} \times 1.176 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$n_{Cl_2} = 1.765 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$

Step 3 — Convert to mg/L of Cl₂:

$$C_{Cl_2} = 1.765 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L} \times 71 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$C_{Cl_2} = 1.253 \times 10^{-2} \text{ g/L} = 12.53 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Answer:** $\approx$ **12.5 mg/L** of Cl₂ is the theoretical chlorine demand at the break-point. In practice, the engineer would dose about 1–2 mg/L **more** than this to maintain a free residual of 0.2–0.5 mg/L in the distribution network.

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix of the Four Methods

| Property | Chlorination | Break-Point Chlorination | Ozonation | UV Irradiation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Disinfectant form | HOCl / OCl⁻ | Free residual Cl₂ | O₃ (dissolved) | 254 nm photons |
| Oxidising power | Moderate | Moderate | Very high (E° = +2.07 V) | None (physical) |
| Effective pH range | 6.5 – 7.5 optimal | Same | 6.0 – 8.5 (broad) | Independent |
| Residual effect | **Yes** (long-lasting) | **Yes** (controlled) | **No** (transient) | **No** |
| Taste/odour issues | Possible (chlorine smell) | Minimised at B-P | None | None |
| By-products | THMs (carcinogenic) | Lower THMs | Bromate (regulated) | Essentially none |
| Contact time | 30 min minimum | 30 min minimum | 4 – 10 min | Seconds |
| Capital cost | Low | Low–Medium | **High** (generators) | Medium |
| Operational cost | Low | Low | High energy | Low–Medium (lamp replacement) |
| Turbidity tolerance | Moderate | Moderate | Low (O₃ demand rises) | **Very poor** |

## 3.3 The pKa Logic Behind Chlorine Speciation

The Henderson–Hasselbalch equation governs the HOCl/OCl⁻ distribution:

$$pH = pK_a + \log_{10} \frac{[OCl^-]}{[HOCl]}$$

With $pK_a = 7.5$ at 25 °C:

- At pH = 6.5: ratio = $10^{6.5 - 7.5} = 0.097$ → HOCl $\approx$ **91%** (Excellent)
- At pH = 7.5: ratio = $1.0$ → HOCl = 50%
- At pH = 8.5: ratio = $10^{1.0} = 10$ → HOCl $\approx$ **9%** (Poor)

This is why municipal plants operating in the slightly alkaline Kerala laterite-water conditions often dose **more chlorine** to compensate.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Break-Point Chlorination Curve (Mermaid Schematic)

The diagram below maps the four regions of the break-point chlorination curve with the underlying chemical transitions. (Note: a pure XY plot is rendered as a labelled block-flow to comply with the Mermaid restrictions on graph plots.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["REGION I: Initial Demand\nDose rises, residual stays low\nReaction: Cl2 + NH3 -> NH2Cl"]
    A1["REGION II: Monochloramine Peak\nResidual chlorine reaches MAX\nSpecies: NH2Cl dominant\nCl2:NH3 ratio about 5:1 mass"]
    A2["REGION III: Destruction Dip\nResidual falls to MINIMUM\nReaction: NH2Cl + HOCl -> N2 + HCl\nTHIS MIN = BREAK-POINT"]
    A3["REGION IV: Free Residual Zone\nResidual rises linearly\nSpecies: HOCl and OCl-\nResidual persists in network"]

    A0 -- add more Cl2 --> A1
    A1 -- add more Cl2 --> A2
    A2 -- add more Cl2 --> A3

    classDef zoneA fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef zoneB fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef zoneC fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef zoneD fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000
    class A0 zoneA
    class A1 zoneB
    class A2 zoneC
    class A3 zoneD
```

## 4.2 Comparative Architecture of the Four Disinfection Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RAW["Raw Water Inlet\ncontains pathogens, NH3, organics"]
    PREDIS["Pre-treatment\n(coagulation, filtration, pH adjustment)"]

    RAW --> PREDIS

    subgraph CHEM["CHEMICAL DISINFECTION ROUTES"]
        direction TB
        CHL["Path 1: Chlorination\nAdd Cl2 / NaOCl / CaOCl2\nForms HOCl / OCl-"]
        BPC["Path 2: Break-Point Cl\nDose exceeds Cl2 demand\nDestroys NH3 as N2 gas\nLeaves free residual"]
        OZN["Path 3: Ozonation\nCorona discharge generator\nO3 + .OH radical attack\nNO residual"]
    end

    subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL DISINFECTION ROUTE"]
        direction TB
        UVP["Path 4: UV Irradiation\nLow-pressure Hg lamp\nlambda equals 254 nm\nPyrimidine dimers in DNA\nNO residual"]
    end

    PREDIS --> CHL
    PREDIS --> BPC
    PREDIS --> OZN
    PREDIS --> UVP

    CHL --> RES1["Residual persists\nTHM formation risk"]
    BPC --> RES2["Residual persists\nMinimal taste and odour"]
    OZN --> RES3["No residual\nMay need post-Cl2 booster"]
    UVP --> RES4["No residual\nTurbidity blocks light"]

    RES1 --> NET["Safe Distribution Network"]
    RES2 --> NET
    RES3 --> NET
    RES4 --> NET
```

## 4.3 Process Flow of an Integrated Disinfection Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Source\nRiver / Well / Reservoir"]
    INTAKE["Intake and Screening"]
    COAG["Coagulation\nAlum or FeCl3"]
    FLOC["Flocculation and Sedimentation"]
    FILT["Rapid Sand Filtration"]
    DC1["Disinfection Step\nCHOOSE: Cl2 / O3 / UV"]
    CT["Clear Water Reservoir\nContact time check"]
    PH["pH Adjustment if required"]
    DIST["Pumped to Distribution Network"]

    SRC --> INTAKE --> COAG --> FLOC --> FILT --> DC1 --> CT --> PH --> DIST

    DC1 -.feedback loop.- CT
```
