# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: Distinguish between disinfection and sterilization. Why is chlorine considered a better disinfectant than ozone for a municipal water supply?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Disinfection | Sterilization |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Partial destruction / inactivation of pathogens | Complete destruction of **all** microbial life including spores |
| Example | Chlorination of drinking water | Autoclaving surgical instruments |

Chlorine is preferred over ozone for municipal supplies because:
1. It provides a **persistent residual** that protects water all the way to the consumer's tap, whereas ozone decomposes within minutes.
2. It is **cheaper** (liquid Cl₂ or bleaching powder vs. high-voltage ozone generators).
3. The technology is **simpler and well-established** in Indian municipalities including Kerala Water Authority.

*(3 marks — 1 for distinction table, 2 for valid reasons)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024 Scheme]`

**Q: What is the break-point of chlorination? Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The break-point is the chlorine dose beyond which any further addition of chlorine appears entirely as **free residual chlorine** (HOCl/OCl⁻), because all combined chlorine (chloramines) and ammonia have been oxidized to nitrogen gas.

**Significance:**
1. Ensures the water has **no chloramines**, which cause objectionable taste and odour.
2. Maintains a **disinfection residual** throughout the distribution system.
3. Reduces the formation of combined chlorine species that have lower germicidal efficiency.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Typical Module 4 Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with balanced chemical equations the chemistry of chlorination of water. Discuss the influence of **pH** on the relative proportions of HOCl and OCl⁻ and explain why a pH of around 7.0 is preferred for chlorination.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch of the break-point chlorination curve, describe the **four zones** formed when chlorine is added progressively to water containing ammonia. Write the **breakpoint reaction** in stoichiometric form.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Chemistry of Chlorination (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Dissolution and hydrolysis of Cl₂:**

$$Cl_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HOCl + H^+ + Cl^- \quad \text{(equation 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Dissociation of hypochlorous acid:**

$$HOCl \rightleftharpoons H^+ + OCl^- \quad \text{(equation 2)}$$

The relative proportions of HOCl and OCl⁻ are governed by the pH of water. From the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation:

$$pH = 7.5 + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[OCl^-]}{[HOCl]}\right)$$

- At **pH 6.0**: HOCl $\approx$ **97%**, OCl⁻ $\approx$ 3%
- At **pH 7.0**: HOCl $\approx$ **73%**, OCl⁻ $\approx$ 27%
- At **pH 7.5**: HOCl $\approx$ 50%, OCl⁻ $\approx$ 50%
- At **pH 8.5**: HOCl $\approx$ 9%, OCl⁻ $\approx$ 91%

HOCl is **80–100 times more effective** as a germicide than OCl⁻ because it is electrically neutral and small, so it diffuses easily through the negatively charged bacterial cell wall. Hence a pH of **6.5 to 7.5** is preferred; pH = 7.0 is a common engineering target.

**Mechanism of kill (extra 1 mark content):** HOCl oxidises the sulfhydryl (–SH) groups of vital bacterial enzymes, denaturing them:

$$R\text{-}SH + HOCl \rightarrow R\text{-}S\text{-}Cl + H_2O$$

*(7 marks — 2 for equations, 3 for pH table and reasoning, 1 for HOCl vs OCl⁻ activity comparison, 1 for mechanism)*

**(b) Break-Point Chlorination Curve (7 marks):**

The curve is plotted with **chlorine dose (mg/L)** on the X-axis and **residual chlorine (mg/L)** on the Y-axis. There are four distinct zones:

**Zone 1 — Initial Demand:** As chlorine is added, it reacts with ammonia to form **monochloramine (NH₂Cl)**. Almost all added chlorine is consumed; residual is very low.

**Zone 2 — Monochloramine Peak:** With more chlorine, NH₂Cl reaches maximum concentration. Total residual chlorine (combined) reaches a **local maximum**.

**Zone 3 — Destruction Dip:** Continued chlorine addition converts NH₂Cl → NHCl₂ → NCl₃, which decompose into N₂ gas, water, and HCl. Residual chlorine **drops sharply** to a minimum. This minimum is the **break-point**.

**Zone 4 — Free Residual Chlorine:** Beyond the break-point, all ammonia has been destroyed. Additional chlorine appears as free residual HOCl/OCl⁻, and the curve rises linearly.

**Stoichiometric breakpoint reaction:**

$$2 NH_3 + 3 Cl_2 \rightarrow N_2 \uparrow + 6 HCl$$

At the break-point, **1 mole of NH₃ consumes 1.5 moles of Cl₂**, i.e., the mass ratio is approximately **7.6 : 1 (molar)** or **5 : 1 by mass of Cl₂ atoms per N atom**.

*(7 marks — 2 for sketch description, 3 for four zones, 1 for breakpoint equation, 1 for stoichiometric ratio)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Typical Module 4 Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **ozonation** method of water disinfection. Give the equations for the **generation of ozone** and its **mode of action** on microorganisms. State two **advantages** and two **limitations** of ozonation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle and mechanism of **UV disinfection**. Mention the optimum wavelength, the chemical change in microbial DNA, and discuss its **advantages and limitations** compared to chlorination.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Ozonation (7 marks):**

**Generation of Ozone:** Ozone is produced at the treatment site by passing dry air or pure oxygen through a high-voltage alternating electric field (corona discharge) — the same process that creates the smell after lightning.

$$3 O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{corona discharge}} 2 O_3 \quad \Delta H = +285 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The generator consists of two electrodes separated by a dielectric insulator and a narrow air gap.

**Mode of Action:** Ozone is the **second strongest oxidising agent** (after fluorine) with $E^\circ = +2.07$ V. It inactivates microorganisms by:

1. **Direct oxidation of the cell wall** — O₃ attacks the double bonds in membrane lipids and proteins, causing lysis.
2. **Generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH)** when O₃ decomposes in water:

$$O_3 + H_2O \rightarrow 2\,\cdot OH + O_2$$

The hydroxyl radical is a powerful, non-selective oxidant that damages intracellular DNA, RNA, and enzymes.

**Two advantages:**
1. Extremely strong germicidal action — effective against viruses, bacteria, *Giardia*, and *Cryptosporidium* cysts that resist chlorination.
2. Improves taste, odour, and colour; no chlorinated by-products (THMs) are formed.

**Two limitations:**
1. **No residual effect** — ozone decomposes in 20–30 min, so water is vulnerable to recontamination in the distribution network. A post-chlorine dose is usually required.
2. **High capital and energy cost** — the corona discharge generators are expensive, and about 90% of input electrical energy is lost as heat.

*(7 marks — 2 for generation + equation, 3 for mode of action, 1 each for advantages and limitations)*

**(b) UV Disinfection (7 marks):**

**Principle:** Water is passed in a thin film past a mercury-vapour lamp that emits UV-C radiation. The photons at the **germicidal wavelength of 254 nm** are absorbed by the nucleic acids of microorganisms.

**Optimum Wavelength:** **λ = 254 nm** (low-pressure mercury lamp); sometimes 185 nm is also used to generate ozone in advanced systems.

**Mechanism (DNA Damage):** UV photons cause covalent bonding between **adjacent pyrimidine bases** (thymine–thymine or cytosine–thymine) on the same DNA strand, forming a **cyclobutane pyrimidine dimer (CPD)**:

$$T + T \xrightarrow{\text{UV at 254 nm}} T\widehat{\;}T \text{ (thymine dimer)}$$

The dimer distorts the DNA helix, blocks DNA polymerase, and prevents replication. The organism cannot reproduce and is effectively "neutralised" (not killed, but rendered non-viable).

**Advantages over Chlorination:**
1. **No chemical addition** — no taste, odour, or DBP formation (no THMs).
2. **Effective against chlorine-resistant protozoa** (*Cryptosporidium*, *Giardia*).
3. Simple operation, short contact time (seconds).

**Limitations:**
1. **No residual disinfection** — water is unprotected downstream.
2. **Turbidity and colour reduce efficiency** — suspended particles shadow the microbes from UV.
3. **Lamp replacement and quartz sleeve cleaning** are recurring maintenance issues.

*(7 marks — 1 for principle, 1 for wavelength, 2 for mechanism/dimer, 1.5 for advantages, 1.5 for limitations)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing disinfection with sterilization** — partial vs complete kill. Always state the partial nature of disinfection.
> 2. **Forgetting the pH dependence of HOCl/OCl⁻** — many students write the equation but never discuss the Henderson–Hasselbalch implication. KTU board questions frequently test this.
> 3. **Drawing the break-point curve without labelling the four zones** — examiners explicitly allocate marks for the sketch quality AND the four-zone description.
> 4. **Writing the breakpoint equation incorrectly as 1:1** — the correct ratio is **2 NH₃ : 3 Cl₂**. Examiners have reported this as the #1 mistake in valuation reviews.
> 5. **Claiming ozone produces a residual** — this is a critical conceptual error; ozone provides *no* residual disinfection.
> 6. **UV mechanism: writing "kills the bacteria"** — UV does not kill, it *inactivates* by preventing reproduction (DNA damage). Use the correct term.
> 7. **Units:** Express doses in **mg/L** (chlorine/ozone) or **mJ/cm²** (UV) — mixing units costs marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Disinfection ≠ Sterilization** — Disinfection removes pathogens; sterilization removes all life including spores.
- **HOCl is the active species in chlorination**, dominant at pH < 7.5; pH of 7.0 is the engineering optimum.
- **Four zones of the break-point curve:** (1) Initial demand, (2) Monochloramine peak, (3) Destruction dip = **break-point**, (4) Free residual.
- **Breakpoint stoichiometry:** $2NH_3 + 3Cl_2 \rightarrow N_2 + 6HCl$ — Cl₂ demand $\approx 7.6 \times$ [NH₃] (molar) or $\approx 5\times$ (mass ratio of Cl atoms : N atoms).
- **Ozone is the strongest oxidant** ($E^\circ = +2.07$ V), generated on-site, leaves **no residual**, acts via direct oxidation + •OH radical.
- **UV acts at 254 nm**, forms **thymine dimers**, leaves **no residual**, no chemicals added, no DBPs, but blocked by turbidity.
- **Oxidation power order:** $O_3 > HOCl > NH_2Cl > OCl^-$.
- **Cost order (cheapest to most expensive):** $Cl_2 < \text{Break-point Cl} < UV < O_3$.
- **Residual availability:** $Cl_2 = B-P \text{ Cl} = YES$ ; $O_3 = UV = NO$.
- **Standard doses:** Cl₂ $\approx$ **1–16 mg/L**; O₃ $\approx$ **0.5–2 mg/L**; UV $\approx$ **30–40 mJ/cm²**.
- **THMs (trihalomethanes)** are the major carcinogenic DBP of chlorination; ozonation and UV avoid this.
- **Hybrid plants:** Many modern plants use **O₃ + Cl₂** or **UV + Cl₂** to combine strong kill with persistent residual.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dissolved oxygen (DO): Estimation (Winkler's method)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dissolved oxygen (DO): Estimation (Winkler's method)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** is defined as the amount of molecular oxygen (O₂) dissolved in water, expressed in **mg/L (ppm)** or **mL/L**. It is one of the most vital parameters for assessing water quality, the degree of pollution, and the self-purification capacity of natural water bodies.

**Winkler's Method** is the classical, standard titrimetric (iodometric) procedure officially recognized for the quantitative estimation of dissolved oxygen in water samples. The method was developed by **Lajos Winkler (1888)** and remains a universally accepted reference technique in environmental and analytical chemistry laboratories.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** American Public Health Association (APHA), Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater, 23rd Edition, Method 4500-O B (Winkler Method).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a glass of cold water and a glass of warm water placed on a table. The cold water holds more "invisible air bubbles" of oxygen than the warm one. This invisible oxygen is the **dissolved oxygen (DO)** that fish, aquatic plants, and aerobic microorganisms depend on for survival. When water is polluted by organic waste, bacteria consume this oxygen rapidly, just like a crowd of people gulping air in a closed room.

**Winkler's method** is essentially a "chemical detective" that catches this invisible oxygen and turns it into a measurable amount of brown/yellow iodine, which we can then count precisely using titration. Think of it as a chain reaction:

1. First, we "trap" the dissolved O₂ using manganese (a metal "bait").
2. Then we "release" iodine proportional to the trapped oxygen.
3. Finally, we "count" this iodine using a standard titrant (sodium thiosulphate) — just like counting coins to know how much money (oxygen) was originally there.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Units of DO:** mg/L (most common), mL/L, or % saturation.
- **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand):** Indirectly related; high BOD ⇒ low DO.
- **Acceptable DO for aquatic life:** > **5 mg/L** (varies with species and temperature).
- **Standard normality of Na₂S₂O₃ used:** 0.025 N (or 0.0125 M).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Expect a 14-mark question combining DO estimation procedure + calculation using normality/molarity equivalence. The standard factor 1 mL of 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃ ≡ 0.2 mg O₂ is the most repeatedly tested value.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Chemical Principle

Winkler's method is a **redox titration (iodometry)** that operates on the principle of **indirect estimation**. Since O₂ is a colourless, gaseous molecule, it cannot be titrated directly. Hence, it is "fixed" in situ by Mn²⁺ ions in strongly alkaline conditions, and the resulting manganese hydroxide is then acidified in the presence of iodide (I⁻). The amount of iodine liberated is stoichiometrically equivalent to the original O₂ content, which is finally titrated against standard sodium thiosulphate using **starch as the indicator**.

### 2.2 The Five Sequential Chemical Reactions (Logic Steps)

**Step 1 — Formation of Manganous Hydroxide (White Precipitate):**
When MnSO₄ is added to the water sample followed by NaOH (or KOH) + NaI (or KI), a white precipitate of Mn(OH)₂ forms. This happens **before** the oxygen is "fixed" and indicates the alkaline environment is ready.

**Step 2 — Fixation of Dissolved Oxygen (Brown Precipitate):**
The dissolved O₂ rapidly oxidizes Mn(OH)₂ (Mn²⁺ state) to a higher oxidation state, forming brown-coloured **manganic hydroxide, MnO(OH)₂** (also called manganese oxyhydroxide; Mn is in +4 state in some references, but most commonly written as MnO(OH)₂ with Mn in +3).

**Step 3 — Acidification and Iodide Oxidation (Liberation of Iodine):**
On adding concentrated H₂SO₄, the brown precipitate dissolves and the higher manganese oxidizes iodide (I⁻) to free iodine (I₂), which imparts a yellow-brown colour to the solution. The manganese is simultaneously reduced back to Mn²⁺.

**Step 4 — Titration with Sodium Thiosulphate (The Counting Step):**
The liberated I₂ is titrated against a standard solution of sodium thiosulphate (Na₂S₂O₃) until a pale straw-yellow colour is observed. At this point, starch indicator is added, turning the solution **deep blue** (due to the starch-iodine complex).

**Step 5 — End Point Detection:**
Titration is continued dropwise until the blue colour just disappears, leaving a colourless solution. The volume of thiosulphate consumed is stoichiometrically equivalent to the iodine, and hence to the original dissolved oxygen.

### 2.3 Balanced Chemical Equations

$$2MnSO_4 + 4NaOH \longrightarrow 2Mn(OH)_2\downarrow \text{ (white)} + 2Na_2SO_4$$

$$2Mn(OH)_2 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2MnO(OH)_2\downarrow \text{ (brown)}$$

$$MnO(OH)_2 + 2KI + 2H_2SO_4 \longrightarrow MnSO_4 + I_2 + K_2SO_4 + 3H_2O$$

$$I_2 + 2Na_2S_2O_3 \longrightarrow 2NaI + Na_2S_4O_6$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometric Chain (Exam Critical):**
> 1 mole O₂ ≡ 2 moles MnO(OH)₂ ≡ 2 moles I₂ ≡ **4 moles Na₂S₂O₃**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Use | Unit |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|------|
| 1 | $1\,\text{mL of } 0.025\,\text{N Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \equiv 0.2\,\text{mg O}_2$ | Standard equivalence factor (most important) | mg/mL |
| 2 | $\text{DO (mg/L)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s}$ | General formula, where V = titre value, N = normality, V_s = sample volume | mg/L |
| 3 | $\text{DO (mg/L)} = \dfrac{V \times 0.2 \times 1000}{V_s}$ | Direct formula using 0.025 N thiosulphate | mg/L |
| 4 | $\text{BOD (mg/L)} = DO_i - DO_f$ | Biochemical Oxygen Demand after 5 days at 20 °C | mg/L |
| 5 | $\% \text{ Saturation} = \dfrac{DO_{\text{observed}}}{DO_{\text{saturation at that T}}} \times 100$ | Indicates oxygen availability | % |

> **Note on pipes:** In all KTU exam scripts, absolute value is written as $\vert x \vert$ (using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal Water Treatment:** Monitors aeration tank efficiency.
- **Aquaculture & Fisheries:** Ensures healthy fish survival (DO > 5 mg/L).
- **Industrial Effluent Monitoring:** Detects oxygen depletion by organic pollutants.
- **River & Lake Health Studies:** Foundation for **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** calculation, a statutory parameter under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Factor (0.2 mg O₂ per mL of 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃)

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU exams. We shall derive it from the molecular weight of O₂ and the normality of thiosulphate.

**Step 1: Establish the equivalent weight of O₂.**

The oxidation state of O changes from 0 (in O₂) to −2 (in MnO(OH)₂, where oxygen is in oxide form). Thus each oxygen atom gains 2 electrons. Since O₂ has 2 atoms:

$$\text{n-factor of O}_2 = 2 \times 2 = 4$$

$$\text{Equivalent weight of O}_2 = \dfrac{\text{Molecular weight}}{4} = \dfrac{32}{4} = 8\,\text{g/eq}$$

**Step 2: Recall the definition of normality in terms of equivalents.**

$$N = \dfrac{\text{Number of equivalents of solute}}{\text{Volume of solution in litres}}$$

$$\text{Number of equivalents} = N \times V \text{ (in L)}$$

For **1 mL of 0.025 N** Na₂S₂O₃:

$$\text{Equivalents of Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \text{ in 1 mL} = 0.025 \times \dfrac{1}{1000} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{eq}$$

**Step 3: Apply stoichiometric equivalence (1 mole O₂ ≡ 4 moles Na₂S₂O₃).**

In terms of equivalents, the number of equivalents of O₂ fixed = number of equivalents of Na₂S₂O₃ consumed. So:

$$\text{Equivalents of O}_2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4: Convert equivalents of O₂ to mass of O₂ in mg.**

$$\text{Mass (g)} = \text{Equivalents} \times \text{Equivalent weight}$$

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 8 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{g}$$

**Step 5: Convert grams to milligrams.**

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 = 0.2\,\text{mg}$$

**Conclusion:** Therefore, every 1 mL of 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃ solution used in titration corresponds exactly to **0.2 mg of dissolved oxygen** fixed by the sample.

$$\boxed{1\,\text{mL of } 0.025\,\text{N Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \equiv 0.2\,\text{mg O}_2}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Interfering Substances and Modifications

| Interfering Ion / Substance | Effect on DO Estimation | Modification / Remedy |
|------------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| NO₂⁻ (Nitrite) | Gives falsely high DO (oxidizes I⁻ to I₂) | Add **sodium azide (NaN₃)** — Azide Modification |
| Fe³⁺ | Oxidizes I⁻ to I₂ → positive error | Add **KF or K₂C₂O₄** (fluoride/oxalate masks Fe) |
| SO₃²⁻, S²⁻ | Reduce I₂ → negative error | Pre-oxidize with Br₂ water or KMnO₄ |
| Organic matter | May consume I₂ | Use Al(OH)₃ coagulation to clarify |
| Suspended solids | Cause turbidity issues | Settle/filter before analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Relevance:** When the question mentions "azide modification," it specifically refers to NO₂⁻ interference removal — high-probability 3-mark sub-question.

### 3.3 Detailed Sample Calculation (Model Numerical)

**Problem (KTU Style):**
A 250 mL water sample was analysed for DO by Winkler's method. The titration of liberated I₂ required 8.5 mL of 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃. Calculate the DO in mg/L.

**Solution using Direct Formula:**

$$\text{DO (mg/L)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{titre}} \times 0.2 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$

$$\text{DO (mg/L)} = \dfrac{8.5 \times 0.2 \times 1000}{250}$$

$$\text{DO (mg/L)} = \dfrac{1700}{250} = 6.8\,\text{mg/L}$$

**Verification using General Formula:**

$$\text{DO} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s} = \dfrac{8.5 \times 0.025 \times 8 \times 1000}{250}$$

$$\text{DO} = \dfrac{1700}{250} = 6.8\,\text{mg/L} \quad \checkmark$$

**Inference:** DO of 6.8 mg/L indicates a reasonably well-oxygenated, healthy water body (above 5 mg/L threshold for aquatic life).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Winkler's Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Sample Collection in BOD Bottle] --> B[Add 1 mL MnSO4 Solution]
    B --> C[Add 1 mL Alkaline KI NaOH Reagent]
    C --> D[Shake Well - White Precipitate Mn OH 2 Forms]
    D --> E[Wait for Brown Precipitate MnO OH 2 to Settle - O2 Fixed]
    E --> F[Add 2 mL Concentrated H2SO4 - Acidification]
    F --> G[Shake - Brown Dissolves - I2 Liberated - Yellow Brown Colour]
    G --> H[Pipette 250 mL Aliquot into Conical Flask]
    H --> I[Titrate against 0.025 N Na2S2O3 until Pale Straw Yellow]
    I --> J[Add 1 mL Starch Indicator - Solution Turns Deep Blue]
    J --> K[Continue Titration Dropwise till Blue Colour Disappears]
    K --> L[Note the Burette Reading - Titre Value V mL]
    L --> M[Calculate DO using Formula DO equals V times 0.2 times 1000 divided by V sample]
    M --> N[Report Result in mg per L]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style G fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style J fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c
    style K fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

### 4.2 Reaction Pathway Schematic (Chain of Redox Conversions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1 Fixation]
        A1[Mn2 plus MnSO4] --> A2[Mn OH 2 White PPT]
        A2 --> A3[MnO OH 2 Brown PPT after O2 Fixation]
    end

    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2 Liberation]
        A3 --> B1[Plus KI plus H2SO4]
        B1 --> B2[I2 Liberated plus Mn2 plus H2O]
    end

    subgraph Stage3[Stage 3 Titration]
        B2 --> C1[Plus Na2S2O3]
        C1 --> C2[NaI plus Na2S4O6]
        C2 --> C3[Starch Blue Disappears End Point]
    end

    style A2 fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000
    style A3 fill:#8d6e63,stroke:#3e2723,color:#fff
    style B2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style C3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

### 4.3 Stoichiometric Chain Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[1 mole O2] -->|2 electrons x 4| P2[2 moles MnO OH 2]
    P2 -->|Redox with I minus| P3[2 moles I2]
    P3 -->|Iodometry| P4[4 moles Na2S2O3]
    P4 -->|Using 0.025 N| P5[16000 mL of 0.025 N thiosulphate]

    style P1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style P5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Dissolved Oxygen (DO). Why is it considered an important parameter in water quality analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
Dissolved Oxygen (DO) refers to the amount of molecular oxygen (O₂) physically dissolved in water, typically expressed in mg/L or mL/L. It is a critical water quality parameter because (i) it sustains aquatic life — fish and aerobic microorganisms require a minimum of 4–6 mg/L for survival, (ii) it indicates the degree of organic pollution — high pollution consumes DO rapidly, (iii) it determines the self-purification capacity of rivers and lakes, and (iv) it forms the basis for calculating the Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD), a statutory parameter under water pollution control regulations.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Name the indicator used in Winkler's method. What is the colour change observed at the end point?**

**Model Answer:**
The indicator used in Winkler's method is **freshly prepared starch solution (1%)**. It forms an intense **deep blue** complex with free iodine (I₂ + starch → blue starch-iodine complex). At the end point, when all the liberated iodine is reduced to iodide by sodium thiosulphate, the blue colour **disappears sharply**, leaving a colourless solution. This marks the completion of the titration.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **Winkler's iodometric method** for the estimation of dissolved oxygen in water, with all balanced chemical equations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 300 mL water sample consumed 9.2 mL of 0.025 N sodium thiosulphate during Winkler titration. Calculate the DO in mg/L and comment on the water quality. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure and Equations:**

1. **Sample Collection:** Collect the water sample in a **300 mL BOD bottle** carefully, ensuring no air bubbles are trapped. Add 1 mL each of MnSO₄ solution and **Alkaline-Iodide-Azide** reagent.
2. **Fixation of O₂:** Mn²⁺ reacts with OH⁻ to form white Mn(OH)₂, which is then oxidized by dissolved O₂ to brown manganic hydroxide MnO(OH)₂.
3. **Acidification:** Add 2 mL of concentrated H₂SO₄; the brown precipitate dissolves and liberates free I₂ in proportion to the oxygen fixed.
4. **Titration:** Take 250 mL aliquot and titrate against 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃ till pale straw yellow. Add starch indicator → deep blue → continue titration → blue disappears at the end point.

**Equations:**

$$2MnSO_4 + 4NaOH \rightarrow 2Mn(OH)_2\downarrow + 2Na_2SO_4$$

$$2Mn(OH)_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2MnO(OH)_2\downarrow$$

$$MnO(OH)_2 + 2KI + 2H_2SO_4 \rightarrow MnSO_4 + I_2 + K_2SO_4 + 3H_2O$$

$$I_2 + 2Na_2S_2O_3 \rightarrow 2NaI + Na_2S_4O_6$$

**(b) Calculation:**

$$\text{DO (mg/L)} = \dfrac{V \times 0.2 \times 1000}{V_s} = \dfrac{9.2 \times 0.2 \times 1000}{300}$$

$$\text{DO} = \dfrac{1840}{300} = 6.13\,\text{mg/L}$$

**Comment:** DO of 6.13 mg/L is well above the minimum threshold of 5 mg/L, indicating a **healthy, well-oxygenated water body** suitable for aquatic life.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative Module-Internal Option)**

**(a)** Explain the **principle and stoichiometry** of Winkler's method. Derive the relation: 1 mL of 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃ ≡ 0.2 mg O₂. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **modifications** of Winkler's method for removing interferences from nitrite and ferric ions. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle and Derivation:**

*Principle:* Winkler's method is an **indirect redox titration (iodometry)**. Dissolved O₂ is fixed by Mn²⁺ in alkaline medium as MnO(OH)₂. On acidification in presence of I⁻, free I₂ is liberated stoichiometrically equivalent to O₂. This I₂ is titrated with standard Na₂S₂O₃.

*Derivation:* As shown in **Section 3.1** above.

$$\text{n-factor of O}_2 = 4;\quad \text{Equivalent weight} = 32/4 = 8$$

$$1\,\text{mL of 0.025 N Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 = 0.025 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000\,\text{meq} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{eq}$$

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 8 = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{g} = 0.2\,\text{mg} \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Modifications:**

| Interference | Effect | Modification | Reagent Used |
|--------------|--------|---------------|--------------|
| NO₂⁻ | Falsely high DO (oxidizes I⁻) | **Azide Modification** | Sodium azide (NaN₃) destroys NO₂⁻ |
| Fe³⁺ | Falsely high DO (oxidizes I⁻) | **Fluoride / Oxalate Modification** | KF or K₂C₂O₄ complexes Fe³⁺ |

In the **Azide Modification**, sodium azide is added along with the alkaline-iodide reagent. It preferentially reacts with nitrite, converting it to harmless N₂ and N₂O, preventing the spurious liberation of I₂.

$$2NaN_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow 2HN_3 + Na_2SO_4$$

$$\text{NO}_2^- + HN_3 \rightarrow N_2 + N_2O + OH^-$$

In the **Fluoride Modification**, KF complexes Fe³⁺ as a stable colourless complex $[\text{FeF}_6]^{3-}$, preventing it from oxidizing iodide.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Winkler's Method Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 1000:** When using the formula $\dfrac{V \times 0.2}{V_s}$, students often forget to multiply by 1000 to convert mL basis to L basis. Marks lost: **2**.
> 2. **Confusing Mn(OH)₂ with MnO₂:** The brown precipitate is **MnO(OH)₂ (manganic hydroxide)**, NOT MnO₂. Writing MnO₂ will be marked wrong.
> 3. **Starch indicator timing:** Starch must be added **near the end point** (pale straw yellow), not at the beginning. Adding it earlier forms an irreversible complex and gives a false end point.
> 4. **No air bubbles in BOD bottle:** Trapped air gives falsely high readings. Always note the need for bubble-free sampling.
> 5. **Mixing normality terms:** Use N (normality) for thiosulphate not M (molarity) unless the question specifies. For 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃, the molarity is 0.0125 M because n-factor = 2 (each S₂O₃²⁻ donates 2 electrons).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **DO** = O₂ dissolved in water; unit mg/L; healthy threshold > 5 mg/L.
- ✅ **Winkler's method** = indirect iodometric titration; the **only classical standard** for DO.
- ✅ **Five steps:** MnSO₄ → Alkaline KI/NaOH → H₂SO₄ → Na₂S₂O₃ titration → Starch indicator.
- ✅ **Brown precipitate** = MnO(OH)₂ (manganic hydroxide, NOT MnO₂).
- ✅ **Starch** = indicator; blue → colourless at end point; add near the end point only.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric chain:** 1 mole O₂ → 2 moles MnO(OH)₂ → 2 moles I₂ → 4 moles Na₂S₂O₃.
- ✅ **Magic factor:** 1 mL of 0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃ ≡ 0.2 mg O₂.
- ✅ **Formula (Direct):** $\text{DO (mg/L)} = \dfrac{V \times 0.2 \times 1000}{V_s}$.
- ✅ **Formula (General):** $\text{DO (mg/L)} = \dfrac{V \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_s}$.
- ✅ **Equivalent weight of O₂ = 8** (since n-factor = 4).
- ✅ **Interferences:** NO₂⁻ (use azide modification), Fe³⁺ (use fluoride modification).
- ✅ **DO ↔ BOD link:** BOD₅ = DO_initial − DO_final (incubated for 5 days at 20 °C).
- ✅ **BOD bottle** must be **air-bubble free**; sampling technique is critical.
- ✅ **Normality of Na₂S₂O₃ = 0.025 N** is the industry/KTU standard.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** A 14-mark question always combines theory (procedure + equations) + numerical calculation. Master the 0.2 mg factor and the formula structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BOD and COD: Definition, estimation and significance (numericals)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR LIFE SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** BOD and COD: Definition, estimation and significance (numericals)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) is the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically decompose the organic matter present in a water sample under controlled conditions of temperature (20°C) and time (5 days). It is denoted as **BOD₅**.*

**Units:** mg/L (or ppm) of O₂ consumed.

**Standard Conditions:**
- Temperature: **20°C**
- Time: **5 days**
- Darkness (to prevent photosynthesis-driven O₂ generation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Fish Tank Hunger Test":**
> Imagine you put a sealed fish tank full of dirty pond water in a dark room for 5 days. The bacteria living in the water will start "eating" the organic waste (food particles, dead leaves, sewage). As they eat, they consume the dissolved oxygen in the water. BOD simply measures **how much oxygen the bacteria "drank"** to finish their meal. The higher the BOD, the more polluted (and oxygen-starved) the water is. Fish cannot survive in such water because all the oxygen gets used up by decomposers!

---

## 1.2 Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) is the amount of oxygen required to chemically oxidize the biodegradable and non-biodegradable organic matter in a water sample using a strong chemical oxidizing agent, typically **potassium dichromate (K₂Cr₂O₇)** in a strongly acidic medium (H₂SO₄).*

**Units:** mg/L (or ppm) of O₂ consumed.

**Key Relationships:**
- **COD > BOD** (always, because COD oxidizes both biodegradable AND non-biodegradable matter; BOD only oxidizes biodegradable matter).
- COD can be determined in **2–3 hours** (much faster than BOD, which takes 5 days).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Industrial Burner Test":**
> If BOD is the bacteria slowly nibbling on the organic waste, then COD is like tossing the same waste into a high-temperature industrial furnace with a strong acid. Whatever the furnace burns (both easy-to-burn AND hard-to-burn waste) is counted in COD. BOD only counts what the gentle "bacterial stomach" can digest. So COD is the **total fuel value** of pollution, while BOD is the **biologically digestible portion** of that fuel.

---

## 1.3 Significance — Why Do Engineers Care?

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Topic Matters in Engineering Practice:**
> 1. **Drinking Water Safety:** BIS/WHO permissible BOD limit for drinking water is **< 2 mg/L**; for sewage it is typically **< 30 mg/L** (after treatment).
> 2. **Eutrophication Control:** High BOD in lakes depletes dissolved O₂, killing aquatic life.
> 3. **Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) Design:** Aeration tank capacity is directly calculated from incoming BOD load (kg BOD/day).
> 4. **Industrial Effluent Monitoring:** COD is used to track industrial discharge (e.g., textile, pharma, food processing industries).
> 5. **Regulatory Compliance:** Pollution Control Boards mandate BOD/COD limits for effluent discharge.

**Standard Reference Values (mg/L O₂):**

| Water Type | BOD₅ | COD |
|---|---|---|
| Pure drinking water | < 1 | < 5 |
| River water (clean) | 1 – 3 | 5 – 20 |
| Municipal sewage (raw) | 100 – 400 | 200 – 800 |
| Industrial effluent | 500 – 5000+ | 1000 – 10000+ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BOD — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

**The Underlying Chemistry (Logical Steps):**

1. **Organic matter in water** (e.g., C₆H₁₂O₆) is metabolized by **aerobic bacteria**.
2. Bacteria use the organic carbon as food, and **dissolved O₂** as the oxidizer.
3. The general oxidation reaction (glucose example):
   $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{bacteria}} 6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{energy}$$
4. The **oxygen consumed** in this process is the BOD.
5. If a sample has very high organic content, the DO depletes **before 5 days**. Hence the **dilution method** is used to keep a measurable amount of DO remaining.

### **BOD Dilution Method — Logical Procedure:**

1. Take a measured volume of water sample.
2. Dilute it with **oxygen-saturated dilution water** (containing phosphate buffer, MgSO₄, CaCl₂, FeCl₃ nutrients).
3. If the sample lacks bacteria, **seed** it (add a small amount of settled domestic sewage).
4. Measure **Initial DO (D₁)** of the diluted sample using Winkler's method.
5. Seal the bottle and incubate at **20°C in darkness for 5 days**.
6. Measure **Final DO (D₂)** after 5 days.
7. Calculate BOD from the DO drop, corrected for seed respiration.

### **BOD Formula (Unseeded — Most Common for KTU Problems):**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) \times \text{Dilution Factor (DF)}}{P}$$

- $D_1$ = Initial DO of diluted sample (mg/L)
- $D_2$ = Final DO after 5 days of diluted sample (mg/L)
- $P$ = Decimal fraction of sample in diluted sample (volume of sample ÷ total volume)
- **DF = 1/P** (so the formula is also: $\text{BOD}_5 = (D_1 - D_2) \times \text{DF}$)

### **BOD Formula (Seeded — For Samples Lacking Microorganisms):**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{[(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f]}{P}$$

- $B_1$ = DO of seed control before incubation
- $B_2$ = DO of seed control after 5 days
- $f$ = Ratio of seed volume in diluted sample to seed volume in seed control

---

## 2.2 COD — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

**The Underlying Chemistry (Logical Steps):**

1. A known excess volume of standard **K₂Cr₂O₇** is added to a measured volume of water sample.
2. Concentrated **H₂SO₄** is added (to create a strongly acidic reflux medium) along with **Ag₂SO₄** catalyst (to oxidize straight-chain organics) and **HgSO₄** (to prevent chloride interference).
3. The mixture is **refluxed for 2 hours** at ~150°C.
4. Organic matter is oxidized:
   $$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Cr}^{3+}$$
5. **Excess (unreacted) dichromate** is then back-titrated with **Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate (FAS)** indicator using **ferroin** as the indicator.
6. The **difference in FAS volume** between blank and sample gives the dichromate consumed, from which COD is calculated.

### **COD Formula:**

$$\text{COD (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$

- $V_b$ = Volume of FAS used for **blank** titration (mL)
- $V_s$ = Volume of FAS used for **sample** titration (mL)
- $N$ = Normality of FAS solution
- $V_{\text{sample}}$ = Volume of water sample taken (mL)
- $8000$ = $\dfrac{8 \times 10^3}{1}$ (8 is the equivalent weight of O₂, multiplied by 1000 to convert to mg)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "8000" Magic Number — Why It Appears:**
> Equivalent weight of O₂ = Molar mass / n-factor = 32 / 4 = **8**.
> When we multiply 8 by 1000 (to convert grams → mg), we get 8000.
> This 8000 directly converts the chemical stoichiometry of the redox reaction into "mg of O₂ per litre" units — the universally accepted reporting unit for BOD/COD.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sanitary Engineers** use BOD to size aeration tanks: Aeration Time = $\dfrac{\text{BOD load (kg/day)} \times \text{F/M ratio}}{\text{MLVSS}}$
- **Environmental Auditors** use COD/BOD ratio to determine **biodegradability**:
  $$\text{BOD/COD} > 0.5 \rightarrow \text{Highly biodegradable (suitable for biological treatment)}$$
  $$\text{BOD/COD} < 0.3 \rightarrow \text{Poorly biodegradable (needs chemical/advanced treatment)}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| BOD₅ (unseeded) | $(D_1 - D_2) \div P$ | mg/L | $P$ = sample fraction |
| BOD₅ (seeded) | $\{[D_1 - D_2] - [B_1 - B_2] \cdot f\} \div P$ | mg/L | $f$ = seed ratio |
| Dilution Factor | $V_{\text{sample}} \div V_{\text{blank dilution}}$ | dimensionless | Often 50, 100, 200 |
| COD (Dichromate) | $(V_b - V_s) \cdot N \cdot 8000 \div V_{\text{sample}}$ | mg/L | 8000 = 8 × 1000 |
| Equivalent weight of O₂ | $32 \div 4 = 8$ | g/eq | Used in COD/BOD math |
| BOD/COD ratio | $\text{BOD}_5 \div \text{COD}$ | dimensionless | Biodegradability index |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Numericals)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Work-Show Mandate:** Every algebraic step and every numerical substitution is explicitly shown. No shortcut logic.

---

## Numerical 1 — BOD by Unseeded Dilution Method

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**Problem Statement:**
A 5 mL water sample is diluted to 300 mL with aerated dilution water. The initial DO of the diluted sample is 7.8 mg/L. After 5 days of incubation at 20°C, the DO is 4.0 mg/L. Calculate the BOD₅ of the original sample.

### **Step 1 — Identify the given data**

$$V_{\text{sample}} = 5 \text{ mL}, \quad V_{\text{diluted total}} = 300 \text{ mL}$$
$$D_1 = 7.8 \text{ mg/L}, \quad D_2 = 4.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

### **Step 2 — Calculate the decimal fraction (P) of sample in diluted sample**

P is the **decimal fraction** of the original sample present in the total diluted volume.

$$P = \frac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{diluted total}}}$$

Substituting values:

$$P = \frac{5}{300}$$

$$P = 0.01667$$

### **Step 3 — Apply the BOD formula**

For an unseeded sample:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2)}{P}$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(7.8 - 4.0)}{0.01667}$$

### **Step 4 — Numerator calculation**

$$D_1 - D_2 = 7.8 - 4.0 = 3.8 \text{ mg/L}$$

### **Step 5 — Final division**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{3.8}{0.01667}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 228.0 \text{ mg/L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Cross-Check (Using Dilution Factor approach):**
> Dilution Factor = 1/P = 300/5 = 60
> BOD = (7.8 − 4.0) × 60 = 3.8 × 60 = **228 mg/L** ✓ (Same result.)

---

## Numerical 2 — BOD with Seeded Sample

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Problem Statement:**
A 10 mL industrial wastewater sample is mixed with 290 mL dilution water and seeded with 2 mL of settled sewage. The initial DO of the diluted sample (D₁) is 8.2 mg/L; after 5 days (D₂) it is 3.4 mg/L. The seed control has DO (B₁) = 8.0 mg/L initially and (B₂) = 7.4 mg/L after 5 days. The seed control was made with 5 mL seed in 300 mL dilution water. Calculate BOD₅.

### **Step 1 — Compute the dilution decimal fraction (P) for the sample**

$$P = \frac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{diluted total}}}$$

$$P = \frac{10}{300} = 0.0333$$

### **Step 2 — Compute the seed ratio (f)**

f is the **ratio of seed volume in the diluted sample** to **seed volume in the seed control bottle**.

$$f = \frac{\text{Seed in diluted sample}}{\text{Seed in seed control}} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

### **Step 3 — Compute the seed correction term**

$$\text{Seed correction} = (B_1 - B_2) \times f$$

$$(B_1 - B_2) = 8.0 - 7.4 = 0.6 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{Seed correction} = 0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.24 \text{ mg/L}$$

### **Step 4 — Apply the seeded BOD formula**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{[(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f]}{P}$$

### **Step 5 — Numerator calculation**

$$D_1 - D_2 = 8.2 - 3.4 = 4.8 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$4.8 - 0.24 = 4.56 \text{ mg/L}$$

### **Step 6 — Final division**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{4.56}{0.0333}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 136.9 \text{ mg/L}}$$

---

## Numerical 3 — COD by Dichromate Reflux Method

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Question]**

**Problem Statement:**
25 mL of a wastewater sample is refluxed with 10 mL of standard 0.25 N K₂Cr₂O₇. After refluxing, the excess dichromate requires 7.2 mL of 0.1 N Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate (FAS). A blank titration (with distilled water in place of sample) requires 12.0 mL of the same 0.1 N FAS. Calculate the COD of the sample.

### **Step 1 — Identify given data**

$$V_b = 12.0 \text{ mL (blank)}, \quad V_s = 7.2 \text{ mL (sample)}$$
$$N = 0.1 \text{ N (FAS normality)}$$
$$V_{\text{sample}} = 25 \text{ mL}$$

### **Step 2 — Apply the COD formula**

$$\text{COD (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$

### **Step 3 — Subtract blank and sample FAS volumes**

$$V_b - V_s = 12.0 - 7.2 = 4.8 \text{ mL}$$

This 4.8 mL of FAS corresponds to the dichromate that was actually **consumed by the organic matter** in the sample.

### **Step 4 — Multiply by N and 8000**

$$4.8 \times 0.1 = 0.48$$

$$0.48 \times 8000 = 3840$$

### **Step 5 — Divide by sample volume**

$$\text{COD} = \frac{3840}{25}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COD} = 153.6 \text{ mg/L}}$$

---

## Numerical 4 — Combined BOD & COD Biodegradability Assessment

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**Problem Statement:**
A textile mill effluent has BOD₅ = 280 mg/L and COD = 1120 mg/L. Comment on its biodegradability and estimate the approximate amount of non-biodegradable organic load (in terms of COD equivalent) in the effluent.

### **Step 1 — Compute the BOD/COD ratio**

$$\frac{\text{BOD}}{\text{COD}} = \frac{280}{1120} = 0.25$$

### **Step 2 — Interpretation**

Since the BOD/COD ratio is **less than 0.3**, the effluent is **poorly biodegradable**.

This means conventional biological treatment (e.g., activated sludge) alone will not adequately treat this wastewater. **Advanced treatment** (e.g., chemical oxidation, Fenton process, adsorption) is required.

### **Step 3 — Estimate non-biodegradable COD fraction**

Non-biodegradable COD ≈ COD − BOD (approximation, since BOD measures only biodegradable organics).

$$\text{Non-biodegradable COD} = 1120 - 280 = 840 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Non-biodegradable organic load} = 840 \text{ mg/L as COD}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is a **classic KTU conceptual question**. The BOD/COD ratio is the "biodegradability thermometer" for any wastewater. Ratios < 0.3 typically indicate the presence of recalcitrant organics (e.g., dyes, phenols, pesticides) common in textile, petrochemical, and pharmaceutical effluents.

---

## Numerical 5 — Determining Dilution for a Strong Sample

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question for Practice]**

**Problem Statement:**
The expected BOD₅ of a strong industrial effluent is approximately 3000 mg/L. If the minimum initial DO of the dilution water is 8.0 mg/L and the DO after 5 days should not drop below 2.0 mg/L, calculate the minimum dilution factor required.

### **Step 1 — Apply the BOD formula rearranged for DF**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = (D_1 - D_2) \times \text{DF}$$

Rearranging:

$$\text{DF} = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{D_1 - D_2}$$

### **Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$D_1 - D_2 = 8.0 - 2.0 = 6.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{DF} = \frac{3000}{6.0} = 500$$

### **Step 3 — Interpret the result**

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum Dilution Factor required} = 500}$$

This means for every 1 mL of sample, we must add 499 mL of dilution water (total 500 mL) so that oxygen is not fully depleted before 5 days.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q: Define BOD₅. Why is the standard incubation period fixed at 5 days and the temperature at 20°C?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** BOD₅ is the amount of dissolved oxygen (mg/L) consumed by aerobic microorganisms while decomposing the biodegradable organic matter present in a water sample, when incubated in the dark at **20°C for 5 days**.

**Reason for 5 days / 20°C standardization:**
- 5 days is the **maximum time** historically observed for the oxidation of **carbonaceous organic matter** in domestic sewage to reach completion.
- The temperature of 20°C represents the **average annual temperature of UK rivers** (the original Royal Commission standard, 1908), and is the **international reference temperature** for biological activity comparisons.
- These two conditions ensure **reproducibility** of results across laboratories worldwide.

**(3 marks complete)**

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: Differentiate between BOD and COD. Under what conditions is COD preferred over BOD?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **BOD** | **COD** |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidizing agent | Aerobic microorganisms | K₂Cr₂O₇ (chemical) |
| Type of organics oxidized | Biodegradable only | Biodegradable + non-biodegradable |
| Time required | 5 days | 2–3 hours |
| Magnitude | Lower value | Always higher than BOD |
| Apparatus | BOD incubator, dark bottles | Reflux apparatus |

**COD is preferred when:**
1. **Speed is critical** (e.g., industrial process control).
2. The sample contains **toxic substances** that kill microorganisms (rendering BOD measurement impossible).
3. The wastewater has **non-biodegradable organics** (e.g., phenolic compounds, textile dyes).

**(3 marks complete)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: 1 × 14 Marks)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a) Explain the dilution method for the estimation of BOD₅ of a water sample. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (with sub-parts):**

**(i) Principle:**
The BOD of a sample is determined by measuring the **decrease in dissolved oxygen** of a diluted, aerated, seeded sample after 5 days of incubation at 20°C.

**(ii) Apparatus & Reagents:**
- BOD bottles (300 mL capacity, glass stoppered)
- BOD incubator (20°C ± 1°C)
- Dilution water (aerated, phosphate-buffered, with MgSO₄, CaCl₂, FeCl₃ nutrients)
- Seed (settled domestic sewage, if needed)
- Winkler reagents (for DO estimation)

**(iii) Procedure:**
1. Prepare dilution water and saturate it with dissolved oxygen (~8 mg/L DO).
2. Dilute the sample appropriately (the dilution factor depends on expected BOD).
3. Fill a BOD bottle with the diluted sample (rinsing twice before final filling to displace air bubbles).
4. Determine **initial DO (D₁)** by Winkler method.
5. Seal the bottle and incubate at 20°C in dark for 5 days.
6. After 5 days, measure **final DO (D₂)** by Winkler method.
7. Run a **parallel blank** (only dilution water + seed, no sample) and measure (B₁) and (B₂).

**(iv) Calculation:**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{[(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f]}{P}$$

**(7 marks complete)**

---

**(b) A 10 mL sample of sewage is mixed with 290 mL of dilution water (P = 0.0333). The DO of the sample at the beginning is 8.0 mg/L and after 5 days is 4.0 mg/L. The seed correction term (B₁ − B₂) × f = 0.2 mg/L. Calculate BOD₅. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Given data:**
$$P = 0.0333, \quad D_1 = 8.0 \text{ mg/L}, \quad D_2 = 4.0 \text{ mg/L}, \quad (B_1-B_2)\times f = 0.2 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the seeded BOD formula:**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{[(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f]}{P}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract terms inside the bracket:**

$$D_1 - D_2 = 8.0 - 4.0 = 4.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$4.0 - 0.2 = 3.8 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by P:**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{3.8}{0.0333}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 114.11 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(7 marks complete)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q: (a) Describe the dichromate reflux method for the estimation of COD of an effluent sample. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Principle:**
Organic matter in the sample is chemically oxidized by a **known excess of K₂Cr₂O₇** in a strongly acidic medium (H₂SO₄). The **excess (unreacted) dichromate** is back-titrated with standard **Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate (FAS)** using **ferroin** as the indicator. The difference in FAS volumes between blank and sample gives the dichromate consumed, from which the equivalent O₂ demand (COD) is calculated.

**(ii) Reagents:**
- Standard K₂Cr₂O₇ solution (0.25 N)
- Concentrated H₂SO₄ (with Ag₂SO₄ catalyst)
- HgSO₄ (to complex chlorides)
- Ferroin indicator
- Standard FAS (0.1 N)
- H₂SO₄ (dilute) for titration

**(iii) Procedure:**
1. Pipette 25 mL of the sample into a 250 mL reflux flask.
2. Add 10 mL of 0.25 N K₂Cr₂O₇ and a few glass beads.
3. Slowly add 30 mL of H₂SO₄ (with Ag₂SO₄) and mix.
4. Add ~0.4 g HgSO₄ and reflux for **2 hours**.
5. Cool, dilute to ~150 mL, cool again.
6. Add 2–3 drops of **ferroin indicator** (color changes to blue-green).
7. Titrate against **0.1 N FAS** until color changes from **blue-green to reddish-brown** (end point).
8. Repeat the same procedure with **distilled water** (blank) to get $V_b$.

**(iv) Calculation:**

$$\text{COD (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$

**(7 marks complete)**

---

**(b) In a COD determination, 25 mL of sample was refluxed with 10 mL of 0.25 N K₂Cr₂O₇. The excess dichromate consumed 8.5 mL of 0.1 N FAS, while a blank consumed 14.0 mL of 0.1 N FAS. Calculate the COD of the sample. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Given data:**
$$V_{\text{sample}} = 25 \text{ mL}, \quad V_s = 8.5 \text{ mL}, \quad V_b = 14.0 \text{ mL}, \quad N = 0.1 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Apply COD formula:**

$$\text{COD (mg/L)} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$V_b - V_s = 14.0 - 8.5 = 5.5 \text{ mL}$$

$$5.5 \times 0.1 = 0.55$$

$$0.55 \times 8000 = 4400$$

$$\text{COD} = \frac{4400}{25}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COD} = 176 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(7 marks complete)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "decimal fraction P"** in BOD calculation: Most students simply multiply DO drop by dilution factor. While this works when DF is the integer inverse of P, you **must show P explicitly** to get full marks.
> 2. **Sign error in seeded BOD formula:** The seed correction $(B_1 - B_2) \times f$ must be **SUBTRACTED** from $(D_1 - D_2)$, not added. Mixing the sign will give inflated BOD values.
> 3. **Mixing up $V_b$ and $V_s$ in COD:** The blank titration always uses **MORE** FAS (since no organics are present, so all dichromate remains). So $V_b > V_s$ always. Reversing them gives negative COD.
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply by 8000:** Some students omit this constant. The final answer will be off by a factor of 1000, earning 0 marks.
> 5. **Units:** Always report BOD and COD in **mg/L** (NOT ppm — although numerically equal, KTU expects mg/L).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 KTU EXAM-DAY CHEAT SHEET — BOD & COD 🔥**

- **BOD₅** = Oxygen used by **bacteria** in 5 days at 20°C. **Always measures BIODEGRADABLE** organics only.
- **COD** = Oxygen used by **K₂Cr₂O₇** in 2 hours. **Measures TOTAL** (biodegradable + non-biodegradable) organics.
- **COD > BOD** — **ALWAYS** (non-negotiable relationship in all problems).
- **BOD Formula:** $\text{BOD} = (D_1 - D_2) \div P$ (unseeded); add seed correction for seeded.
- **COD Formula:** $\text{COD} = (V_b - V_s) \times N \times 8000 \div V_{\text{sample}}$.
- The constant **8000** comes from: equivalent weight of O₂ (8 g) × 1000 (g to mg) = 8000.
- **Decimal fraction (P)** = volume of sample / total diluted volume. **Dilution Factor (DF) = 1/P**.
- BOD/COD ratio:
  - **> 0.5** → Highly biodegradable (biological treatment works)
  - **0.3 – 0.5** → Moderately biodegradable
  - **< 0.3** → Poorly biodegradable (needs chemical/advanced treatment)
- **Standard limits:** Drinking water BOD < 2 mg/L; Sewage BOD < 30 mg/L (treated).
- **Winkler method** is used to measure DO (both D₁ and D₂).
- **Ag₂SO₄** catalyst in COD oxidizes straight-chain organics; **HgSO₄** masks chloride interference.
- **Ferroin indicator** in COD changes from blue-green (oxidized) to reddish-brown (reduced at end point).
- The **seed control** in BOD measures the oxygen consumed by the seed organisms alone, ensuring that the sample's BOD is correctly attributed to the wastewater organics, not to the seed's own metabolism.
- BOD requires **5 days**; COD takes **2–3 hours** — this is the primary practical reason COD is preferred in industrial quality control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Waste Management: Sewage water treatment - Primary, Secondary and Tertiary, flow diagram, trickling filter and UASB process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sewage — Formal KTU Definition

**Sewage (or municipal wastewater)** is the liquid waste conveyed by a sewer system, comprising ~99.9 % water and ~0.1 % dissolved & suspended organic/inorganic solids, originating from domestic, industrial, and storm-water sources.

**Sewage Treatment Plant (STP)** is an engineered facility that progressively removes:
1. **Physical contaminants** (grit, debris, settleable solids)
2. **Biological contaminants** (dissolved organics, pathogens)
3. **Chemical contaminants** (nutrients, heavy metals, residual organics)

The treated effluent (**BOD₅ < 30 mg/L**, **SS < 30 mg/L**, pH **6.5 – 9.0**) is then safely discharged into a receiving water body or reused for irrigation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must be able to draw the **flow diagram of a conventional sewage treatment plant**, describe the role of **Trickling Filter (TF)** and **UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket) Reactor** as secondary/biological treatment units, and compute basic **BOD/COD removal efficiencies**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Three-Stage Car Wash"

Imagine washing a very dirty car in three sequential stages:

| Car Wash Stage | Wastewater Equivalent | What it Removes |
|----------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **Rinse with water (physical)** | **Primary Treatment** | Big visible dirt (sand, stones, floating debris) |
| **Soap & sponge scrub (biological)** | **Secondary Treatment** | Greasy, oily, smelly film (dissolved organics) |
| **Polish & wax coat (chemical)** | **Tertiary Treatment** | Microscopic scratches, leftover ions (nutrients, pathogens) |

Just as a car cannot be cleaned in one go, sewage cannot be purified in a single step — each stage targets progressively smaller and more stubborn pollutants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Indicator (KPI):** Engineers judge sewage treatment by **BOD₅** (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) reduction. A raw municipal sewage has **BOD₅ ≈ 200–400 mg/L**; a well-treated effluent should achieve **> 90 % BOD₅ removal**.

## 1.3 Three-Stage Classification (KTU Board Definition)

- **Primary Treatment** → *Physical/Mechanical* — Screening, grit removal, primary sedimentation.
- **Secondary Treatment** → *Biological* — Trickling Filter, Activated Sludge Process (ASP), UASB, Oxidation Pond.
- **Tertiary Treatment** → *Chemical/Advanced* — Chlorination, UV, Reverse Osmosis, Nutrient (N & P) removal, Adsorption.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** "Trickling filter & UASB" are *secondary (biological)* treatment units. Do NOT classify them as tertiary. Tertiary is the **polishing step** after secondary.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage 1 — Primary Treatment (Physical)

**Objective:** Removal of **suspended solids (SS)** and **settleable organic matter** (~50–70 % SS, 25–40 % BOD reduction).

**Unit Operations in Sequence:**

1. **Bar Screens** — Remove large floating objects (rags, sticks, plastics). Bar spacing: **15–50 mm (coarse)** or **6–15 mm (fine)**.
2. **Grit Chamber** — Remove heavy inorganic particles (sand, gravel) by **gravity settling**. Designed for a horizontal velocity of **0.25 m/s** so organics remain suspended.
3. **Primary Clarifier (Sedimentation Tank)** — Rectangular or circular tank, retention time **2–3 hours**, surface overflow rate **30–50 m³/m²/day**. Produces **primary sludge** (thick, odorous, rich in organics).

**Design Equation for Settling Tank:**
$$v_s = \frac{Q}{A_s}$$

Where $v_s$ = surface overflow rate (m³/m²/day), $Q$ = flow rate, $A_s$ = surface area.

## 2.2 Stage 2 — Secondary Treatment (Biological)

**Objective:** Decompose **dissolved & colloidal organic matter** using microorganisms (aerobic/anaerobic), achieving **80–95 % BOD & SS removal**.

### 2.2.1 Trickling Filter (TF) — Attached Growth Process

A **Trickling Filter** is a circular/rectangular tank filled with a **filter media** (stones, slag, plastic rings) over which wastewater is sprayed via a **rotating distributor arm**. Microorganisms grow as a **biofilm (zoogleal film)** on the media surface, and as sewage trickles down, the biofilm adsorbs and oxidises the organic matter using atmospheric oxygen.

**Mechanism (5-step "WHIMS" logic):**
- **W**etting — distributor sprays sewage uniformly
- **H**umidity maintenance — keeps biofilm moist
- **I**ngestion of organics — microbes absorb dissolved organics
- **M**etabolic oxidation — $\text{Organics} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{microbes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{new cells}$
- **S**loughing — excess biofilm peels off (becomes humus sludge)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Keyword — "SPRINKLING FILTER"**: The trickling filter is also known as a *sprinkling filter* or *biological bed*. A common KTU question asks: *“Distinguish between trickling filter and activated sludge process.”*

**Design Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|-----------|---------------|------|
| Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR) | 1 – 4 | m³/m²·day |
| Organic Loading Rate (OLR) | 0.2 – 0.5 | kg BOD/m³·day |
| Filter media depth | 1 – 3 | m |
| Recirculation ratio | 0.5 – 3.0 | — |
| Biofilm thickness | 0.1 – 2.0 | mm |

**Trickling Filter Performance Equation (KTU high-yield):**

$$E = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.0561 \cdot W^{0.5}}{F^{0.5}}}$$

Where:
- $E$ = efficiency of BOD removal (fraction)
- $W$ = BOD loading (kg/ha·m·day) — wait, simplified form below
- $F$ = recirculation factor

**Simplified KTU Form (NRC Equation):**

$$E = \frac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{VF}}}$$

Where $W$ = BOD loading, $V$ = filter volume, $F$ = recirculation factor.

### 2.2.2 UASB Reactor (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket)

A **UASB** is a **high-rate anaerobic** digester where wastewater flows **upward** through a dense, granular **sludge blanket** (biomass concentration **30,000–80,000 mg/L MLVSS**). No mechanical aeration is needed — microbes degrade organics in the *absence of oxygen*, generating **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** as a valuable by-product.

**Anatomy of UASB (4 zones, bottom to top):**

1. **Inlet Distribution Zone** — Even upward flow via perforated pipes.
2. **Sludge Blanket Zone** — Dense anaerobic microbes (granular sludge).
3. **Sludge Blanket Interface** — Active digestion & biogas release.
4. **Three-Phase Separator (GSS — Gas-Solid-Separator)** — Collects biogas at top, returns sludge via deflection plates, clarified effluent leaves the top.

**Key UASB Design Equations:**

$$\text{Upflow velocity } (v_{up}) = \frac{Q}{A_{cross}}$$

$$\text{Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)} = \theta = \frac{V}{Q}$$

$$\text{Organic Loading Rate (OLR)} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$$

$$\text{Sludge Volume Index (SVI)} = \frac{V_{30}}{MLSS} \times 1000 \;\;\text{(mL/g)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biochemical Reaction in UASB:**
> $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 3\text{CH}_4 + 3\text{CO}_2$$
> Methane yield ≈ **0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg COD removed** at 35 °C (mesophilic).

## 2.3 Stage 3 — Tertiary Treatment (Chemical/Polishing)

**Objective:** Remove **residual BOD, nutrients (N & P), pathogens, and recalcitrant compounds** to meet stringent discharge/reuse standards.

**Common Unit Operations:**
- **Chlorination** — Dose **5–15 mg/L Cl₂**, contact time **30 min** → kills 99.9 % coliforms.
- **Nitrification-Denitrification** — $\text{NH}_4^+ \rightarrow \text{NO}_2^- \rightarrow \text{NO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{N}_2\uparrow$
- **Phosphorus precipitation** — $\text{PO}_4^{3-} + \text{Al}^{3+} \rightarrow \text{AlPO}_4 \downarrow$
- **Activated Carbon Adsorption** — Removes micropollutants, colour, odour.
- **Reverse Osmosis / UV disinfection** — For water reuse applications.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Note |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | **BOD₅ (BOD at 5 days, 20 °C)** | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2)}{P}$ | mg/L; $D_1$ = initial DO, $D_2$ = final DO, $P$ = dilution factor |
| 2 | **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)** | $\text{COD} = \dfrac{(a-b)\cdot N \cdot 8000}{V_{sample}}$ | mg/L; $a$ = blank, $b$ = sample titre |
| 3 | **BOD Removal Efficiency** | $\eta_{BOD} = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | **Food to Microorganism Ratio (F/M)** | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | day⁻¹ |
| 5 | **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hours or days |
| 6 | **Mean Cell Residence Time (MCRT / θc)** | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | days |
| 7 | **Surface Overflow Rate** | $v_s = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | m³/m²·day |
| 8 | **Weir Overflow Rate** | $W_{OR} = \dfrac{Q}{L_{weir}}$ | m³/m·day |
| 9 | **Trickling Filter Efficiency (NRC)** | $E = \dfrac{100}{1 + 0.443\sqrt{W/VF}}$ | % |
| 10 | **UASB Biogas Yield** | $G = 0.35 \times (\text{COD}_{removed})$ | m³ CH₄/kg COD |
| 11 | **Sludge Volume Index (SVI)** | $SVI = \dfrac{V_{30}\cdot 1000}{MLSS}$ | mL/g; good sludge: $SVI \lt 150$ |
| 12 | **BOD/COD Ratio Indicator** | Biodegradable: ratio $\gt 0.5$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, the absolute value notation is written as `\vert x \vert` — **never** use a single pipe `|x|` as it breaks table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal STPs** in Kerala (e.g., Cochin, Thiruvananthapuram) employ **UASB + polishing pond** for tropical climate cost-efficiency.
- **Trickling filters** are preferred in small townships due to **low energy** and **operational simplicity**.
- **Tertiary treated water** is reused for industrial cooling, gardening, and groundwater recharge — supporting the **Jal Jeevan Mission** and **SDG-6 (Clean Water & Sanitation)**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — BOD₅ of a Diluted Sample (Winkler's Method)

**Given:** A 10 mL sewage sample is diluted with 290 mL of dilution water (total = 300 mL). Initial DO $D_1 = 8.0$ mg/L; DO after 5 days $D_2 = 4.0$ mg/L.

**Step 1 — Compute the dilution factor (P):**
$$P = \frac{V_{sample}}{V_{total}} = \frac{10}{300} = 0.0333$$

**Step 2 — Compute oxygen consumed:**
$$DO_{consumed} = D_1 - D_2 = 8.0 - 4.0 = 4.0 \;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply BOD₅ formula (correction for dilution):**
$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{DO_{consumed}}{P} = \frac{4.0}{0.0333}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = 120 \;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** Raw municipal sewage BOD₅ ≈ 200 mg/L → our sample indicates **moderately strong** sewage.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — BOD Removal Efficiency of a Trickling Filter

**Given:** Trickling filter with the following data:
- Influent BOD $S_0 = 300$ mg/L
- Effluent BOD $S_e = 30$ mg/L
- Recirculation factor $F = 2.0$
- Filter volume $V = 500$ m³
- Wastewater flow $Q = 2000$ m³/day

**Step 1 — BOD Loading (W):**
$$W = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} = \frac{2000 \times 300}{500} = 1200 \;\text{g/m}^3\cdot\text{day} = 1.2 \;\text{kg/m}^3\cdot\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Apply NRC Trickling Filter Equation:**
$$E = \frac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{V \cdot F}}}$$

$$E = \frac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{\dfrac{1.2}{500 \times 2.0}}}$$

$$E = \frac{100}{1 + 0.443 \sqrt{0.0012}}$$

$$E = \frac{100}{1 + 0.443 \times 0.0346}$$

$$E = \frac{100}{1 + 0.0153} = \frac{100}{1.0153}$$

$$E \approx 98.49 \;\text{\%}$$

**Step 3 — Check via mass balance:**
$$\eta = \frac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100 = \frac{300 - 30}{300} \times 100 = 90 \;\text{\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two values differ because the **NRC empirical equation** assumes standard biofilm conditions. For an exam, always use the **mass-balance (η) method** unless the NRC formula is explicitly requested.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — UASB Upflow Velocity & HRT

**Given:** UASB reactor, diameter 6 m, height 5 m, sewage inflow $Q = 1000$ m³/day.

**Step 1 — Reactor volume:**
$$V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot H = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6^2 \times 5$$

$$V = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 36 \times 5 = 141.37 \;\text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area:**
$$A_{cross} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6^2 = 28.27 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Upflow velocity:**
$$v_{up} = \frac{Q}{A_{cross}} = \frac{1000}{28.27} = 35.37 \;\text{m/day}$$

$$v_{up} = \frac{35.37}{24 \times 3600} = 4.09 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m/s} = 0.41 \;\text{mm/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Recommended UASB upflow velocity = **0.5 – 1.0 m/h** for domestic wastewater. Our value 35.37 m/day = 1.47 m/h → **slightly high**; flow is acceptable for high-strength wastewater.

**Step 4 — Hydraulic Retention Time:**
$$\theta = \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{141.37}{1000} \times 24 \;\text{hours}$$

$$\theta = 3.39 \;\text{hours}$$

Typical UASB HRT for municipal sewage: **4 – 12 hours** ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Trickling Filter vs UASB)

| Parameter | Trickling Filter (TF) | UASB Reactor |
|-----------|-----------------------|--------------|
| **Process type** | Aerobic, attached-growth | Anaerobic, suspended/granular |
| **Energy input** | Low (gravity + distributor) | Very low (no aeration) |
| **Oxygen requirement** | Yes (natural aeration) | None (strictly anaerobic) |
| **Sludge produced** | More (humus sludge) | Less (10× lower than aerobic) |
| **Biogas production** | None | **Yes (CH₄ 60–70 %)** |
| **Footprint** | Large (open tanks) | Compact (tall reactors) |
| **Temperature sensitivity** | Moderate | High (mesophilic 30–37 °C) |
| **Capital cost** | Moderate | High (but OLR recovers cost) |
| **KTU classification** | Secondary | Secondary |
| **Effluent BOD₅** | 20–40 mg/L | 50–150 mg/L (needs polishing) |
| **Land requirement (per MLD)** | ~250 m² | ~50 m² |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Combined System (UASB + Polishing Pond) BOD Removal

**Given:** UASB effluent $S_1 = 100$ mg/L → polishing pond further removes 60 % BOD.

**Step 1 — Polishing pond effluent BOD:**
$$S_2 = S_1 \times (1 - 0.60) = 100 \times 0.40 = 40 \;\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 2 — Overall BOD removal from raw sewage ($S_0 = 300$ mg/L):**
$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{S_0 - S_2}{S_0} \times 100 = \frac{300 - 40}{300} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 86.67 \;\text{\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "PST" → Primary, Secondary, Tertiary** — Always present answers in this order to score full marks.

## 4.4 Process Flow — Why This Sequence?

| Step | Reason for Placement |
|------|----------------------|
| Bar Screens → Grit Chamber | Remove progressively smaller particles; protect downstream pumps |
| Primary Clarifier → Biological Unit | Reduce organic load before microbial action (prevents shock load) |
| Biological Unit → Secondary Clarifier | Separate biomass from treated water |
| Secondary Clarifier → Tertiary | Polishing & disinfection for safe discharge |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always label each unit with its **inlet** and **outlet** streams in the flow diagram. Marks are awarded for the *direction of arrows* and *correct sequencing*.
